results.summary:
The Board agrees with the Cancellation Division in finding that the EUTM proprietor acted in bad faith at the time of filing the contested EUTM application.

The objective facts established in the present case, assessed globally, are as follows: (i) the name WIOHAIR was conceived within the framework of the contractual relationship between the parties and its origin is directly linked to the cancellation applicant; (ii) at the time of filing the application, the EUTM proprietor was fully aware of the existence of the sign and of its link with the cancellation applicant; (iii) the Agreement was fully in force on the filing date; (iv) the Agreement did not contain any express and unconditional authorisation allowing the EUTM proprietor to proceed with the registration of the mark unilaterally, but rather contemplated registration as a future decision to be taken in the context of the development of the project; (v) the EUTM proprietor filed the application unilaterally, without prior communication to or consent from the cancellation applicant; and (vi) the EUTM proprietor has not put forward any plausible justification for the commercial rationale underlying its conduct.

All these elements, taken together, are incompatible with honest commercial practices and reveal a dishonest intention on the part of the EUTM proprietor, consisting in the unilateral appropriation of the sign which was the subject of the contractual relationship and of which the cancellation applicant was the creator and legitimate owner, thereby effectively preventing the latter from continuing to use the sign for its products.

Nothing in the file, however, shows any presence of a dishonest intention of the EUTM proprietor at the time of filing. On the contrary, it would rather seem that the business relationship between the parties ended sourly and that the cancellation applicant, when the cooperation resulted in an unpleasant ‘divorce’, intends to claim the right on the contested EUTM, alleging that the EUTM proprietor acted in bad faith when filing it. The filing of the EUTM in the name of the EUTM proprietor appears to have been made in the pursuit of a common legitimate goal rather than as an act of misappropriating someone else’s designation. As regards the existence of the alleged copyright on the name ‘Virtue’, copyright does not typically protect single words or short phrases because these are considered too minimal to meet the threshold of originality required for copyright protection and that words themselves, when used as trade marks, are not subject to copyright protection. The existence of any possible copyright (which will become clear does not exist) is in any event irrelevant for the purpose of this case.Mr S and the cancellation applicant decided to enter into an employment and service agreement with the EUTM proprietor, clearly knowing that all intellectual property rights of the project related to ‘Virtue’ would be owned by Snautic / Virtue Yachts. The contested EUTM was filed in the context of the cooperation with Mr S and the cancellation applicant on the ‘Virtue’ project, who actively were part of it through employment and service agreements, but who appear to be unsatisfied with the termination of the cooperation and with the fact that the intellectual property rights of the project remained with the EUTM proprietor.

The Board of Appeal confirms the contested decision of the Cancellation Division, which upheld the application for a declaration of invalidity on the ground of bad faith. The invalidity applicant argued that the mark was filed in bad faith (Article 59(1)(b) EUTMR) and was deceptive (Article 59(1)(a) in conjunction with Article 7(1)(g) EUTMR).

results.summary: The objective circumstances of the case, including the origin of the expression “topes de gama” with the invalidityapplicants, who were the first to use it in the relevant sector, the fact that the EUTM proprietor was aware of this, and the chronology of the events, lead to the conclusion that the EUTM proprietor did not act in good faith when filing the contested trade mark.

The contested sign "Breitling for woman" for Class 3 has been applied for in bad faith because4 it incorporates the mark "Breitling" of the invalidity applicant which is reputed for watches and has a high recognition and prestige in the market. Moreover, it has been shown that the EUTM proprietor had a strategy according to which it filed several marks in Hungary and also before the Office which included reputed marks of third parties as DIESEL, NIKE, VICHY etc.. in order to have an unfair advantage. The EUTM proprietor's intentions when filing the contested mark were disloyal and against the accepted principles of good faith. Therefore, the contested decision was to be confirmed and the contested mark declared invalid in its entirety.

The cancellation applicant has not overcome the burden of proving that the EUTM proprietor acted in bad faith when filing the application of the contested EUTM with the intention of free riding on its goodwill and success. There is a comprehensible connection between the contested EUTM, and the EUTM proprietor’s activities . A study of the relevant market, including the United States, where the concept of fast food was developed, would reasonably be expected of an entrepreneur wishing to enter the market as likewise their desire to expand if they succeed. No imitaiton of the cancelaation applicant's business model. 74	General references to the EUTM proprietor using cheddar cheese from the United States and peanut oil to fry (the contested decision erred in stating that the EUTM proprietor uses peanut oil from the United States), are too vague and indeterminate to establish a connection with the cancellation applicant.

The claim that the contested mark has been filed in bad faith because it would be a refiling with the intention to avoid the negative consequences of revocation has to be rejected as unfounded. The reasons are first that the contested mark has been used by the proprietor of the contested EUTM, second that the refiling has been made seven years after the end of the grace period of the earlier EUTMs, third the signs show important differences and the services as well are more clear and precise in the contested mark compared to the earlier EUTMs.

Bad faith not proven. The cancellation applicant argues that the sign was filed in bad faith by his ex-wife, but it is not clear that at the date of filing of the contested mark the relationship had already broken down. It appears that the parties had a commercial collaboration but the circumstances thereof are not clear. Since the EUTM owner was the director of a company whose name includes "Adenauer", there is commercial logic behind her decision to file the trade mark. Moreover, the date of one of the contracts filed as evidence does not match with the dates of the same contract filed as  evidence in parallel proceedings, thereby casting doubt on its authenticity.

The principles established by the legislator in Article 21(1) UMV and the assessments that can be derived from it are also applicable to the facts of the present case, in which a Union trade mark is applied for jointly by an association and its president as co-owners. 

In the present case, the burden of proof therefore lies with the appellant to demonstrate that there is justification for the co-ownership, in particular that the association consented to the specific trade mark application, especially since it is undisputed that the appellant was the president of the association at the time of the application for the EU trade mark and thus the statutory representative of the respondent.

It cannot be established that the association's executive committee formed the intention to apply for the pure word mark “BLACK WINGS” as an EU trade mark for the goods in classes 9, 16, 18, 21, 24, 25, 26 and 28, as well as for services in classes 35 and 41, jointly owned with the complainant, cannot be established and cannot be inferred from other circumstances, such as the complainant's assertion that it was customary practice to always file trademark applications jointly with the association's president. Any doubts regarding the subject matter and scope of the association's declaration of consent are to the detriment of the appellant, as the burden of proof of a valid declaration of consent by the association for the specific self-dealing transaction in question lies with him.

The appellant has failed to prove that the appointment of P* S* as legal representative to carry out the specific registration of the EU trademark for him and the respondent as joint co-owners of a word mark ‘BLACK WINGS’ was made with the necessary consent of one of the two vice-presidents of the association together with another member of the association's executive committee prior to the registration or was subsequently approved.

results.summary: The decision establishes that the configuration of the contested mark can be one of the objective factors that show intent. In particular, when an EUTM holder, in creating their sign, omits the distinctive elements of earlier signs and takes over only some non-distinctive components, their intention when filing to register such a sign may not be a dishonest one. Rather, they may be seeking to maintain a sufficient degree of differentiation from the other party’s sign. If only the non-distinctive elements are used and new, distinctive, and non-coinciding features are added, the EUTM holder may genuinely intend to distance themselves from the other party’s sign so as to avoid causing any harm.

results.nickName: HILLTOP Ceylon YAPRAK ÇAYI Blatt Tee Schwarzer Tee Thé noir Ceylon Black Tea (fig.) / TANAY Ceylon  YAPRAK CAYI Blatt Tee Schwarzer Tee Thé noir Ceylon Black Tea (fig.)
results.summary: The overall assessment reveals a cohesive body of objective indicia pointing to an intention inconsistent with honest commercial practices. These include the EUTM proprietor’s presumed knowledge of the cancellation applicant’s long standing earlier brand and trade dress, given the highly specific niche market and the significant commercial presence of the cancellation applicant in the EU, evidenced by sales amounting to nearly EUR 1 million across Belgium, Germany, France and the Netherlands between 2015 and 2021, and a volume exceeding 2 000 tonnes in Germany in 2019 alone, as well as the corporate links to the Swiss market where the cancellation applicant was also active; the striking similarity of the contested mark to the cancellation applicant’s established trade dress, characterised by the specific red oval shape, its colour and arrangement, including the combination of outer black and white surrounding borders, extending to additional details such as the specific multilingual text sequence, which manifestly cannot be fortuitous; the lack of any plausible commercial logic or independent explanation for this imitation other than an intention to free ride on the cancellation applicant’s reputation; the apparent systematic pattern of conduct involving related entities in other jurisdictions, suggesting a broader strategy of appropriating established brands in the ethnic tea sector; and the subsequent use of the contested mark to block the original user from the register by means of filing an opposition (B 3 210 585). Therefore, the contested decision is annulled and European Union trade mark No 18 631 369 is declared invalid in its entirety pursuant to Article 59(1)(b) EUTMR.

results.summary: The contested EUTM is not deceptive as to the geographical origin of the registered goods. It was not filed in bad faith, either, and there is no likelihood of confusion.

Taking into acocunt evidence filed on appeal the sole and effective aim of the EUTM proprietor in seeking the registration of the contested EUTM, as to prevent the cancellation applicant from using the sign ‘TECORA’ in the identical field of the analysis of monitoring air and industrial emissions tha tit had lawfully acquired in a judicial liquidation in France. The EUTM proprietor had been an employee for many years in the group company liquidated and knew of that acquisition. Based on this knowledge it sought to dissuade customers from continuing business with the EUTM proproietor and even stated in its website that it wished to eliminate the French.

In the overall assesment of all the circumstances no bad faith has been proven upon presentation of the contested EUTM. The Board confirms that there is no refiling nor that the EUTM proprietor acted with a dishonest intention.

The Cancellation Division was correct to hold that, in light of all the objective circumstances of the case, and taking into account all the submissions and evidence of the cancellation applicant, it can be determined that the intention of the EUTM proprietor at the time of filing the contested EUTM was part of a bad faith strategy to register a trade mark almost identical to trade marks it knew very well that the cancellation applicant had been using for many years in the Middle East, and indeed which would grant monopoly rights to the EUTM proprietor which would add further obstacles to any future development of the cancellation applicant’s beverage business in the EU. It has been shown that the EUTM proprietor filed the application for registration of the contested EUTM with the aim not of engaging fairly in competition but with the intention of undermining, in a manner inconsistent with honest practices, the interests of third parties.

results.summary: In light of the EUTM proprietor's direct knowledge of the strategic importance of the EU and the extent of activities carried out under the ‘AYN RAND’ sign by the cancellation applicant, the misuse of the permissions granted in relation to the use of the sign 'AYN RAND' and in the absence of any legitimate justification, the Board concludes that the contested EUTM was filed in bad faith within the meaning of Article 59(1)(b) EUTMR. Since the cancellation application based on bad faith under Article 59(1)(b) EUTMR is entirely successful, there is no need to assess the remaining ground, namely Article 60(1)(c) EUTMR in conjunction with Article 8(4) EUTMR.

The identity between the signs does not seem to be coincidental. The cancellation applicant was established in the cryptocurrency market at the time of filing of the contested EUTM, so the EUTM proprietor’s knowledge of the existence of the cancellation applicant and the use of its earlier mark on the market must be presumed. The scale and complexity of the tasks involved in creating a secure, legally compliant, and successful exchange platform make it virtually impossible for one individual to manage every facet of the business alone. Therefore, the EUTM proprietor's explanations regarding her alleged business model and the reasons for non-use are implausible. The EUTM proprietor has not been able to justify the choice of the specific term ‘Gate.io’ that she had registered as an EUTM, how she prepared to enter the market without being aware of the existence of the cancellation applicant (one of the major players in the field), the fact that her crypto platform was still not operational at the time of the parties’ exchange of submissions before the Board, the questionable quality of her business plan, or why her colleagues were unable to see it through while she was unwell. Furthermore, the fact that the cancellation applicant was asked to pay such a high price for acquiring the contested EUTM, coupled with no use (or at least no proven use) of the sign before or thereafter by the EUTM proprietor, and the blocking position brought about by the oppositions filed by the EUTM proprietor against the cancellation applicant’s applications, all point to a dishonest intention on the part of the EUTM proprietor. The cancellation applicant has succeeded in proving that the EUTM proprietor breached the duty of fair play and was acting in bad faith when filing the contested EUTM. As a consequence, the cancellation applicant is considered to have established a dishonest intention on the part of the EUTM proprietor. The application for invalidity is totally successful. The contested decision must be confirmed and European Union trade mark No 18 689 933 must be declared invalid in respect of all the registered goods and services.

results.quotation: (06/11/2025, R 1691/2024‑2, ASBIR AVRUPA SIVASLILAR BIRLIGI HOLLANDA-NEDERLAND-NETHERLANDS (fig.) / ASBIR)
results.nickName: ASBIR AVRUPA SIVASLILAR BIRLIGI HOLLANDA-NEDERLAND-NETHERLANDS (fig.) / ASBIR
keywords: Used in the course of trade
results.summary: In the absence of evidence showing use in Germany of more than mere local significance of the earlier sign an essential requirement of Article 8(4) EUTMR has not been fulfilled. Bad faith has not been provem: there is a comprehensible connection between the contested EUTM, and the activities of the EUTM proprietor. The cancellation applicant argues that the EUTM proprietor should have requested its authorization and approval but does not refer any article of the association requiring such approval and authorisation. there is no evidence that the cancellation applicant was itself responsible for the design of the logo of the earlier mark which might have necessitated its approval and authorisation. The evidence falls short of what would be required to establish any reputation, goodwill or client base at the filing date of the contested EUTM.

The Cancellation Division decided that the contested mark filed in red colour constituted a repeated application in bad faith for goods and services identical and closely related to those covered by the earlier mark filed in black and white in classes 18, 25, 38, 39, 41, 43 and 44. However, the final decision partially validating the registration of the earlier mark (30/05/2023, R 828/2020-1, The standard (fig.)) must necessarily be taken into account in assessing the contested trade mark application. Consequently, at least for the ‘services of hotels, restaurants, bars, cafés and lounge bars’ in Class 43, the Cancellation Division could not consider that the contested mark constituted a repeated application solely to avoid revocation. Furthermore, while it is true that the differences between the contested mark and the earlier mark are limited to colour (red and white instead of black and white), the colour red is an element that differentiates the two marks. The filing of the contested mark is part of a filing strategy of the same sign by the EUTM proprietor, who had already been using its mark in red and white for several years. In the circumstances of the present case, the contested mark constitutes a variation of the earlier mark, so that the application for registration of the contested mark was justified by normal commercial practice. The mere fact that the application for registration of the contested mark was filed the day after the action for revocation of the earlier mark was brought is not sufficient, in the absence of any other evidence tending to establish the bad faith of the EUTM proprietor, to rebut the presumption of good faith.

results.quotation: (21/10/2025, R 2455/2024‑4, Remington Synergy World (fig.) / REMINGTON et al.)
results.ipRight: EUTM on which IA is based
results.nickName: Remington Synergy World (fig.) / REMINGTON et al.
results.summary:
The fact that, firstly, the EUTM proprietor must have known the cancellation applicant’s mark when it applied for the contested mark; secondly, the contested mark is visually highly similar and phonetically identical to the cancellation applicant’s mark ‘REMINGTON’; thirdly, the EUTM proprietor has used a sign that is almost identical to the cancellation applicant’s mark ‘REMINGTON’ in the same economic sector as the cancellation applicant’s mark (i.e. in the hunting sector and even for hunting equipment in Class 13); and fourthly, there has been actual confusion between the marks on the market, clearly lead to the conclusion that the EUTM proprietor’s intention when it filed the contested mark was to take advantage of the cancellation applicant’s recognition and goodwill on the hunting market to increase its sales numbers. The mere fact that the contested mark is registered in classes for which the cancellation applicant’s mark ‘REMINGTON’ has not been registered and that the contested mark contains additional decorative and laudatory elements that are not included in the cancellation applicant’s mark ‘REMINGTON’ cannot outweigh these clear indications for bad faith 
It follows from the wording of Article 61(1) EUTMR that the coexistence of the marks on the market on which the EUTM proprietor relies could not exclude bad faith on its part in the present case, even if it were proven, which is not the case
Since bad faith on the part of the EUTM proprietor at the time of filing the contested mark has been proved, the application for declaration of invalidity must be upheld on the grounds of Article 59(1)(b) EUTMR and the contested mark must be declared invalid for all the contested goods.

The EUTM’s proprietor kowledge of the earlier brand, combined with the striking similarity of the signs’ get-up in actual use, the flawed commercial logic of its defence, and its subsequent actions to prevent the cancellation applicant’s market access, form a body of relevant and consistent indicia. These factors, when assessed together, point to an intention that was not to engage fairly in competition, but rather to benefit from the cancellation applicant’s established goodwill and to undermine its interests in a manner inconsistent with honest commercial practices.

The cancellation applicant has not overcome the burden of proving that the EUTM proprietor acted in bad faith when it filed the application of the contested EUTM with the intention of hindering the cancellation applicant from entering the European Union market. The the possibility cannot be excluded that the EUTM proprietor adopted the term ‘xTransfer’ by chance or coincidence.  The evidence adduced does not support the existence of a global reputation that would have been established from its startup i to the date when the contested EUTM was filed, in three years. There is a comprehensible connection between the contested EUTM and the business activities of the EUTM proprietor. 60	While the cancellation applicant claims that it has a branch in the same city as the EUTM proprietor’s headquarters, it has provided no evidence on the size and activities of this branch, which could indicate that its activities in all probability came to the attention of the EUTM proprietor . The parties are not direct competitors according to the cancellation applicant’s own evidence, even though they are both financial technology companies. There have been no direct relations between the parties. There is a comprehensible connection between the contested EUTM and the business activities of the EUTM proprietor.

The EUTM proprietor acted in bad faith when filing the contested mark, based on the prior long-standing use of an identical mark by the cancellation applicant and the proprietor’s deliberate actions to undermine that presence. This behavior, aimed at appropriating an existing market identifier rather than genuine competition, violates honest trade mark practices and applies even to goods and services only related or dissimilar to those of the prior mark.

It is not disputed that the EUTM proprietors had knowledge of the use of the sign 'KOMBI' prior to the filing of the contested EUTM 'kombii', and that the two signs are confusingly similar. Nevertheless, the cancellation applicant failed to demonstrate that the EUTM proprietors had dishonest intentions at the time of filing of the contested EUTM. On the contrary, the sequence of events shows that the filing of the contested EUTM is consistent with a practical business approach, that is, to protect the name for which the proprietors had gained notoriety. Consequently, bad fiath is not establoshed and the Cancellation Division correctly rejected the application for a declaration of invalidity in its entirety. The appeal is dismissed.

Genuine use of a trade mark for distributorship services in Class 35 requires clear evidence that the applicant was offering such services as an independent commercial activity, typically for the benefit of third parties, and not merely reselling goods as part of its own business operations. Therefore, genuine use in Class 35 has not been demonstrated. Moreover, the invalidity applicant did not demonstrate that the EUTM proprietors’ intention when filing the contested EUTM was to misappropriate its earlier sign or prevent it from continuing to use the earlier sign. Consequently, the presumption of good faith prevails.

results.ipRight: EUTM on which IA is based

results.summary:
In the view of the Board of Appeal, the EUTM proprietor’s behavior is contrary to ethical and honest commercial practice. Taking into account the evidence and the specific circumstances of the case, and by paying particular attention to the commercial relationship and logic underlying the filing, as well as to the chronology of events, it must be considered that the proprietor of the EUTM acted with the intention of preventing the applicant for invalidity from continuing its ordinary business activities in the sector concerned (11/07/2013, T-321/10, GRUPPO SALINI / SALINI, EU:T:2013:372, §§ 21, 23, 28; 06/03/2024, T-639/22, GEOGRAPHICAL NORWAY EXPEDITION (fig.) / NAPAPIJRI geographic (fig.) et al., EU:T:2024:149, § 89).
The Board of Appeal notes, in particular, that the relationship between the parties over a period of five years created a sufficiently close link to suggest that it was fair to expect the proprietor of the European Union trade mark not to file, independently and without prior notice to the applicant for invalidity, an almost identical EUTM application, and without granting the latter sufficient time to bring an action against the contested EUTM (13/11/2024, T-1127/23, CorrosionX, EU:T:2024:812, § 70; 13/12/2004, R 582/2003-4, EAST SIDE MARIO’S, § 23).
The EUTM proprietor did not comply with its general duty of trust and loyalty arising from the commercial relationship with the applicant for invalidity and was aware that it was causing harm to the latter, as well as that such harm was the consequence of conduct reprehensible from a moral or commercial standpoint (11/07/2013, T-321/10, GRUPPO SALINI / SALINI, EU:T:2013:372, § 44; 21/04/2010, R 219/2009-1, GRUPPO SALINI / SALINI, § 66).
Consequently, the Board of Appeal confirms that it has been established that the proprietor of the EUTM acted in bad faith when filing the application for the contested EUTM.

When the extracts of the national law provided cannot be interpreted in the way suggested by the invalidity applicants, further evidence must be provided in order to prove that the national law must be interpreted in the manner invoked by them (§ 40).

The commercial logic of the registration of the contested sign by its applicant remains unknown. The circumstances surrounding the application for the contested EUTM do not presuppose an honest intention and no arguments were submitted by the EUTM proprietor to rebut this presupposition. It becomes apparent from the circumstances and the chronologic succession of events that, by filing the contested mark, the EUTM proprietor’s intention was to profit and take advantage from the link and association the consumers will have with the invalidity applicant’s signs, which had a certain recognition in the EU.

Given the identity of the signs and the identity or similarity of the goods between the earlier trade mark and the contested EUTM, as well as the chronology of events, i.e. that the application for the contested EUTM had been filed only five days after the Invalidity Applicant's letter informing the EUTM proprietor that the earlier trade mark was liable to be revoked for lack of use, it can be concluded that the EUTM proprietor deliberately applied for registration of the contested EUTM for the sole purpose of circumventing the revocation of the earlier mark (§ 71-72).

results.summary: The cancellation applicant has proven that the etimology of the contested EUTM ‘BITOMAT’, t is ‘bit +‎ -o- +‎ -mat’ or ‘bit(coin) +‎ -o- +‎ -mat’, is in Polish a Bitcoin automated teller machine (self-service device that allows one to buy or sell cryptocurrencies, whcih is supported by extracts from official Polish sources defining the compound word ‘BITOMAT’,  and that this meaning already existed at the date the contested EUTM was filed. The contested EUTM is descriptive in Polish of the means by which the contested services will be provided, namely cryptocurrency automatic teller machines (ATMs). It is also clear from the evidence th that the contested EUTM was not capable of performing its function as a distinctive sign at the date it was filed.

results.summary:
As far as the cancellation application is based on the grounds of Article 60(1)(a) EUTMR, both earlier EUTMs are not valid at the time of the Board’s decision (one not renewed, the other revoked due to non-use). As there are no valid earlier rights to form a basis under Article 60(1)(a) EUTMR in conjunction with Article 8(1)(b) and Article 8(5) EUTMR, the cancellation action, as far as based on those grounds, has subsequently become unfounded. For the sake of completeness, even if it was considered that for the Board to review the legality of the contested decision on its merits, it was decisive that both earlier marks be in existence at the date of filing of the cancellation application and valid when the contested decision was taken, the cancellation application on the grounds of Article 60(1)(a) EUTMR in conjunction with Article 8(1)(b) and Article 8(5) EUTMR would not in any event be successful due to insufficient evidence of use of the earlier marks. 

Insofar the cancellation application is based on the grounds of Article 59(1)(b) EUTMR, the cancellation applicant did not demonstrate that the EUTM proprietor’s intention when filing the contested EUTM was to misappropriate its earlier sign or prevent it from continuing to use the earlier sign. The circumstances arising from the submissions of the parties cannot substantiate a finding that the EUTM proprietor was acting in bad faith when filing the application. The cancellation applicant failed to demonstrate that by filing the contested mark, the EUTM proprietor pursued a different aim than to use it in accordance with the essential trade mark function. Consequently, the presumption of good faith prevails.

The cancellation applicant failed to prove bad faith. The evidence consists to a large extent on affidavits concerning the contradictory statements that were made or not made by various employees of the parties. It is impossible to get a clear picture of the facts on the basis of the evidence on file. As a result, the Board based itself on the very few objective elements, namely the contract between the parties, the existence of an earlier registration of a different ROSA & ME mark in Germany and the factual elements that the parties seemed to agree on. The filing of the contested EUTM follows a commercial logic because it is in line with the objectives pursued by the contract between the parties. Also, the contested sign was not applied for by the EUTM proprietor but by a different company, which also affects the assessment of the subjective intent at the time of filing.  The elements available are thus not sufficient to counter the presumption of good faith.

The cancellation applicant did not prove the national Polish law in relation to the ealier non-registred trade mark relied on under Article 8(4) EUTMR. However, the application for a declaration of invaldity was successuful under Article 59(1)(b) EUTMR. Given the chronology of events, the objective circumstances of the case and all the indications considered together, the EUTM proprietor used the sign ‘KONTURVNIA’  to deliberately create a connection with the cancellation applicant and benefit from her recognition as a make-up artist and professional under the same sign in Poland. The EUTM proprietor potentially gained undue benefits by giving the false impression to the public that its own services in the same make-up-related field, albeit related to permanent make-up, under the sign ‘KONTUROVNIA’, had been endorsed by the cancellation applicant herself.

results.summary: The EUTM proprietor's contested mark includes all the graphic and verbal elements of the signs on which the invalidity applicant relied on. These signs have been previously used in Lebanon and the invalidity applicant has also shown that the enjoyed previously of the relevant filing date of the contested mark a certain recognition and well-known character. Moreover, there are additionally several circumstances that weigh in favor of bad faith on the EUTM proprietor's side: He was a Syrian citizen and had to leave Syria due to the civil war. He is active in the business of foodstuffs from Arabic countries exactly as the invalidity applicant in Lebanon. The way of combining all the graphic and verbal elements clearly shows that the EUTM proprietor filed the contested mark with the intention of getting as close as possible to the invalidity applicant's marks and thereby profit from their recognition of the Arabic consumers in the EU.

results.summary: The Board confirms the contested decision which found no likelihood of confusion between the signs in conflict nor bad faith in the filing of contested EUTM.

The Board suspends the present proceedings until the completion of the proceedings pending before the courts in Poland at the claim of the proprietor of the ZTUE for a declaration of non-infringement of the earlier Polish trade marks with the numbers R 104 382 and R 105 866 and the earlier ZTUE with the number 18 125 393 by the disputed ZTUE.

The EUTM proprietor has expressly acknowledged that the application was filed solely to prevent the cancellation applicants or one of the cancellation applicants from submitting their own applications. Such conduct constitutes prima facie dishonest behavior, as evidenced by the EUTM proprietor’s lack of intention to use the trade mark within the European market. The statement of grounds leaves no doubts that the application of the contested EUTM was filed without any intention of use and only with the aim to obstruct the cancellation applicants; whether the EUTM proprietor could have achieved the same result, i.e. blocking the registration of a similar trade mark to his own Polish trade mark or the EUTM by relying on those trade mark in opposition proceedings, has no bearing on the outcome.
The application was purely defensive in nature, intended to obstruct the cancellation applicants from registering their trade marks before the Office. In the case at stake, there is no objective justification for the proprietor’s decision to file an application for a trade mark that is identical to one already used and registered in the United States of America on behalf of one of the cancellation applicants.
Even if one of the cancellation applicants had intended in bad faith to seek protection for the sign by designating the European Union in an international registration, the EUTM proprietor’s actions would still be regarded as being in bad faith. Such behavior remains indicative of bad faith regardless of any purported intent to secure protection.

The cancellation applicant has not seeded any doubts on the assessment about the Cancellation Division, and the Board cannot see any bad faith by the EUTM proprietor when applying for the contested EUTM. To the contrary, the EUTM proprietor acted within the established norms in business and only tried to secure its rights to its own business name.

results.summary:
The right to be heard does not apply to the final position which the administration intends to adopt. The BoA is not obliged to hear the EUTM proprietor on its final assessment as regards the definition of a mark, and the question whether that mark was devoid of distinctive character when its assessment was based on the file containing all the arguments and evidence submitted by the parties before the EUIPO (§ 25, 27).

While the presumption of validity of the EUTM’s registration restricts the EUIPO’s obligation to examine the relevant facts, it does not preclude it from relying not only on the arguments and evidence produced by the invalidity applicant, but also on well-known facts observed in the context of the invalidity proceedings (§ 49).

The EUIPO must, in its examination of distinctive character, refer to the reproduction of the mark as applied for and examine it by reference to the goods or services applied for and by reference to the perception of the relevant public. It cannot be required to examine actual use of the mark on the market (§ 72).

Signs consisting of a combination of non-distinctive elements, such as basic geometrical figures, are only distinctive if there is concrete evidence, such as the way the elements are combined, that indicates that the sign is greater than the sum of its parts (§ 63).

The red and white colour combination of the contested mark constitutes a basic colour combination, commonly and widely used in everyday life (§ 68).

results.summary: Considering the totality of the objective circumstances – the original EUTM proprietor’s clear and admitted knowledge of the defendant’s ‘Nannys’ marks and their reputation in Cyprus for baby care products; the similarity of the main distinctive verbal element ‘NANNYS’; the initial filing for identical and a wide range of similar and other goods; the contradictory and unconvincing explanations for the adoption of the ‘NANNYS’ element; and the documented approach to NANNYS S.A. to produce goods under the contested mark for the Cypriot market – the Board finds that the only plausible inference is that the original EUTM proprietor’s intention at the time of filing was to take unfair advantage of the defendant’s established goodwill and reputation. The EUTM proprietor, therefore, departed from honest business practices and standards of accepted principles of ethical behaviour when it applied for the contested mark.

results.summary: The cancellation applicant did prove that the EUTM proprietor had acted in bad faith when applying for the contested mark.

Taking into account all the factual circumstances relevant to the present case, it is concluded that the filing of the application of the contested mark, insofar as it includes the goods already covered by an earlier identical EUTM registration, was made without any commercial logic. There are sufficient indications, i.e. chronology, scope of protection of the goods and lack of justification, that the application for the contested mark, in so far as it includes goods already covered by an earlier mark, did not make much commercial sense, except for circumventing the proof of use requirement. All of the circumstances imply that the intention of the EUTM proprietor was indeed to take advantage of the EU trade mark rules by artificially creating the situation where it would not have to prove genuine use of its earlier mark. Whether or not it could have actually proven such use is irrelevant, as it is the intention of the applicant for a mark which is to be evaluated.

Bad faith must be assessed on the basis of an overall examination of all relevant factors specific to the case, with the EUTM proprietor enjoying a presumption of good faith. The mere fact that the contested trade marks were registered in the name of the distributor, or a company of its group, cannot in itself be sufficient to establish bad faith. That fact must be taken into account in conjunction with all objective circumstances that may clarify the intentions of the EUTM proprietor’s predecessors in title when applying for the contested trade marks (§ 51-52).

The contested mark ‘SOLID 226’ was filed only ten days after the invalidity applicant started using its non-registered mark. Considering the very short period between the marketing campaign and the filing of the contested mark, however intensive the campaign may have been, it cannot be concluded, without any further evidence, that the EUTM proprietor had knowledge of the invalidity applicant’s ‘SOLID 225’ energy bars. Furthermore, considering the weak distinctive character of the combination ‘SOLID 226’ for the goods applied for in Class 25, it cannot be ruled out that the EUTM proprietor came up with that combination independently. Therefore, the contested decision’s conclusion that the EUTM proprietor was aware of the use made by the invalidity applicant of the sign ‘SOLID 225’ because both parties are competitors in the rather limited market of sport nutrition for endurance sports is based on mere speculation and not on convincing facts. In any event, the other objective circumstances of the case do not show the EUTM proprietor’s bad faith at the time of filing the application for registration.

results.quotation: (14/05/2025, T‑327/24, Dr.Albert / DR. ALBERTS et al., EU:T:2025:490)
results.summary:
The fact that use of a sign for which registration is sought would enable the EUTM proprietor to take unfair advantage of the reputation of an earlier trade mark or sign, or even of the name of a famous person, is such as to establish bad faith on the part of the EUTM proprietor (§ 34).

A claim that the EUTM proprietor never intended to disrupt the business of the invalidity applicant is irrelevant in a context where the BoA’s finding of bad faith is based on the EUTM proprietor’s having taken unfair advantage of the invalidity applicant’s renown (§ 99).

The finding that the intention of the EUTM proprietor was to create an association in the minds of the relevant public between the name by which the invalidity applicant is known and the contested mark justifies the declaration of invalidity of the contested mark in respect of all the goods it covered (§ 117).

The ground for invalidity based on bad faith is founded on public interest and cannot depend on the existence of bad faith on the part of the person applying for the invalidity of the contested mark. Therefore, the potential bad faith on the part of the invalidity applicant, even if it were established, is not capable of demonstrating an error affecting the BoA’s conclusion that the invalidity of the contested mark was justified on the ground that it was filed in bad faith by the EUTM proprietor (§ 122).

The principle of prohibition of abuse under EU law precludes reliance on EU rules for purposes that undermine their objective. Establishing an abusive practise requires (i) objective circumstances indicating that the purpose of the rules has not been achieved despite formal compliance, and (ii) a subjective element reflecting the intention to obtain an undue advantage by artificially creating the conditions for applying those rules (§ 126).

results.summary: Given that the contested EUTM has been revoked in its entirety by a final decision, the invalidity and appeal proceedings have become devoid of purpose and must therefore be closed.

It was general knowledge in the economic sector concerned (namely the market of snacks) in the countries of the use of an identical/similar earlier right for identical/similar goods by the cancellation applicant, and that use was also long standing. This is demonstrated by the invoices, sales figures, screenshots, distribution agreements in several countries and territories of ex-Yugoslavia as explained.
Furthermore, it has been established that the parties have been in a business relationship with each other, and as a result thereof, the EUTM proprietor could not have been unaware that the cancellation applicant had been using the contested EUTM.
The cancellation applicant argues that the EUTM proprietor attempted to rush the cancellation applicant into buying the contested EUTM, which also demonstrates that the EUTM proprietor intended to receive a considerable financial gain in exchange for not trying to obstruct the cancellation applicant’s business on the relevant markets, including the EU. It has been established that the parties have been in a business relationship with each other, and as a result thereof, the EUTM proprietor could not have been unaware that the cancellation applicant had been using the contested EUTM , or a sign similar to it. However, the fact that the contested trade mark and the earlier marks are identical or nearly identical cannot be mere coincidence.

Article 71(1) EUTMR and the structure of the EUTMR establish that the BoA has the same powers as the First Instance deciding department and must examine the dispute as a whole at the time of its ruling. Functional continuity between EUIPO’s first-instance bodies and the BoA means the latter must consider all factual and legal arguments raised at either stage. The term ‘may’ confirms the BoA’s broad discretion to decide the case or remit it. In the present case, the Applicant expressly requested the BoA to rule on the remaining invalidity grounds if the plea on lack of distinctiveness was inadmissible. The parties had also exchanged arguments on those grounds before both instances. In this procedural context, the decision not to remit was justified, fell within the BoA’s discretion, and did not infringe procedural rights, as the parties had three opportunities to submit arguments and evidence (§ 60-62).

The arguments invoking Article 3bis of the Madrid Agreement, including the annexed list of Contracting States, are inadmissible and inapplicable, given that the EU is not a party to the Agreement and only a minority of Member States were contracting parties at the relevant date. Furthermore, Article 3bis does not extend beyond the provisions of Articles 7(1)(g) and 51(1)(a) CTMR concerning absolute grounds for invalidity based on deceptive trade marks. Its scope is limited to prohibiting deceptive marketing practices and does not regulate trade mark registration or invalidity criteria. Therefore, Article 3bis of the Madrid Agreement is not pertinent to the resolution of the present matter (§124-126).

The applicant for a trade mark is not required to indicate or even to know precisely, on the date on which his or her application for registration of a mark is filed or of the examination of that application, the use he or she will make of the mark applied for and he or she has a period of five years for beginning actual use consistent with the essential function of that trade mark. The bad faith of the trade mark applicant cannot, therefore, be presumed on the basis of the mere finding that, at the time of filing his or her application, that applicant had no economic activity corresponding to the goods and services referred to in that application (§ 145, 147).

results.quotation: (28/03/2025, R 1149/2024‑4, QUEENFIT / QUEEN FIT et al.)
keywords: Similarity of the goods and services
Similarity of the goods and services (Yes)
results.summary: A literal interpretation of the contested online ordering services refers to services provided via the internet that enable consumers to browse, select, order, and pay for goods or services through a digital platform. These services encompass platforms, websites, or applications that facilitate the placement of orders for products or services online. They generally allow users to browse and select items, review their orders, and complete transactions using digital payment methods. 51	The cancellation applicant’s online sales of various goods and the contested services involve the selection, purchase, and delivery of goods, often supported by digital infrastructure, rendering their method of use similar.They function within the same commercial ecosystem with the purpose of providing product availability to consumers and businesses. The Board considers that the services are at least similar to a low degree.The signs are visually highly similar, and aurally and conceptually identical. Despite the below-average degree of distinctiveness of earlier mark 1, there is a likelihood of confusion. Essentially, the two signs are quasi-identical. Conceptually and phonetically, they are identical. Visually, they are extremely similar, differing only by a space between the terms ‘QUEEN’ and ‘FIT’ in earlier mark 1. This difference is likely to be overlooked due to its position within the contested sign. Given that consumers must rely on an imperfect recollection of the marks they have encountered, it is evident that there is a likelihood of confusion.

Without prejudice to the existence of a  discrepancy in the interpretation of the fifth clause of the contract of sale of company shares, which the BoA cannot judge, the chronology of the events, the purpose of commercial continuity and use as a trademark of the disputed sign and the lack of usurpation of the surname of the applicant for annulment, and taking into consideration lla the evidence, for the BoA it has not been proven that the applicaion for the contested mark has been done in bad faith. within the meaning of Article 59(1)(b) EUTMR has not been proven. The continuity of action in the distinctive sign carried out by the holder of the EUTM with the registration of the contested EUTM is part of a practice commonly accepted among fair and honest uses in the market to maintain commercial activity governed by good commercial practice that is usually acquired over time.
On the contrary, the behaviour of the proprietor of the contested EUTM before registration, at the time of registration and subsequently cannot be characterised as sinister or as departing from accepted principles of ethical behaviour or honest commercial and business practices. The case is remitted to the Cancellation Division to further analyse the ground based on Article 60(2) EUTMR.

The EUTM proprietor withdrew the appeal following an agreement between the parties and the cancellation proceedings are closed. Agreement on costs between the parties.

results.quotation: (12/03/2025, R 0124/2022‑2, GEOGRAPHICAL NORWAY EXPEDITION (fig.) / NAPAPIJRI geographic (fig.) et al.)
results.nickName: GEOGRAPHICAL NORWAY EXPEDITION (fig.) / NAPAPIJRI geographic (fig.) et al.
results.summary: The Case was remitted to the Second Board further to the judgment of 06/03/2024, T‑639/22, GEOGRAPHICAL NORWAY EXPEDITION (fig.) / NAPAPIJRI geographic (fig.). Considering the commercial success of the figurative mark ‘NAPAPIJRI geographic’ used by the invalidity applicant and the past relations between the parties, on the day the contested figurative mark ‘GEOGRAPHICAL NORWAY EXPEDITION’ was filed, the predecessor in title of the EUTM proprietor could not have been unaware of the existence of the invalidity applicant’s figurative mark as used on the market with the Norwegian flag, for clothing, footwear and bags. Further, the contested mark bears a strong resemblance to the trade mark used by the invalidity applicant and is registered for the same goods (clothing, footwear and bags) as well as for goods belonging to neighbouring market segments. Furthermore, the parasitic use of the contested trade mark after its filing against the invalidity applicant and the acts of parasitism of the EUTM proprietor towards third parties confirm the bad faith of the predecessor in title of the EUTM proprietor on the date of filing of the trade mark.

Although the EUTM proprietor rightly contested the first instance decision with respect to the erred findings of bad faith based on repetitive filings, considering all the objective criteria at hand, the Board considers that following the conflictual relations between the parties and the lack of any commercial logic, the contested mark was not filed in good faith.

Firstly, the cancellation applicant has failed to adduce evidence to substantiate its assertion that the deletion of the company activities of manufacture and sale of alcoholic beverages, shortly before the contested EUTM was applied for in respect of such goods in Class 33, demonstrates bad faith. The register of company activities in Poland must reflect the actual scope of activity and not the future scope, but it can certainly be altered at a later date. As such, this classification does not mean that the EUTM proprietor does not have a right to enter into the alcoholic beverages market. As regards the assertion that it would be unreasonable or illegal for the EUTM proprietor to expand its business into the manufacture and sale of alcoholic beverages, given that its existing brands have been marketed by way of being healthy foods and drinks, that its charitable and sponsorship activities show a commitment to health and wellbeing, and that Polish law prohibits the advertising of alcohol products which relate them to health, sport, wellbeing, etc. as well as to the young, this too fails to stand up to scrutiny: regardless of the legal situation in Poland and even assuming that the EUTM proprietor would be prohibited from marketing the contested EUTM for alcoholic beverages in that country, it has not been shown that this legal restriction applies in other EU Member States. The existence of an EUTM registration for goods for which it has not yet been used and the reliance on such an EUTM to issue a cease-and-desist letter to companies which prima facie infringe the EUTM proprietor’s rights in this trade mark, is also not indicative of bad faith per se, nor is it in the factual scenario at hand.

The invalidity applicant failed to provide sufficient evidence to establish that the EUTM proprietor acted in bad faith. The EUTM proprietor's explanations were found to be plausible and consistent with honest commercial practices.

The cancellation applicant has demonstrated and proved that, at the time of filing the application, the EUTM proprietor knew of the prior use of an identical sign for services related to adult entertainment and filed an application for goods and services connected with the same subject-matter, whose purpose cannot be reconciled with the function of the European Union trade mark or with the goals of the European Union Trade Mark Regulation. In making that assessment, it is of particular significance that, during the entire course of the proceedings, the EUTM proprietor disputed neither that it was aware of the existence and use of the earlier mark nor its dishonest conduct at the time of filing the application. The EUTM proprietor’s filings appear the result of a plan or a strategy, consisting in registering modest figurative variations of existing marks for partially overlapping or at least for goods and services having some aspects in common with those for which the earlier rights are protected.

On the basis of the evidence submitted, it is beyond any doubt that the EUTM proprietor, when filing the contested EUTM, knew about the use of the mark ‘Munkens Bisalva’ in relation identical or closely related goods. When the contested mark was filed, a rather long and intense business relationship between the parties had ended. The Board of Appeal concludes that a motivation for filing the contested EUTM was to secure the exclusive marketing and sales rights regarding goods bearing the mark ‘Munkens Bisalva’, which the cancellation applicant was not willing to grant to the EUTM proprietor. Furthermore, the facts and evidence presented, demonstrate that the EUTM proprietor filed the contested EUTM with the primary motivation to impede the business operation of the cancellation applicant, to which the EUTM proprietor had been acting as a trade and sales agent before.

Contrary to the findings of CD, the Board considered that the contested EUTM was filed in bad faith. The sign at issue has been extensively used by the cancellation applicant throughout Germany for merchandising goods, services of provision of food and drink and entertainment services since 2014. In particular, the SCHUETZENFESTZELT is one of the main beer tents at the Oktoberfest in Munich. Based on this, the EUTM proprietor knew or should have known about the existence of this sign at the time of filing the contested mark. Contrary to the findings of CD, the fact that the cancellation applicant also used its sign as two separate words linked by a hyphen is immaterial due to the minor difference with the contested EUTM and the fact that in any event the cancellation applicant also used the sign as one word. Moreover, the EUTM proprietor is the owner of at least another  20 EUTM registrations for signs in very distinct fields of business (such as, inter alia, helicopters, automobiles, clothing, entertainment, sporting equipment), all applied for in the space of less than two months between July and September 2022. All of these signs were used but had not been registerered by the companies using them. After securing registration, the EUTM proprietor then contacted the company using the sign, using the registration as leverage to obtain license payments. The EUTM proprietor itself does not appear to have its own business operations; in the company register it is indicated as a real estate company. Considering that its registered marks cover goods and services in completely different business areas, it is implausible that the EUTM proprietor had planned to do business in all of these areas and much more likely that it never intended to use the trade marks at all. Many of the EUTM proprietor's registered signs have already been cancelled in similar proceedings (listed in the decision); one of these parallel cases is dealt with by R 2480/2023-2.

results.quotation: (04/12/2024, R 0433/2024‑5, DEVICE OF A DEMONSTRATOR THROWING A BOUQUET OF FLOWERS (fig.))
results.nickName: DEVICE OF A DEMONSTRATOR THROWING A BOUQUET OF FLOWERS (fig.)
results.summary: The claimed bad faith based on refiling of the same mark by the EUTM proprietor has not been shown. When the EUTM proprietor filed the contested mark (second mark) a request for declaration of invalidity against the first mark has already been filed. Therefore, from the outset the filing of the contested mark cannot be considered a refiling with the aim of circumventing the rules concerning the obligation to use the first mark. Moreover, the EUTM proprietor explained coherently the circumstances that led to the filing of the contested mark. Therefore the claimed bad faith has not been shown and the request for declaration of invalidity had to be rejected in its entirety.

The request for invalidity was filed by a company of the same group as the EUTM holder. There is evidence (national court judgments) that the EUTM holder's director misused a power granted by the mother company's director to illegally purchase all shares in the company (the EUTM holder, as a legal person). However, the request for invalidity based on bad faith is dismissed because the EUTM holder had started using, and had registered the contested mark at national and EU levels, before the relationships with the mother company deteriorated. The question of ownership of the proprietor’s shares is unrelated to the legitimate interest which the EUTM proprietor (as a legal person) justifies in filing the contested mark. The fact that the applicant’s parent company was the victim of fraud when the EUTM holder's director bought back the shares in the company has a direct impact only on the question of ownership of the proprietor’s capital, and not on the commercial logic of the contested filing.
The individual liability of the EUM holder’s director when buying back the shares in the company is therefore independent of the conduct of the proprietor, taken as a legal person, which could legitimately file the contested mark in order to consolidate its intangible assets as part of the natural expansion of its commercial activity.

results.quotation: (26/11/2024, R 2061/2023‑2, RAPPRESENTAZIONE DI UN ELEMENTO ORNAMENTALE (fig.))
results.summary: The Board confirmed the invalidity of the contested mark for the bad faith of the applicant at the moment of the filing. The mark at issue consists of a specific feature of a well-known monument of the city of Verona. Before the filing, it had been used by the city council as a symbol of the city , as well as by economic operators and other local entities and association in such a way as to represent their connection with Verona. Once the owner of the mark applied for it, it sent a series of ceise and desist letters to many of those local operators, including the local football team and its supporters, that had been using the sign till then. The cancellation applicant proved the illegal intention of the owner to prevent others from the use of the mark and to take advantage of the fact that it was considered to be the symbol of the city of Verona.

Bad faith has not been established.The evidence submitted by the invalidity applicant fails to identify any goods and hence does not support its allegation of prior use ‘for identical or similar goods’. Moreover, the evidence submitted by the invalidity applicant does not establish the identity of the customers that requested goods bearing the ‘MAN SCENT’ trade mark. Accordingly, it is apparent that this evidence also does not establish use by a ‘third party’ as alleged by the invalidity applicant. The new evidence filed before the Board by the invalidity applicant, even if considered admissible in accordance with Article 27(4) EUTMDR as it is aimed to supplement and reinforce the evidence filed before the first instance, does not bring much information on the alleged earlier use of the mark. Furthermore, there is no evidence on file that would allow for a conclusion that the EUTM proprietor intended to block the invalidity applicant or did actually prevent the use of the mark by the invalidity applicant.

results.summary: The contested EUTM is to be declared invalid pursuant to Article 60(1)(c) EUTMR in conjunction with Article 8(4) EUTMR. The cancellation applicant has proven that under Italian law it had acquired the non-registered trade mark 'MX-sensor' for electronic sensors for measuring tire pressure. The evidence taken in its entirety shows that the cancellation applicant used the sign ‘MX-sensor’ for electronic sensors for measuring tire pressure in Italy between at least 2017 and 2023, i.e. before the filing of the contested EUTM until and beyond the filing of the invalidity request. Taking into account the sales figures for the years 2017 to February 2019 and the fact that the ‘MX-sensor’ products were offered online throughout Italy, the evidence is sufficient to prove that the use of the sign ‘MX-sensor’ before the filing date of the contested EUTM was of more than mere local significance and that this use continued at the time when the invalidity request was filed. The sign is also used as an indication of commercial origin.

The EUTM holder is a partner in Eastern EU of the cancellation applicant. Both parties had interests in the same group known as "Bentley" or "Bentley Farm", which is active in the farming industry. Bad faith was established without distinguishing the goods and services used by or for farmers, and those which related to human care (beauty services, nursing homes etc.). After establishing bad faith in respect of the majority of the goods and services, the Board considers that the existence of business relations between the parties operates a shift of the burden of proof onto the EUTM holder to explain the commercial logic underlying the filing for specific goods and services. This is so whether or not the goods or services at issue are prima facie not concerned by a situation of an abuse of right.

The Eutm proprietor's line of defence for the filing of the contested EUTM not only fails to establish its honesty and good faith, but to the contrary tends to further reinforce the conclusion that there was and remains an absence of those qualities.

results.summary:
The registration of a mark for goods in an incorrect class does not in itself demonstrate a dishonest intention. In any event, the incorrect classification was not such as to confer on the EUTM applicant any advantage over its competitors (§ 38-41).

Bad faith cannot be presumed on the basis of the mere finding that the EUTM applicant had no economic activity corresponding to the goods and services at the date of filing of the contested mark. The fact that the contested mark was revoked for the goods and services in Classes 29 and 35 for lack of genuine use does not mean that the proprietor acted in bad faith when it applied for registration of that mark, in the absence of other relevant and consistent evidence. Revocation proceedings are the appropriate proceedings for reducing the scope of protection conferred on a trade mark where there is no genuine use (§ 43-46).

It must be borne in mind that where the objective circumstances of the particular case relied on by the applicant for a declaration of invalidity may lead to the rebuttal of the presumption of good faith applying to the application for registration of the mark at issue, it is for the proprietor thereof to provide plausible explanations on the objectives and commercial logic pursued by the application for registration of that mark.66	Considering the absence of any logical explanation or objective commercial trajectory by the EUTM proprietor for the filing of the contested EUTM, and taking into account all the circumstances prevailing in the present case, including the identity of the goods and the identity of the signs, the Board finds that there are strong indications that the contested mark was applied for by its applicant without clear commercial logic and with no intention to use it, but rather to block the entry of the cancellation applicant on the EU market. 
Furthermore, , the cancellation applicant has proven that the earlier rights enjoy copyright protection in China and that the applicant is the proprietor of the earlier copyrights. Furthermore, the applicant submitted all the necessary legislation and arguments proving that the earlier copyrights enjoy copyright protection in Spain and that the use of the contested EUTM can be prohibited in Spain under the Spanish Law on Intellectual Property. Consequently, the invalidity application is well-founded under Article 60(2)(c) EUTMR and the contested EUTM must be declared invalid in its entirety.

The application of national law and the interpretation of a contract governed by national law are matters for the national courts alone, without prejudice to the explicit provisions of EUTMR which expressly refer to national law. Therefore, EUIPO does not have jurisdiction to interpret the terms of a contract for the sale of shares governed by Spanish law (§ 38).

Even though contractual relations may provide indicia of bad faith, the evidence adduced by the invalidity applicant is not sufficient to prove bad faith where it shows only that there was a disagreement between the parties as to the interpretation and scope of the obligations resulting from a specific clause in an agreement, without, however, proving the EUTM proprietor’s dishonest intention or its intention to infringe the invalidity applicant’s rights at the time when the application for registration of the mark at issue was filed (§ 39).

The practice of a company continuing to use the same sign that it used previously in its business dealings and with its customers, to indicate the commercial origin of the goods and services, and especially after the sale of shares involving the departure of certain shareholders, is part of a system of fair competition and constitutes normal commercial practice. The registration and use of a sign which has already been used in the course of trade by the same company is part of a fair commercial logic which is not detrimental to third parties (§ 61, 65).

It was then affected by the termination of the permission to use that right and according its own statements its economic existence depended on the right to use that mark. results.outcome: Cancellation totally upheld and EUTM/IR declared partially invalid

results.summary: The application for the contested trade mark was filed for purely speculative purposes in order to obtain financial compensation and thus in bad faith. In particular,the EUTM proprietor knew or should have known at the time of filing the contested EUTM that the cancellation applicant had been using the contested EUTM and a similar sign for at least highly similar goods on the market for more than 10 years (§ 39-40). The EUTM proprietor did not file the application for the contested EUTM in the context of a comprehensible business model but primarily in order to use the mark as a means of leverage for license payments (§ 64). This follows from the fact that the EUTM proprietor does not have its own business operations. Moreover, the mark applied for are completely different and relate to completely different business areas. It cannot be assumed that the EUTM proprietor actually intended to do business in all these areas. Therefore, at the time of filing the contested EUTM, the EUTM proprietor had no recognisable intention to us the mark according to its main function as an indication of origin for the relevant goods (§ 51-56). Nevertheless, shortly after the contested trade mark was registered, the EUTM proprietor sent a letter to the cancellation applicant with the aim of agreeing a "solution" in the form of a licence payment. The UM proprietor also proceeded in a similar manner towards other companies (§ 58).

results.summary:
Where at the time of filing the contested EUTM, the EUTM proprietor had the uncontested intention to use that mark for all the goods covered by the application for registration, the case-law – according to which the application may constitute an act of bad faith only for part of the goods or services – is not applicable. 29/01/2020, C‑371/18, Sky, EU:C:2020:45, § 80, is set in a context where a finding of bad faith is based on the lack of intention to use the contested mark for the goods and services covered by the registration. It is thus in that context that the CJ held that, where the lack of intention to use the trade mark in accordance with its essential functions relates only to certain goods or services, such an application constitutes an act of bad faith only in so far as it relates to those goods or services (§ 42-44).

results.quotation: (25/04/2024, R 1275/2022‑1, DEVICE OF RED AND WHITE CONCENTRIC RINGS (fig.))
results.nickName: DEVICE OF RED AND WHITE CONCENTRIC RINGS (fig.)
results.summary:
A sign which is simple and is constituted by a basic geometric figure, such as a circle, a line, a rectangle or a conventional pentagon, is not, in itself, capable of conveying a message which consumers will be able to remember, with the result that they will not regard it as a trade mark unless it has acquired distinctive character through use. The sign at issue here consists of a red circle (a dot) inside a red outline of a circle, on a white background. As such, the sign is unable to convey any message capable of being remembered by consumers, and it will not be perceived as an indication of commercial origin allowing consumers to differentiate between the goods and service of one under-taking as opposed to those of others. It cannot be said that this simple reproduction of two such banal and simple geometric shapes (a dot and a circle) one inside the other enjoys even a minimum degree of singularity and memorability.
While it may be possible for a combination of simple geometric shapes to enjoy the mini-mum degree of distinctive character, depending on the representation, number of shapes and manner of combination, this is not so in the case at hand: the simple placement of a simple red circle within the outline of a red circle does not endow the sign with distinctive character for any of the services concerned.
As shown by the cancellation applicant, such banal figurative devices are used as mere decoration, also as bullet points and as ‘radio buttons’ in particular on web¬page services, such as selection of goods or services, as has been shown and indeed as is commonly known. The mere fact that the circular outline and inner circle in the contested sign are in red is not in itself sufficient to endow the sign with distinctive character, since red is a commonly used colour merely to catch the attention, and does not alter its simple geometrical character in any way.
It is no less simple than, for example, basic geometric shapes such as triangle inside a circle, an orange circular outline, an octagon-shaped frame or a triangular black frame with an offset inner triangular white space.
Furthermore, the red circular outline, as pointed out by the cancellation applicant, is not a distinctive device, and is used commonly in daily life merely to draw attention to what is inside (a speed limit, etc.). To that extent, merely placing a red circle inside such a red circular outline does not suffice to provide the latter with any distinctive character either.
The argument that commonplace signs may in a different context function as trade marks, and that many examples of successful trade marks which, in other contexts, are descriptive and/or non-distinctive, misses the point. The contested EUTM contains no such restriction on how the sign may be used in the context of the services at issue. Actual use of the trade mark is only relevant with respect to the claim of distinctive character acquired through use in accordance with Article 59(2) EUTMR in conjunction with Article 7(3) EUTMR. However, the EUTM proprietor has explicitly stated that Article 7(3) EUTMR is not relevant to these proceedings, and that the evidence of use the EUTM proprietor filed at first instance was merely to rebut the submission from the cancellation applicant that the contested EUTM had not even been used in the EU.
The assertion that there is no question of members of the public associating or linking the sign with bullet points/selection boxes/menu buttons, since these are clearly not trade marks, and are not being used in a trade mark context, does not bear scrutiny because it is a circular argument. It assumes that the contested EUTM will be perceived ab initio as a trade mark, which is unfounded and unconvincing as explained above.
Contrary to the EUTM proprietor’s arguments, it certainly cannot be said that the examples given by the cancellation applicant are not similar to the contested sign. In particular, the examples of use of such symbols as online ‘radio buttons’ as well as used as merely decorative devices and also as bullet points, which are essentially identical to the contested sign here. As for any tiny differences such as the size of the inner circle and distance from the outer circle, these are determinative of nothing because the relevant public will not notice such minute details by examining what are immediately familiar simple geometrical shapes with such a degree of scrutiny.
In all the circumstances,s, the composition of the sign does not endow it with any distinctive character, since the shapes and their combination are too simple, and they are commonly used as non-distinctive devices such as online radio buttons for selecting goods and services, as bullet points or indeed as merely banal decoration. It is irrelevant that signs could allegedly be used in a trade mark context, since that does not detract from the established principle that signs which are too simple also lack distinctive character, but instead relates to Article 7(3) EUTMR which is not relied upon

results.ipRight: EUTM on which IA is based

results.summary: The cancellation applicant was not able to prove bad faith of the EUTM proprietor. The parties involved formally worked together cooperatively, the cancellation applicant acted as a sales manager (distributor) for the goods (special health care beds) produced by the EUTM proprietor (supplier). After the cooperation ended, disputes arose which side owns rights regarding the signs / trademarks related to the goods (special health care beds). The cancellation applicant mainly referred to an own prior use of the sign in question, a breach of contractual duties by the EUTM proprietor, and the malicious intention of the EUTM proprietor to register the EUTM (the cancellation applicant argued this only happened to damage its business). However, the cancellation applicant was not able to sufficiently prove this line of argumentation, serious doubts remain. Therefore, the cancellation request must (at least) be refused due to the burden of proof.
