results.quotation: (06/03/2024, T‑639/22, GEOGRAPHICAL NORWAY EXPEDITION (fig.) / NAPAPIJRI geographic (fig.) et al., EU:T:2024:149)
results.nickName: GEOGRAPHICAL NORWAY EXPEDITION (fig.) / NAPAPIJRI geographic (fig.) et al.
results.summary:
When the identity or similarity with a prior sign is invoked to establish that the registration of the contested trade mark was applied for in bad faith, the manner in which this prior sign has been used on the market from its inception cannot be overlooked in the assessment of bad faith, regardless of whether this sign has been registered as a trade mark. In the present case, the use of the Norwegian flag as well as the graphical elements of the signs in question should be taken into account in the analysis of the similarities between the figurative sign NAPAPIJRI geographic and the contested mark (§ 48-54).

Although the EUTM proprietor has an earlier French word mark ‘GEØGRAPHICAL NØRWAY’ registered prior to the filing of the invalidity applicant’s figurative EUTM ‘NAPAPIJRI geographic’, the filing of the contested EUTM does not align with a commercial logic aimed at securing protection across the EU for the said French mark. Firstly, the EUTM proprietor had already applied for an IR designating the EU based on that French mark. Secondly, the contested mark cannot be regarded as a mere variant of the French word mark ‘GEØGRAPHICAL NØRWAY’, due to the figurative nature of the contested trade mark, the addition of the term ‘expedition’, the composition of its elements, and the claimed colours. In any event, even if the contested EUTM were considered a simple variant of the French mark, this would not automatically exclude the bad faith of the EUTM proprietor (§ 69-72).

While no contractual relationship between the parties to the dispute has been established, the evidence submitted demonstrates that the market relationship between the parties was fraught with tension. From at least 2002, the parties were well aware of each other, with their legal predecessors engaging in multiple oppositions. These past conflicts, albeit involving different trade marks than those in the current dispute, have to be taken into account. They are integral to the sequence of events leading up to the filing of the contested EUTM, providing valuable context for evaluating the EUTM proprietor’s intentions (§ 77-80).

The invalidity applicant provided several examples of trade marks that the legal predecessor of the EUTMR proprietor attempted to register, either in France or with the EUIPO, consisting of signs very similar to marks of other companies, also aiming to ride on their coattails. That evidence, regardless of it concerning different trade marks from the signs in question, is relevant for assessing bad faith (§ 87-90).
Where trade marks present some similarity, even if faint, a comprehensive assessment must be carried out to determine whether, notwithstanding the low degree of similarity between them, there exists, due to the presence of other relevant factors such as the reputation of the earlier trade mark, a likelihood of confusion or a link between these trade marks in the mind of the relevant public. Having concluded the absence of a link ‘even if the other criteria were met’, the BoA did not undertake any examination of these other factors, particularly regarding whether they would allow the relevant public to establish such a link despite certain differences between the conflicting marks (§ 116-118).

results.entityName: NEW YORKER FINE LAGER BEER NY

results.summary: Contrary to the findings of CD, the Board concluded that bad faith was not proven. Good faith is presumed to exist until the opposite is proven, and it is only the cancellation applicant that carries the burden of proof. It follows that it is not for the EUTM proprietor to justify its legitimate intentions. In this case, the contested EUTM was filed only once the cancellation applicant's identical earlier right had expired. Given that the EUTM proprietor had contributed to its creation, it seems reasonable and commercially logical that he might have wanted to (re)register it. As for the alleged lack of use of the contested EUTM at the time of filing the cancellation action, it is irrelevant given that the grace period for use of said mark had not yet ended at that date.

39	The existence of trade mark registrations per se is not proof of use on the market (even if such rights are properly substantiated which is not the case here). The evidence filed does not suffice even to prove genuine use of the claimed earlier sign, let alone that it is allegedly well-known in some Arabic countries, and so there is no prima facie reason shown why the EUTM proprietor would have ‘had’ to have known about it when it applied for the contested EUTM. The allegations that the EUTM proprietor had such knowledge at this date based on the screenshot of the on-screen communication also do not bear scrutiny. Not only is this screenshot undated but the cancellation applicant does not even assert that the communication took place before the date of application for the contested EUTM; indeed, he only says that this took place prior to the filing of the application for a declaration of invalidity (May 2022, over three years after the filing date of the contested EUTM), to ‘avoid damage from the improper use of its trade mark’. This is consistent with the undated extract being an exchange which took place after the cancellation applicant learnt of the filing or even registration of the contested EUTM. Nor does it appear to identify the cancellation applicant and no corroborating evidence in that regard has been lodged. He makes no reference to any specific trade marks at all. Furthermore, even assuming that all of that had been proven (quod non), it at most shows that the prima facie intention of the EUTM proprietor was not to harm anyone. Nor does a comparison of the conflicting signs show, as argued, that the EUTM proprietor has copied the earlier sign. Other than the descriptive Arabic words, which are different in their figurative depictions, their configuration, size and colours, the signs coincide only as to the dark green colour used just for the Arabic descriptive wording in the contested EUTM and two small Arabic words below it, for which there is no counterpart in the earlier sign, and the fact that each sign has a two-leaf logo and in very similar colours. However, the leaves in the earlier sign are simple and cartoon-like, in a lighter shade of green, while those in the contested sign are more detailed and realistic, and they are reproduced in different parts of the signs at different angles. Furthermore, the use of a simple leaf figurative element for vegetables and foodstuffs is highly allusive and has not been shown to form a distinctive part of either sign. The overall impression is certainly not very similar at all, and it cannot be said from a comparison of the signs that the EUTM proprietor knew of and copied the earlier sign, as alleged. As for the silence of the EUTM proprietor, and the assertion that this suffices to reverse the burden of proof as to its bad faith, the Board cannot agree. The EUTM proprietor is under no legal obligation to expend time and money on what it may view as vexatious or frivolous legal claims against it. Contrary to the arguments of the cancellation applicant, no objective circumstances at all have been shown that are capable of reversing the presumption of good faith applicable to the application for the contested EUTM. It is irrelevant that the Office has found that to be the case in the context of different factual and legal constellations of other proceedings. In all the circumstances, the arguments and evidence presented by the cancellation applicant are insufficient to lead to the conclusion that the EUTM proprietor acted in bad faith when applying for the contested EUTM.

results.entityName: KRAKUS Premium since 1962 POLISH VODKA Pure Grain

results.summary:
A strategy that consists of filing successive national trade mark applications for the same sign in order to obtain a blocking position beyond the six-month priority period and to monopolise the sign applied for, is not to be regarded as legitimate commercial conduct, but rather as conduct that runs counter to honest practices in industrial and commercial matters and which is contrary to the objectives of the CTMR; therefore, this strategy is regarded as being in bad faith. Bad faith particularly follows from the fact that there was no intention to use the contested mark. The fact that the applicant was not aware of any identical or similar sign on the priority date or at the time of filing of the contested trade mark cannot call into question the finding of bad faith, where the existence of an abusive strategy of repeated applications to monopolise a contested sign without any intention to use it has been sufficiently established (§ 50, 55, 58, 74).
The party submitting facts and evidence for the first time before the BoA must show how that submission satisfies the conditions laid down in Article 27(4) EUTMDR. Despite being expressly requested to do so by the BoA rapporteur, the applicant did not explain which specific parts of the new and voluminous evidence were relevant to the appeal proceedings and what conclusions could be drawn from it for the present case. Therefore, the BoA was able to find that this evidence was not to be taken into account under Article 27(4)(a) EUTMDR as, prima facie, not relevant to the outcome of the case (§ 65, 67).
The mere fact that the BoA rapporteur was a member of another BoA that adopted the decision in another case, even if that other case involved a company whose managing director was the same as that of the contested EUTM proprietor, cannot be used to infer that the rapporteur was biased. Furthermore, in the absence of any other evidence of bias or personal prejudice on the part of the rapporteur against the EUTM proprietor, the fact that the BoA rapporteur and the invalidity applicant’s lawyer are members of the same association for further education in the field of intellectual property, even if it is considered to be proven, is not capable of establishing the existence of a breach of the requirement of impartiality affecting the legality of the contested decision (§ 92, 93).

results.ipRight: EUTM on which IA is based
results.outcome: Closed without deciding on the merits
results.summary: The cancellation application was based on two grounds: Article 59(1)(b) EUTMR (bad faith) (‘ground 1’) and Article 60(1)(a) EUTMR in conjunction with Article 8(1)(b) and Article 8(2)(c) EUTMR (‘ground 2’). The Cancellation Division rejected ground 1 and correctly upheld the cancellation application based on ground 2, since the EUTM proprietor had filed the contested EUTM in bad faith. There was no need to examine the cancellation applicant's cross appeal since it was fully successful on one of the invalidity grounds. Also, the cancellation applicant's own appeal was dismissed as inadmissible, since it was not adversely affected by the contested decision, since the contested EUTM was declared invalid in its entirety.

results.summary: The fact that the EUTM proprietor registered a trade mark which may be similar to the earlier expired French registration does not amount to bad faith. The invalidity applicant has not demonstrated any dishonest intention on the part of the EUTM proprietor. The appeal is dismissed.

The fact that the EUTM proprietor registered a trade mark which may be similar to the earlier expired French registration does not amount to bad faith. The invalidity applicant has not demonstrated any dishonest intention on the part of the EUTM proprietor. The appeal is dismissed.

The Board agrees with the contested decision that it can be deduced from the particular course of events that the EUTM proprietor’s primary intention was (i) to hinder the cancellation applicant’s activities (by filing for services in Class 41 to which the cancellation applicant explicitly referred in its reply of 18 January 2021), (ii) enhance its position vis-à-vis the cancellation applicant (by re-filing the same word mark ‘SANDOKAN’, given the potential vulnerability of the previous IR) and thus (iii) secure a better position with a view of obtaining some sort of benefit in the negotiations with the cancellation applicant. Bad faith was found to exist.

results.outcome: Cancellation totally upheld and EUTM/IR declared partially invalid

results.summary: The Invalidity applicant claims that the proprietor had filed the contested trade mark in bad faith. However, the evidence does not show bad faith. The proprietor had filed the model number of face masks of a Chinese company as a trade mark. The cancellation applicant filed a number of pieces of evidence to show that he had acquired face masks with that model number legitimately in the Netherlands prior to the filing date of the earlier trade mark. However, this in itself is not, even if it was proven, sufficient to show bad faith.  Nor was there evidence that the Chinese producer was not in agreement with  the registration of the mark. One of the pieces of evidence given by the cancellation applicant was even signed with a Chinese seal that was apparently forgery.

results.quotation: (05/10/2023, R 0379/2023‑2, TARGA FLORIO / FLORIO et al.)
results.summary: The Board agrees that the image of the earlier marks would inevitably be transferred to the contested goods if they were marketed under the contested mark. Indeed, in the beverage industry, tradition and quality are important to consumers as they make a link with the past as a guarantee of authenticity.
The evidence filed by the cancellation applicants does not substantiate the claim of bad faith. The appeal is dismissed.

results.quotation: (28/09/2023, R 0770/2023‑1, INSTITUTO CANARIO DE MARCAS Y PATENTES ICAMP (fig.))
results.entityName: INSTITUTO CANARIO DE MARCAS Y PATENTES ICAMP
results.nickName: INSTITUTO CANARIO DE MARCAS Y PATENTES ICAMP (fig.)
results.summary: In light of the facts and evidence submitted the contested EUTM had not been filed in breach of Article 7/1)(b), (c), (d) or (g) EUTMR. In addition, the contested EUTM had not been filed in bad faith within the meaning of Article 59(1)(b) EUTMR.

The contested sign, containing the combined word elements ‘FIZZ’ and ‘Cider’, informs the English-speaking consumer and professionals familiar with English about relevant characteristics of the contested goods, as does the word ‘FIZZ’ alone, the figurative elements in the sign merely serve to emphasise the word ‘FIZZ’ in particular, and as such the sign as a whole is directly descriptive for all the contested goods in the sense of Article 7(1)(c) EUTMR. Since it is descriptive, it is also non-distinctive under Article 7(1)(b) EUTMR. The claim to acquired distinctiveness through use throughout the EU fails: even if the evidence filed could be argued to show that it has acquired such enhanced distinctive character in  Finland, Estonia, Latvia and Lithuania , the EUTM proprietor has fallen far short of showing, for the remaining EU Member States where the relevant public has a good understanding of English (at least Sweden, Denmark and the Netherlands as well as in Ireland and Malta, not to mention other countries where the professional public is likely to understand English), the market share of the brand, the extent to which the use of the mark has been intense, geographically widespread and continuous; the amount invested by the entity to promote the brand, or the proportion of the relevant class of persons who, because of the trade mark, identify the goods as originating from a particular undertaking, including any statements from chambers of commerce and industry or other trade or professional associations. In this context, in Germany and Austria the sign could also be understood.

results.summary: From the evidence submitted by both parties it becomes evident that the EUTM proprietor launched an idea for a project named NYKUR consisting in marketing organic vodka with water from the Faroes Islands. From the beginning, the cancellation applicant has been involved in that specific project. The parties undertook different steps and activities to that end and from the contact and exchange between the parties it might be clearly inferred that the protection and preservation of the denomination NYKUR as a trade mark applied to the EUTM proprietor. The filing of the contested mark is a logical step in order to protect the denomination NYKUR as a mark in the vodka market. The cancellation applicant has not provided any evidence that the EUTM proprietor should have informed or asked the cancellation applicant's agreement before filing the contested mark. In lack of any evidence the request for declaration of invalidity based on bad faith had to be dismissed.

The EUTM proprietor acted in bad faith when registering the contested EUTM. The EUTM proprietor knew at the time of filing the contested mark in 2015 and even at the time of filing the German trade mark in 2004 that the cancellation applicant used an almost identical sign for identical goods. Furthermore, the evidence demonstrated that the EUTM proprietor had acted as distributor of ‘CorrosionX’ products of the cancellation applicant in Germany. Accordingly, there was a commercial relationship between the parties before the filing of the contested mark which was sufficiently close to give rise to a duty of fair play, given that a company has a right to expect a certain degree of loyalty from its distributors, or commercial partners. This includes a legitimate expectation not to register the company’s signs as trade marks in the distributor’s name without consent. By filing the contested mark without the consent of the cancellation applicant, the EUTM proprietor breached the duty of fair play, did not act in accordance with honest commercial practices and acted in bad faith within the meaning of Article 59(1)(b) EUTMR.

results.quotation: (05/09/2023, R 2552/2022‑5, QWAN KI DO QUÁN KHÍ DAO (fig.) / QUAN KHI et al.)
results.ipRight: EUTM on which IA is based
results.entityName: QWAN KI DO QUÁN KHÍ DAO
results.nickName: QWAN KI DO QUÁN KHÍ DAO (fig.) / QUAN KHI et al.
results.summary:
Confidentiality request and additional evidence accepted. 

Proof of use of earlier marks (nature and extent of use): ‘QWAN KI DO’ is a Vietnamese martial art that originated in Vietnam and Ancient China. It was founded by the EUTM proprietor in France in 1981. It is the name of a martial art as Tae Kwon Do or Karate and was not used as a trade mark. The earlier mark QWAN KI DO was used either as part of the name of a sport association (Unione Italiana Qwan Ki Do) active in the field of the respectively described martial art, as a descriptive reference to the subject matter of the courses/training sessions this entity organised/provided or as a descriptive reference to a martial art of Vietnamese origin. In quantitative terms, the amount of use shown is anyway extremely low.

Likelihood of confusion: Even assuming that genuine use of any of the earlier marks, and especially of QWAN KI DO, was proven, this verbal element would be considered very weak. Overall, taking into account the, at most, low degree of visual, aural and conceptual similarity, the low degree of distinctiveness of the earlier mark, the greatly differing overall impression and the average level of attention of the relevant public, a likelihood of confusion can be safely excluded, even for identical goods and services.

Bad faith: In their capacities as master and disciple, the parties had a long lasting relationship and cooperation that resulted in the worldwide promotion and development of the martial art QWAN KI DO. As to their mere professional/business relationship, the parties signed a licence agreement in 2004, according to which the EUTM proprietor became the licensee of the cancellation applicant in relation to the latter’s five earlier Italian word marks. However, the evidence on file shows that the EUTM proprietor had the better right over the commercial use of the terms ‘QWAN KI DO / QUAN KHI DAO’ standing for the martial art that he originally created and was legally entitled to file the contested mark.

Unauthorised filing by an agent or representative: Even assuming that the EUTM proprietor would be considered as an ‘agent’ due to a broad interpretation of the licence agreement between the parties, the EUTM proprietor acted in an honest manner having legitimate reasons to justify his filing of the contested EUTM. Since one cumulative condition is not met, there is no need to examine further the remaining conditions of Article 8(3) EUTMR.

results.entityName: GERMANY'S NEXT top kebab by Uncle Charlie

results.quotation: (29/08/2023, R 0193/2023‑2, AUTOFLASHER VEHICLE PROGRAMMING (fig.) / AT AUTOTUNER AUTOMOTIVE TOOL (fig.))
results.nickName: AUTOFLASHER VEHICLE PROGRAMMING (fig.) / AT AUTOTUNER AUTOMOTIVE TOOL (fig.)
results.summary: The common element ‘AUTO’ will be perceived and understood by all the relevant public as it is internationally used to refer to ‘motor vehicle’. It is therefore non-distinctive in relation to the relevant goods and services. Furthermore, the structures of the signs present clear differences in fonts and colours, and their common features, namely the letters combinations on the left side and the verbal elements in two lines on the right  side, is rather usual way to depict complex marks on the market. The common presence of weak elements is not decisive for a finding of a likelihood of confusion. It follows that there is no likelihood of confusion and the invalidity application is rejected insofar as it was based on this ground. The application for invalidity is also rejected insofar it was based on bad faith as the the invalidity applicant failed to demonstrate knowledge of the use made by the earlier mark on the part of the EUTM proprietor, which cannot be presumed. As a consequence, the invalidity application is rejected, the appeal is allowed, and the contested decision is annulled in its entirety.

results.quotation: (24/08/2023, R 1981/2022‑1, NovaBlast / basket with novablast 2 logo et al.)
results.nickName: NovaBlast / basket with novablast 2 logo et al.
results.summary: The cancellation applicant has proven that it used the sign 'NOVABLAST' prior to the filing of the contested EUTM for a running shoe. The evidence also demonstrates that the EUTM proprietor should have known abouth this use due to the broad press coverage and advertising worldwide that accompanied the launch of the shoe, including in China. The only activity undertaken by the EUTM proprietor under the contested EUTM is the filing of an opposition against the EUTM application filed by the cancellation application for the word 'NOVABLAST' and goods in Class 25 that are identical or similar to the goods for which the contested EUTM is registered. In the absence of any arguments or evidence from the EUTM proprietor, there is no indication that it had any intention to use the contested EUTM but with the purpose of preventing the cancellation applicant to continue the use of the ‘NOVABLAST’ sign for its running shoe.

results.quotation: (16/08/2023, R 2391/2022‑4, NEO SAFETY / neo tools et al.)
results.nickName: NEO SAFETY / neo tools et al.
keywords: Similarity of the goods and services
results.summary: The EUTM proprietor departed from accepted principles of ethical behaviour and honest commercial business practices, and thus was acting in bad faith. The EUTM proprietor had a previous business relation with the cancellation applicant and used the word NEO which is the cancellation applicants main brand with the laudatory and descriptive term SAFETY deliberately with the intention of either creating an association with the mark of the cancellation applicant and to ride on the coattails of its attractive force.  Thus consumers could believe that the very similar goods under the contested EUTM were a variation of the cancellation applicants brand, created for perhaps a new line of products. Consequently the EUTM proprietor exploited the recognition of the cancellation applicant’s earlier marks resulting from the long-lasting use of those marks and investment made. The contested EUTM is declared invalid in its entirety.

In light of all the foregoing, putting into context the chronological development of the events, the lack of clear evidence on the sole ownership of the sign, the existence of a previous commercial relationship between the EUTM proprietor and the cancellation applicant´s predecessor-in-title, the identity or closeness of the sign, the corporate name, and the continuous use of the sign by the latter, the absence of a legitimate reason or true reason for registering the sign and the negative consequences of the registration of the EUTM for the business of the cancellation applicant all of which cumulatively assessed show consistent indicia of a dishonest intent on the EUTM proprietor’s part. The registration intentionally sought to obstruct the cancellation applicant in the conduct of its business. As a result, the Cancellation Division rightly declared the contested trade mark invalid pursuant to Article 59(1)(b) EUTMR.

The Board concurs with the Cancellation Division that the US trade mark No 6 259 661 on which the application for a declaration of invalidity is based as regards Article 60(1)(b) EUTMR in conjunction with Article 8(3) EUTMR is not earlier than the contested mark. In that sense, it cannot serve to establish that the cancellation applicant owned a ‘mark’ in the sense of a registered mark for the sign at issue when the EUTM was filed. Furthermore, insofar no additional evidence has been provided in relation to the acquisition of rights through use in the territory of the US to show that the cancellation applicant owned a non-registered mark at the time when the contested EUTM was applied for, the Board considers that the application for invalidity is rejected as inadmissible insofar as it is based on Article 60(1)(b) EUTMR in conjunction with Article 8(3) EUTMR. Regarding the assessment of bad faith, the Board observes that, while it is true that some more consistent evidence would have been preferred to show the relationship between the parties (such as a collaboration or employment contract between the parties or between the cancellation applicant and Mr Kimi Lee), good faith on the part of the EUTM proprietor is presumed until the opposite is proven and the responsibility for showing that rests on the cancellation applicant. Nevertheless, the standard the latter must reach should not be so strenuous that the cancellation applicant is placed at a significant disadvantage. The evidence and arguments submitted by the cancellation applicant are sufficient to conclude, in the absence of evidence to the contrary, that the objective circumstances of the present case indicate bad faith on the part of the EUTM proprietor in filing the contested EUTM. The business relationship between the EUTM proprietor’s owner (Mr Kimi Lee) and the cancellation applicant’s company serves to show that the EUTM proprietor was aware of the mark and also of its use within the European Union and beyond. Thus, the EUTM proprietor was also aware of the legitimate interest in the contested sign on the part of the cancellation applicant and it would appear that the EUTM proprietor used its knowledge of the cancellation applicant and its mark in order to obtain an exclusive right for itself, constituting a breach of a duty of fair play towards the cancellation applicant. To that extent, the registration of the contested mark would appear to be an act to deprive the cancellation applicant of its rights to a sign that had been previously used in a number of countries, including countries within the European Union, covering identical or similar goods.

The Board found that there are no consistent indicia that there was a dishonest intent on the part of the EUTM proprietor that would depart from the common business practices at the time of the EUTM registration.
In particular, all the factual circumstances relied on by the cancellation applicant are not inconsistent with honest practices: firstly, the contested EUTM was used for some of the relevant services for a continuous period of time from 1997 until, at least 2020. Therefore, it cannot be said that the EUTM filing pursued the sole objective of preventing a third party from entering the market and that the EUTM proprietor never had the intention of using the contested EUTM. Secondly, the evidence submitted by the cancellation applicant cannot prove any cessation of use of the ‘E Plus’ trade mark but mainly prove the fact that the ‘E Plus’ company disappeared after the merger with the EUTM proprietor’s company and that the ‘E Plus’ tariffs have been integrated under the ‘O2’ umbrella. Thirdly, even if the contested EUTM is registered for a broader list of services than the earlier EUTM, many of the relevant services correspond to the field of actual and potential commercial activity of the EUTM proprietor. Fourthly, the fact that some of the contested services do not correspond to the EUTM proprietor’s commercial activity is not, in principle, proof of bad faith. In any event, it is not a condition for the registration of a trade mark that the proprietor’s field of business coincides with the list of goods and services applied for. Finally, it has not been proven that the filing of the contested EUTM was made to circumvent use obligation of other earlier ‘E Plus’ trade marks. In particular, there is no evidence of repeated applications for ‘E Plus’ by the EUTM proprietor. The contested EUTM differs from the earlier one to the extent that the former is a word mark while the latter is a figurative mark. The contested services are partially different from the earlier goods and services. In addition, the application for registration of the contested EUTM was filed approximately 23 months after the publication of the article where the EUTM proprietor informed the customers of its intention to allegedly discontinue the ‘E Plus’ trade mark. The fact that the EUTM proprietor actually showed some use of the trade mark, including at the time of filing, further supports the conclusion that the cancellation applicant’s argument is not substantiated.

The Board considers that there are no consistent indicia that there was a dishonest intent on the part of the EUTM proprietor that would depart from the common business practices at the time of the EUTM registration.
In particular, all the factual circumstances relied on by the applicant are not inconsistent with honest practices: firstly, it cannot be said that the EUTM filing pursued the sole objective of preventing a third party from entering the market and that the defendant (EUTM proprietor) never had the intention of using the contested EUTM. Secondly, the evidence submitted by the appellant (cancellation applicant) cannot prove any cessation of use of the ‘E-Plus’ trade mark but mainly prove the fact that the ‘E-Plus’ company disappeared after the merger with the defendant’s (EUTM proprietor’s) company and that the ‘E Plus’ tariffs have been integrated under the ‘O2’ umbrella. Thirdly, even if the contested EUTM is registered for a broader list of goods than the earlier EUTM, many of the relevant goods correspond to the field of actual and potential commercial activity of the defendant (EUTM proprietor). Fourthly, the fact that some of the contested goods do not correspond to the defendant’s (EUTM proprietor’s) commercial activity is in principle unable to prove bad faith. In any event, it is not a condition for the registration of a trade mark that the proprietor’s field of business coincides with the list of goods and services applied for. Finally, it has not been proven that the filing of the contested EUTM was made to circumvent use obligation of other earlier ‘E-Plus’ trade marks. In particular, there is no evidence of repeated applications for ‘E-Plus’ by the defendant (EUTM proprietor). What holds true is that the contested EUTM differs from the earlier one to the extent that the former is a word mark while the latter is a figurative mark. The contested goods are partially different from the earlier ones. In addition, the application for registration of the contested EUTM was filed approximately 23 months after the publication of the article where the defendant (EUTM proprietor) informed the customers of its intention to allegedly discontinue the ‘E-Plus’ trade mark. The fact that the defendant (EUTM proprietor) actually showed some use of the trade mark, including at the time of filing, further supports the conclusion that the appellant’s (cancellation applicant’s) argument is not substantiated

The objective circumstances ndicate bad faith on the part of the EUTM proprietor in filing the EUTM. By having the EUTM registration, the EUTM proprietor was able to secure the closure of the invalidity applicant’s Instagram pages, which were only later reinstated  following the invalidity applicant’s defence. His intention in filing the EUTM was to thwart the invalidity applicant’s activities as a competitor in the same microfibre cloth business in which he was operating since 2016 inasmuch as he admits that he did not want to partner with the invalidity applicant.  EUTM proprietor's claim that invlaidity stole the PELLA trade mark, which he himslef created, is unsubstantiated.

Taking into account all the factual circumstances relevant to the present case, it is concluded that the filing of the application of the contested EUTM, insofar as it includes the goods and services already covered by an earlier identical EUTM registration, was made without any commercial logic. There is no justification for protecting trade marks unless they are actually used on the market according to the genuine use requirements prescribed by relevant (national/EU) law. Therefore, registrations that are intended to prevent third parties from registering or using identical/similar rights for identical/similar goods and/or services in the future (in relation to all or some of the identified goods and/or services), without any honest commercial logic, may be an indicator of the applicant’s dishonest intention. Therefore, in this case, the application of the contested EUTM is considered to have been made in bad faith and the EUTM is invalidated to the extent it covers identical goods and services to those of the earlier identical mark.

The EUTM proprietor filed the contested mark which with the intention to use the family name of the invalidity applicant as trade mark although there was an express agreement between the parties not to use commercially the invalidity applicants name. The surrounding circumstances corroborate the EUTM proprietor's bad faith when it filed the contested mark. Therefore the EUTM is to be declared invalid on that ground.

7. Since the cancellation applicants have failed to bear the burden of proof and the application for a declaration of invalidity on the grounds of bad faith must be rejected as unfounded.

1. Following the remittal of the case from the General Court, the Fourth Board had to render a new decision on the appeal, respecting the ratio decidendi of the General Court’s judgment.
2. The additional evidence submitted by the cancellation applicants, together with the statement of grounds, is admissible.
3. However, the new evidence and facts submitted by the cancellation applicants after the judgment of the General Court are inadmissible.
4. No earlier ʻneheraʼ trade mark was registered at the time when the application for registration of the contested mark was filed. Nor has it been established or even alleged that Mr Jan Nehera’s name enjoyed specific legal protection on the date on which the application for registration of the contested mark was filed. 
5. Nor were the former Czechoslovak trade mark ʻneheraʼ and Mr Jan Nehera’s used by the cancellation applicants or by a third party to market clothing at the date of filing of application for registration of the contested mark. 
6. The evidence does not establish that in 2013, the former Czechoslovak trade mark still enjoyed a certain reputation in the Czech Republic, Slovakia or in other Member States or that Mr Jan Nehera’s name was still famous among a significant portion of the relevant public.
7. The cancellation applicants have not established that the EUTM proprietor was driven by a dishonest state of mind or intention when he filed the application for registration of the contested mark. The evidence shows that his intention was ʻto pay a tribute to both quality of outer garment industry in Czechoslovakia prior to WWII and also to great figures of that time industry including Jan NEHERA, Rolny, etc.ʼ.
8. Since the cancellation applicants have failed to bear the burden of proof and the application for a declaration of invalidity on the grounds of bad faith must be rejected as unfounded.

results.nickName: Juana de Origen (fig.) / Juana (fig.) et al.
results.summary: The invalidity applicant has shown that the proprietor of the contested EUTM knew the invlalidity applicant's marks containing as main element the name 'JUANA' and the depiction of a woman offering cornmeal registered in Venezuela. It has been further established that the EUTM proprietor intentionally has chosen the contested mark which is similar to the invalidity applicant's marks which are well-known in Venezuela. By the application and registration f the contested mark it wanted to attract the interest of the immigrants from Venezuela in Europe for their nutritional goods and thereby take profit of the well-known venezolano marks of the invalidity applicant. This intention which was present at the date of filing of the contested mark amounts to bad faith and therefore the contested mark has to be declared invalid in its entirety.

results.nickName: GARAGE ITALIA TAILOR-MADE (fig.) / I GARAGE ITALIA CUSTOMS (fig.)
results.summary:
The Board considers that the EUTM proprietor was in bad faith when filing the contested mark.
The EUTM proprietor was aware of the applicant’s business at the time of filing the trade mark at issue. The evidence showed that the cancellation applicant’s business has been subject of an extensive media campaign, which enabled the cancellation applicant to acquire reputation among the public. This fact could not be ignored by the EUTM proprietor.
The intention of the EUTM proprietor when filing the contested trade mark was to create an association in the mind of the relevant public between its goods and the cancellation applicant’s trade mark. Indeed, according to the evidence, (i) the contested EUTM contains the same wording for which the applicant’s earlier mark has acquired reputation, (ii) it is very likely that the goods for which the proprietor applied constitute a commercial extension of the activities carried on by the cancellation applicant (iii) the filing of the contested EUTM took place at a time when the applicant’s business had already benefited from an intense media campaign.
The behavior of the EUTM proprietor following the filing of the contested trade mark tends to support that it sought to take unfair advantage of the cancellation applicant trade mark’s reputation. The EUTM proprietor failed to prove that its business strategy could justify the filing of the EUTM at issue.
Pursuant to Article 59(1)(b) EUTMR, the contested mark is invalid for all the goods and services claimed. Therefore, there is no need to examine the other grounds for invalidity relied on by the applicant for a declaration of invalidity.

results.summary: All the cumulative conditions for the application of Article 60(1)(b) in conjunction with Article 8(3) EUTMR are met in relation to all the goods, the request for an assignment pursuant to Article 21(2)(a) EUTMR is granted.

The reasons why the BoA considered that former members of an association continued to enjoy legal protection on the earlier sign following the acquisition of the corresponding national mark by the EUTM proprietor are not disclosed in a clear and unequivocal manner (§ 30). The findings that, on the one hand, legal protection of the earlier sign can be inferred from the multilateral agreement between the members of the association and, on the other hand, that the termination of that agreement had no effect on that legal protection, are contradictory (§ 31). The BoA should have explained on what legal basis it found the legal protection favouring the members of the association (§ 32, 36).

The determination of the legal rules conferring legal protection on the earlier sign is a decisive factor in ascertaining, first, whether the applicant had knowledge thereof at the time of filing the contested trade mark and, secondly, whether, when filing that trade mark, the applicant was not, in fact, in possession of all the rights relating to the earlier sign through the national marks of which it is the proprietor, including the reputation of the earlier sign (§ 38).

results.summary: The Board had to decide whether the EUTM proprietor had been acting in bad faith at the time of filing the contested application. The Board finds that this is not the case. In sum, it is not apparent that the EUTM proprietor filed its application for registration not with the aim of engaging fairly in competition, but with the intention of undermining the cancellation applicant’s interests, in a manner inconsistent with honest practices, or with the intention of obtaining an exclusive right for purposes other than those falling within the functions of a trade mark, in particular the essential function of indicating origin. In fact, the contrary is true.

According to the cancellation applicant, the EUTM proprietor acted in bad faith at the time of the EUTM’s application, namely, 1 April 1996, because it knowingly filed part of the goods in Class 30 (inedible decorations for cakes and pastries) in the wrong class. However, the cancellation applicant has failed to prove that the EUTM proprietor had a subjective motivation, dishonest intention or any other sinister motive at the time of filing the contested EUTM that would depart from accepted principles of ethical behaviour or honest commercial and business practices. Moreover, the EUTM proprietor has sufficiently demonstrated that the filing of the EUTM followed a commercial logic. The presumption of good faith of the contested EUTM is therefore maintained.

The fact that Alpak B.V. sought to register a trade mark that was virtually identical to certain of the earlier marks of the cancellation applicant and covering identical or similar goods and services does not make any sense from a business perspective at all for the reasons indicated above.  Alpak B.V. does not rely on, or even make an attempt to start any line of argument, there being any commercial logic whatsoever to justify its course of action. Instead, it merely and repeatedly, despite all the extensive and convincing arguments of the cancellation applicant to the contrary, puts forward, in essence, a number of simple arguments to the effect that it was unaware of the existence of the earlier trade marks belonging to the cancellation applicant when it filed the application for registration of the contested mark. The combination of all the elements discussed above, including the registered activity of Alpak B.V. and its connection to the Turkish company Alkapida Teknoloji Pazarlama Elektronik Limited Şirketi together with the Turkish residence of their common director, the way the sign ‘hepsiburada’ was used on the website www.hepsiburada.life and the impression Alpak B.V. sought to give that it operates the European sales of Hepsiburada without doubt being aware of the well-known and even reputed character of that trade mark in Turkey as well as of use of it in the European Union, the Netherlands included, and the filing of at least one other mark in similar circumstances, is sufficient to establish, with the requisite degree of certainty, that Alpak B.V. acted in bad faith at the time of filing of the contested mark.

results.ipRight: EUTM on which IA is based
results.summary: The cancellation applicant did not provide any proof that could objectively confirm the fraudulous intention of the EUTM proprietor, but relies mainly on future probabilities and personal suggestions.

Bad faith can be characterised by, inter alia, the intention of obtaining an exclusive right for purposes other than those falling within the functions of a trade mark, in particular the essential function of indicating origin. Where it is apparent from the chronology of events that the filings of the contested EUTMs were a key part of a coordinated strategy, between their applicant and the proprietor of earlier national trade marks, to remove those of some value from the proprietor’s assets before applying for declaration of insolvency, while ensuring that these trade marks were registered by means of equivalent EUTMs covering the same goods, the contested EUTMs have been filed with the aim of undermining the interests of third parties and not with the aim of supplying the goods concerned to the European market in a climate of fair competition (§ 49-52). The intention of the proprietor of the EUTMs is dishonest where its aim was to ‘hollow out’ the earlier national trade mark rights belonging to a company under special administration, prior to any claim by its creditors, while ensuring, by means of equivalent EU trade marks, that they were protected. As that dishonest scheme is incompatible with honest practices and does not show that the applicant had the intention of engaging fairly in competition, it constitutes bad faith (§ 78). The argument as regards the alleged bad faith on the part of the applicants for a declaration of invalidity is ineffective. Even if bad faith on the part of the applicants for a declaration of invalidity were proved, that fact alone could not call into question the finding that the applicant of the EUTMs was acting in bad faith when it filed its applications for registration, as the assessment of whether there has been bad faith on the part of the applicants for a declaration of invalidity, as alleged, is totally independent of the bad faith on the part of the EUTM applicant (§ 74).

results.earlierName: KAMALEI IL MARCHIO D IMPRESA, DI TIPO FIGURATIVO, E COSTITUITO DALLA DICITURA KAMALEI IN CARATTERI MINUSCOLI AD ECCEZIONE DELLA LETTERA

results.summary: The EUTM proprietor is the owner of the earlier EUTM No 8 293 755 for the word mark WONG LO KAT identical with the contested EUTM and for identical goods. The cancellation applicant claims that the filing of the contested mark which occurred six days before the expiry fo the grace period of the earlier mark was made in bad faith in order to circumvent the obligation of use of the earlier mark. The cancellation applicant has also taken parallel revocation proceedings against the earlier EUTM of the EUTM proprietor for non-use. In the context of the overall analysis undertaken pursuant to Article 59(1)(b) EUTMR, account may also be taken of the origin of the contested sign and its use since its creation, the commercial logic underlying the filing of the application for registration of that sign as an EU trade mark, and the chronology of events leading up to that filing. Although repeat filings of a mark are not prohibited per se, the fact remains that such a filing which is carried out in order to avoid the consequences entailed by non-use of earlier marks may constitute a relevant factor which is capable of establishing bad faith on the part of the person who filed that mark. Although the onus of evidence of bad faith leans on the Cancellation applicant, the General Court explained that the EUTM proprietor is best placed to provide the Board with information on his intentions at the time of applying for registration of that mark and in order to provide it with evidence capable of convincing it that, in spite of the existence of objective circumstances, those intentions were legitimate. in view of all the circumstances of the case, the Board cannot see any reasons in the re-filing other than the dishonest intention to perpetuate the monopoly on the mark by circumventing the obligation of use of the earlier mark thus obstructing competition by limiting the range of signs which can be registered as trade marks by others and by denying competitors the opportunity to use that trade mark or a similar one when putting onto the internal market goods or services which are identical or similar to those covered by the mark in question. Besides, the EUTM proprietor was given ample opportunities to explain the commercial logic of the filing of the contested EUTM but remained silent.
There is no bad faith.
There is no bad faith.

All the objective elements on file serve to establish that the EUTM proprietor in registering the contested EUTM acted in a manner inconsistent with honest practices and with the interests of the cancellation applicant.

results.ipRight: EUTM on which IA is based
results.summary: All the objective elements on file serve to establish that the EUTM proprietor in registering the contested EUTM acted in a manner inconsistent with honest practices and with the interests of the cancellation applicant.

The existence of bad faith must be assessed globally in the light of all the relevant factors specific to the case at the time of the application for the contested mark. In that regard, the global analysis of those factors leads to the conclusion, essentially, that the identity between the contested mark and the earlier unregistered mark clearly cannot be the result of chance, and that the original proprietor of the EUM intended, by applying for it, in reality to exploit in a parasitic manner the goodwill of the applicant for the declaration of invalidity or of its registered marks, and to profit from it.

results.entityName: QWAN KI DO QUÁN KHÍ DAO

results.quotation: (25/10/2022, R 1246/2021‑5, DEVICE OF A BANKSY'S MONKEY (fig.))
results.summary:
The cancellation applicant has not shown that the contested mark would be descriptive or non-distinctive. The mere statement that the sign would be perceived as decoration without any capacity to indicate the origin of the goods is not sufficient.
As to the bad faith, the cancellation applicant did neither show that the EUTM proprietor acted in bad faith at the filing date. None of the arguments submitted by him would justify any bad faith, as in particular the fact that the previous copyright protection of the sign would be difficult to impose and therefore the contested mark has been filed, or that the EUTM proprietor is against copyright protection, or that it has not yet used the contested mark having regard that the contested mark is still in the grace period. Consequently, the cancellation applicant has not shown that the EUTM proprietor had no intention to use the mark or filed the contested mark circumventing provisions of copyright law. The cancellation request had to be rejected.

Dishonest intention of the EUTM proprietor when filing the contested EUTM has been duly demonstrated and established. As follows from the special circumstances of the case, the EUTM proprietor’s actions constitute a strong indication of a speculative registration, the ultimate aim of which was merely to impede the operation of the competing association of lawyers and possibly obtain financial compensation. The EUTM proprietor failed to show any proper, economically viable use of the contested EUTM or intention to use it, or, in fact, any business activity in connection with the sign. Consequently, the contested decision is annulled and the EUTM declared invalid in its entirety.

The concept of bad faith relates to a subjective motivation on the part of the trade mark applicant, namely a dishonest intention or other sinister motive. It involves conduct which departs from accepted principles of ethical behaviour or honest commercial and business practices (§ 29). 
There is bad faith: if it is apparent from the overall analysis of the case that the applicant knew about the invalidity applicant’s business activities and virtually identical sign used by the invalidity applicant, on the day on which the contested mark was filed; if the applicant’s intention at the time of filing the application for registration of the contested mark is clear from the content of its sales offers and if from those offers it is possible to identify aspects of undue pressure, in particular due to the unsolicited and repeated nature of those offers, the high prices charged, the unexplained increase in the sales price upon the involvement of a new investor in the invalidity applicant’s activities, the reference to short periods of validity of the offers and items already on sale experiencing success; if the evidence of a commercial activity consistent with honest commercial logic has not been adduced (§ 81). 
The use of the same stylisation of the mark applied for and invalidity applicant’s mark cannot be attributed to chance, when the applicant has acknowledged that the sign constituting the contested mark was inspired by the sign used by the invalidity applicant in its business activities (§ 82). 
From the particular circumstances of this case, it is clear that by applying for registration of the contested trade mark, the commercial logic of the applicant seeking that registration was to ‘free-ride’ on the invalidity applicant’s sign’s reputation and take advantage of thereof, in particular by selling the contested mark to the invalidity applicant for a significant amount (§ 83).

The concept of bad faith relates to a subjective motivation on the part of the trade mark applicant, namely a dishonest intention or other sinister motive. It involves conduct which departs from accepted principles of ethical behaviour or honest commercial and business practices (§ 29). 
There is bad faith: if it is apparent from the overall analysis of the case that the applicant knew about the invalidity applicant’s business activities and virtually identical sign used by the invalidity applicant, on the day on which the contested mark was filed; if the applicant’s intention at the time of filing the application for registration of the contested mark is clear from the content of its sales offers and if from those offers it is possible to identify aspects of undue pressure, in particular due to the unsolicited and repeated nature of those offers, the high prices charged, the unexplained increase in the sales price upon the involvement of a new investor in the invalidity applicant’s activities, the reference to short periods of validity of the offers and items already on sale experiencing success; if the evidence of a commercial activity consistent with honest commercial logic has not been adduced (§ 81). 
The use of the same stylisation of the mark applied for and invalidity applicant’s mark cannot be attributed to chance, when the applicant has acknowledged that the sign constituting the contested mark was inspired by the sign used by the invalidity applicant in its business activities (§ 82). 
From the particular circumstances of this case it is clear that by applying for registration of the contested trade mark, the commercial logic of the applicant seeking that registration was to ‘free-ride’ on the invalidity applicant’s sign’s reputation and take advantage of thereof, in particular by selling the contested mark to the invalidity applicant for a significant amount (§ 83).

It is settled case-law that there is a degree of overlap between the respective scopes of the absolute grounds for refusal set out in Article 7(1)(b) to (d) EUTMR. There is also a degree of overlap between the respective scopes of Article 7(1)(b) and (g) EUTMR since a trade mark is incapable of fulfilling its essential function of guaranteeing the identity of origin of the goods or services covered where the information it contains is of such a nature as to deceive the public (§ 58, 59). 
However, it is clear from equally settled case-law that each of the grounds for refusal to register listed in Article 7(1) EUTMR is independent of the others and requires separate examination (§ 60). To deduce that a trade mark lacks distinctive character under Article 7(1)(b) EUTMR solely from the fact that it is descriptive under Article 7(1)(c) EUTMR constitutes an error in law (§ 62). The overlap between the absolute grounds for refusal set out in Article 7(1) EUTMR cannot compensate for the total lack of any arguments in relation to one of the grounds for refusal on the part of the applicant for a declaration of invalidity (§ 63). In the absence of any arguments, in the application for a declaration of invalidity, seeking to show that Article 7(1)(b) EUTMR applies, the BoA infringed Article 63(2) EUTMR, because, even though the application contained no reasoning in that regard, the adjudicating bodies of EUIPO nevertheless relied on that ground. In that respect, merely selecting that ground from the drop-down menu on the form is not sufficient (§ 68-69).

results.entityName: Le Clos Château Isenbourg Les Tourelles

results.summary: Taking into account the previous clarifications (points A - C), the considerations reiterated by the proprietor, throughout its appeal, are devoid of foundation or in any case of legal relevance, and are not such as to cast doubt on the conclusion of the contested decision, that, given the circumstances of the case, it acted in bad faith, by filing the contested semi-figurative mark, for "knives, cutlery" in class 8. The elements of evidence filed by the EUTM owner, are, for the most part, irrelevant, or rather they support the thesis of the invalidity applicant, that the EUTM owiner acted in bad faith, in order to prohibit the Municipality and its cutleries from using a sign necessary to their activities and to misappropriate the name of the Municipality and its repute for knives.

results.ipRight: EUTM on which IA is based

results.summary: In a global assessment of the facts of the case and their chronological development over time, along with the furnished documents it follows that the registration of the EUTM was done in bad faith, within the meaning of Article 59(1)(b) EUTMR.

The objective circumstances of the case indicate that the EUTM proprietor’s only aim was to free ride on the reputation in the invalidity applicant’s INKAS name and logo identical to the contested EUTM, registered in third countries and used worldwide over the course of a number of years prior to the filing of the contested EUTM. The EUTM proprietor merely puts forward, in essence, a number of unsubstantiated arguments to the effect that he was unaware of the existence of the earlier marks, that he planned to expand to the EU and that he was legally authorised to conduct business in the United Arab Emirates when he filed his application for registration of the contested EUTM. From his earlier business relationships with the invalidity applicant, the EUTM proprietor knew from the outset of the invalidity applicant’s presence and interest in the worldwide market for armoured vehicles.

results.entityName: YASMINA THÉ JASMIN JASMINTEE JASMIJN THEE TÉ JAZMÍN
YASMINA THÉ JASMIN JASMINTEE JASMIJN THEE TÉ JAZMÍN

results.summary: misuse of trademark system

The GC confirms 07/07/2016, T‑82/14, LUCEO, EU:T:2016:396, according to which the concept of bad faith involves conduct which departs from accepted principles of ethical behaviour or honest commercial and business practices. In order to assess whether an applicant is acting in bad faith, it is necessary inter alia to examine whether he intends to use the mark applied for. The intention to prevent the marketing of a product may, in certain circumstances, be an element of bad faith on the part of the applicant. That is in particular the case when it becomes apparent, subsequently, that the latter applied for registration of a EUTM without intending to use it, solely with a view to preventing a third party from entering the market (§ 25-27).
By filing successive applications for registration of national trade marks before the expiry of the six-month period provided for by Article 34(1) EUTMR, which were held to be withdrawn for non-payment of the application fees, the applicant artificially extended the six-month period during which it could claim the priority of the contested mark. Such conduct was part of an abusive filing strategy and cannot be regarded as legitimate business activity. It is contrary to the objectives of the EUTMR (§ 35-37).

The objective circumstances of the present case point to the presence of bad faith on the part of the proprietor of the EUM in applying for the contested mark. That is principally because there is a similarity between the signs, an identity or similarity between the goods, and because it has been established that the proprietor of the EUM was necessarily aware of the applicant's trade mark 'MASGLO' as a result of its status as a trader of its goods. Furthermore, it has not been demonstrated that the proprietor of the EUM obtained the express consent of the applicant for cancellation to the application for the contested mark. The alleged legitimate aim of the proprietor of the EUM is rejected as unfounded.

results.quotation: (23/08/2022, R 0124/2022‑4, GEOGRAPHICAL NORWAY EXPEDITION (fig.) / NAPAPIJRI geographic (fig.) et al.)
results.nickName: GEOGRAPHICAL NORWAY EXPEDITION (fig.) / NAPAPIJRI geographic (fig.) et al.
results.summary: The cancellation applicant's trade mark, 'GEOGRAPHIC', is used in combination with the word 'NAPAPIJRI' on the one hand and, on the other, with the Norwegian flag but it is never used alone, and has no dominant character, which makes it inseparable from the sign "NAPAPIJRI", to which it refers. The circumstances of the case do not indicate that the proprietor of the international mark designating the EU filed the application for registration of the contested mark with the intention of infringing, in a manner contrary to honest practice, the interests of the cancellation applicant, or with the intention of obtaining an exclusive right for purposes other than those relating to the functions of a mark.

results.entityName: @ Aytac Foods Quality Plain Flour Ekstra UN
results.nickName: @ aytac FOODS Quality Plain Flour Ekstra UN (fig.)
results.summary: Request of a transfer of the contested trade mark because of an agent mark unfounded. No fiduciary relationship between cancellation applicant and EUTM proprietor established. Article 8(4) not substantiated as to relevant national law and use of more than mere local significance of earlier rights invoked. Article 60(2)(c) EUTMR fails as no ownership of earlier copyrights proven. No bad faith proven.

Advertising agencies or trade mark designers may have an intent to use. In the present case, the applicant was not able to able to prove any commercially relevant use or exploitation of its trade mark rights to date. Consequently, the applicant’s conduct differs from the honest conduct of an advertising agency or a trade mark designer, and the trade mark must be declared invlaid in accordance with Article 59(1)(b) EUTMR. This is also confirmed by an earlier judgment of the general Court (07/07/2016, T-82/14, Luceo, EU:T:2016:396) and a judgment of the Frankfurt Higher Regional Court, Germany and the Supreme Court, Austria.

results.outcome: Cancellation totally upheld and EUTM/IR declared partially invalid

results.quotation: (13/07/2022, T‑287/21, Salatina / Salatina (fig.) et al., EU:T:2022:441)
results.summary:
Where the contested mark is similar to earlier pledge marks, the potential effect which the registration of the contested mark might have on the value of the earlier marks and on their attractiveness to potential purchasers of the pledge marks may be an indication of dishonest intentions (§ 63). From the point of view of a potential purchaser of the earlier pledged marks, the contested mark, which is similar to the earlier marks, might be perceived as being capable of exploiting their distinctive character or their reputation. The value of the pledged marks might thus be reduced by the mere fact of there being another mark in existence which does not form part of the group of pledged marks, but which is similar to those marks. Moreover, the existence of the contested mark risks jeopardising the essential function of the earlier marks, which may have an effect on their value and the intention and readiness of a potential purchaser to purchase them (§ 50-53).
The links between all the parties involved, in particular their contractual relationships and their potential economic interdependence, are relevant when assessing the context in which the contested mark was filed (§ 69).
Even if filing of the contested mark were part of a normal business practice, this would not be sufficient to rule out the existence of bad faith without an assessment of all the relevant factors specific to the particular case (§ 76).

Extending the protection of a sign by having it registered as an EU trade mark is part of the normal business strategy of an undertaking. In that regard, the applicant’s argument that the commercial logic underlying the filing of the application for registration is undermined by the fact that the name Gugler was not used as a mark before the date of filing is irrelevant. There is no obligation to use a mark before filing it (§ 44, 45). 
The fact of informing or not informing the other party of the application for registration of the contested mark is not capable of altering the EUTM applicant’s intention when it filed that application and of calling into question the fact that commercial logic underlay that filing and that it was not carried out in bad faith (§ 40).
The applicant does not put forward any argument capable of establishing that the application for registration of the contested mark did not have any underlying commercial logic for Gugler GmbH or that that application had been made with the intention of obtaining an exclusive right for purposes other than falling within the functions of a trade mark (§ 48).

The bringing of infringement proceedings by the proprietor of a trade mark cannot constitute an act of bad faith or ‘bad use’ of the contested mark. Furthermore, the invalidity applicant cannot maintain that infringement actions brought six years after the date on which the application for registration of the contested EUTM was filed would be capable of establishing its bad faith at the time the application was filed (§ 70, 71).

results.earlierName: Pappa Rotí One Bite, Different Expressions

results.summary: The Board, on the basis of the evidence concludes that the EUTM proprietor applied for registration of the EUTM, which still has not been used to date, without intending to use it, her sole objective being to prevent the invalidity applicant from entering the EU market and to continue to use the trade mark  in Romania (see cease and desist letters and criminal complaint filed by the EUTM Proprietor)

When the contested mark was filed, the earlier trade mark and the name of its owner (the same as the trade mark) were forgotten by the relevant public, and the applicant himself devoted considerable effort, time and money to revive the mark and to make known the history of its owner and of his business. Far from merely having exploited in a parasitic way the past reputation of the former trade mark and the name of its owner, the applicant made his own commercial efforts in order to revive the image of the former trade mark and thus, at his own expense, to restore that reputation. In those circumstances, the mere fact of having referred, for the purposes of promoting the contested mark, to the historic image of the owner of the former trade mark and to the former trade mark as such does not appear to be contrary to honest practices in industrial or commercial matters (§ 66).

It cannot be ruled out that, in certain specific circumstances, reuse by a third party of a previously renowned former mark or of the name of a previously famous person may give a false impression of continuity or of inheritance with that former mark or with that person. That could be the case, in particular, where the trade mark applicant presents itself to the relevant public as the legal or economic successor of the holder of the former mark, whereas there is no continuity or inheritance relationship between the holder of the former mark and the trade mark applicant. Such a circumstance could be taken into account in order to establish, where appropriate, bad faith on the part of the trade mark applicant (§ 68). The applicant did not claimed a family tie with the owner of the former trade mark nor he presented himself as his heir and the legal successor or of his business. Moreover, by stating that he had revived and resurrected a mark that flourished in the 1930s, the applicant suggested rather an interruption and, therefore, a lack of continuity between the previous activity and his own. Therefore, it does not appear that the applicant deliberately sought to establish a false impression of continuity or inheritance between his undertaking and that of the owner of the former reputed trade mark (§ 70).

The circumstances of the case do not indicate that the proprietor of the international mark designating the EU filed the application for registration of the contested mark with the intention of infringing, in a manner contrary to honest practice, the interests of the cancellation applicant, or with the intention of obtaining an exclusive right for purposes other than those relating to the functions of a mark.

results.quotation: (30/06/2022, R 0937/2021‑4, SpaClubMatahari / salon matahari (fig.) et al.)
keywords: Sign used in the course of trade
results.summary:
1. Since the existence of a non-registered well-known earlier right 1 within the meaning of Article 8(2)(c) EUTMR has not been established, the ground for invalidity based on Article 60(1)(a) EUTMR in conjunction with Article 8(1)(a) and (b) EUTMR must be rejected.
2. With regard to the earlier rights 2 and 3 used in the UK, the invalidity application has become unfounded as a consequence of the withdrawal of the UK from the European Union.
3. The cancellation applicant failed to provide  the content of the national law in relation to earlier rights 2 and 3 in the Czech Republic, Germany, the Netherlands, Sweden, Austria, Slovakia, Hungary and Poland as well as in relation to earlier right 4 (Czech domain name), contrary to the requirements set out in Article 8(4) EUTMR in conjunction with Articles 16(1)(b) and Article 7(2)(d) and (4) EUTMDR. 
4. The facts and evidence submitted by the cancellation applicant are not conclusive for demonstrating that the contested mark was filed in bad faith. 
5. The appeal is dismissed in its entirety.

Articles 52 to 54 CTMR [now Articles 59 to 61 EUTMR] govern the grounds for invalidity of an EUTM, whereas a separate provision, Article 51 CTMR [now Article 59 EUTMR], concerns grounds for revocation. The misleading nature of a mark constitutes an absolute ground for invalidity of that mark under the provisions of Article 52(1)(a) CTMR, in conjunction with Article 7(1)(g) CTMR, and, in addition, a ground for revocation under Article 51(1)(c) CTMR. Article 51(1)(c) CTMR provides expressly that the misleading nature of a registered trade mark, which justifies a declaration that the rights of its proprietor are revoked, arises from the use made of that mark, whereas the provisions of Article 52(1)(a) CTMR, in conjunction with Article 7(1)(g) CTMR, which render invalid a mark that has been registered in spite of its deceptive nature, do not contain any reference to such use (§ 63-65).
In principle, the examination of an application for revocation within the meaning of Article 51(1)(c) CTMR requires that account be taken of the actual use of the mark and thus of evidence subsequent to its filing, whereas that is not the case for the purpose of examining an application for a declaration of invalidity brought under the provisions of Article 52(1)(a) CTMR, in conjunction with Article 7(1)(g) CTMR. The examination of such an application for a declaration of invalidity requires that it be established that the sign filed for the purposes of registration as a mark was per se of such a nature as to deceive the consumer at the time of filing of the application for registration, since the subsequent management of that sign is irrelevant (§ 66).
The only date relevant for the purposes of the assessment of an application for a declaration of invalidity is the date of filing of the application for the mark at issue and material subsequent to the date of filing of the trade mark application may be taken into account only if it relates to the situation on that date. In the case of invalidity, the question which arises is whether the mark should not have been registered ab initio for reasons already existing on the date of the trade mark application, since the consideration of subsequent evidence can serve only to clarify the circumstances as they were on that date (§ 67, 68).
For the purpose of applying Article 7(1)(g) CTMR, the BoA should ascertain whether, on the date of the application for registration of the mark, there was any inconsistency between the information which the contested mark conveyed and the characteristics of the goods designated in that application. Since the list of goods did not contain any indication of their geographical origin and could therefore cover goods originating from Ireland, it was not possible to find that the contested mark was deceptive on the date of the application (§ 71). 
The insertion of a geographical origin limitation into the list of goods is not required by Article 7(1)(g) CTMR. The BoA erred in criticising the applicant for not having limited the list of goods to goods originating from Ireland (§ 72). 
Once the applicant had extended the use of the mark LA IRLANDESA to goods other than butter of Irish origin, Spanish-speaking consumers, who constitute the relevant public, were likely to be misled as to the geographical origin of those goods, since they had become accustomed over the course of several decades to the contested mark being affixed to butter originating from Ireland. Such conduct is evidence of bad faith inasmuch as it shows that, when filing the application for the contested mark, the applicant intended unfairly to transfer the advantage derived from the association with Ireland to goods not having that geographical origin, in particular after the end of its business relationship with the intervener which supplied it with Irish butter (§ 98).
Judgments and decisions of the Spanish judicial and administrative authorities confirm that the contested mark could be perceived by the relevant public as indicating that the goods to which that mark was affixed were of Irish origin. They also indicate that the use of the contested mark for goods not of Irish origin was controversial as regards its potential deceptive nature; a fact of which the applicant was necessarily aware on the date of the application for registration of that mark and which is therefore capable of supporting the existence of bad faith on the part of the applicant on that date (§ 100).
The Grand Board of Appeal was entitled to rely on the evidence submitted to it, which makes it possible in particular to establish a chronology of events characterising the filing of the application for registration of the contested mark, in order to find that the applicant had adopted a commercial strategy of association with the marks containing the element ‘LA IRLANDESA’ which were linked to the applicant’s former business relationship with the intervener, in order to continue to take advantage of that relationship, which has been terminated, and the marks which were linked to it (§ 101).

Where the operative part of a decision is based on several pillars of reasoning, each of which would in itself be sufficient to justify that operative part, that decision should, in principle, be annulled only if each of those pillars is vitiated by an illegality. In such a case, an error or other illegality which affects only one of the pillars of reasoning cannot be sufficient to justify annulment of the decision at issue because that error could not have had a decisive effect on the operative part adopted by the decision maker (§ 21). 
The fact that the first plea is well founded has no bearing on the legality of the contested decision, since the second plea directed against the second pillar of that decision is not, for its part, well founded and that second pillar is such as to justify, by itself, the operative part of that decision. The action must therefore be dismissed (§ 108, 109).

results.summary: Contrary to the findings of the contested decision, the cancellation applicants did not prove that the EUTM proprietor was acting in bad faith when he filed the contested EUTM. There is a presumption of good faith. The EUTM proprietor claimed that the cancellation applicants were acting as the EUTM proprietor's agents and representatives and the cancellation applicants were not able to convincingly prove the opposite. Moreover, there is evidence on file which shows that one of the cancellation applicants was paid a salary by the EUTM proprietor, along with other evidence which supports the EUTM proprietor's version of events. Even if the cancellation applicants played a significant role in the creation of the contested sign, this would have been done in their capacity of agents and representatives of the EUTM proprietor.

The EUTM Proprietor refiled an identical mark for services that were identical to the services already covered by its earlier registration.  The chronology of events  indicate that the EUTM proprietor’s filing strategy was used with the intention to circumvent the obligation to prove genuine use of the mark.  It is clear that the intention of the EUTM proprietor was indeed to take advantage of the EU trade mark rules by artificially creating the situation where it would not have to prove genuine use of its earlier mark for the services at stake.  Therefore the application insofar as it included the services already covered by its earlier registrations was made in bad faith.

results.summary: The appellant neither proved that the EUTM deceived consumers on the application date nor that there was a sufficiently serious risk that the consumer would be deceived. A serious risk presupposes more than a pure allegation, it needs evidence that the EUTM proprietor had already used the EUTM on the application date in a deceptive way or any other similar behaviour. By the same token, it did not submit any evidence that the proprietor acted in bad faith. The Board also fails to understand how the figurative element could be considered as a "heralidc" imitation of the Swiss coat of arms.

results.summary: The Cancellation Division correctly concluded that the contested trade mark application does not constitute a repeat application made in bad faith.
