Two limbs of bad faith claim: the EUTM proprietor had no intention to use the mark for the goods applied for, and repeat filings to circumvent proof of use. There are no objective, relevant and consistent indicia tending to show that contested EUTM  was filed with the intention of circumventing the provisions on proof of use, or with the intention of undermining, in a manner inconsistent with honest practices, with the intention of obtaining an exclusive right with no intention to use the contested EUTM for the goods registered. The Monopoly case is distinguished.

keywords: Used in the course of trade
results.summary:
The relevant public, faced with the composite sign ‘NAPAPIJRI GEOGRAPHIC’, will not perceive the non-registered sign ‘geographic’ as an independent sign under Article 8(4) CTMR [now EUTMR] (§ 26).

The non-registered sign ‘geographic’ has no actual and real independent presence on the relevant market. Only together with the sign ‘napapijri’, which is dominant, and with the Norwegian flag does it have such presence. It has not been proved that a substantial part of the relevant public would know the non-registered sign ‘geographic’ and would associate it with the goods without any effort. Therefore, the BoA was fully entitled to find that that word did not constitute an independent sign used in the course of trade (§ 38).

results.summary:
Nature of the market ― Period of use of identical or similar mark ― Chronology of events constitute relevant factors for assessing the presumption of knowledge ― The absence of use of the contested mark may be relevant when assessing the existence of dishonest intention ― Request for investigative measures

Given the relatively limited nature of the market for the sale of automobile tyres in Bulgaria and the common origin in China of the tyres, the Board of Appeal (BoA) was entitled to presume that, at the time of filing the application for registration, the applicant had knowledge of the activities of its direct competitor in Bulgaria and, in particular the marketing of tyres made in China under a mark highly similar to the contested mark (§ 45). The evidence provided by the intervener shows only use of the Chinese mark ‘Agate’ in Bulgaria between 2014 and 2017, that is to say during a period which may appear relatively short. However, the duration of use of a sign is only one of the considerations which may be taken into account to presume knowledge of it for the purposes of assessing bad faith (§ 46). Knowledge can also be established considering the chronology of the events, namely the short period between the filing of the contested mark and its registration on 7 March and 21 June 2017 respectively on the one hand, and the application for coercive measures lodged by the applicant with the Bulgarian customs authorities against the intervener and its distributor on 5 July 2017 and the infringement action filed on 6 July 2017 before the Bulgarian Patent Office against that distributor, on the other (§ 61, 63).

In the context of the assessment of bad faith, it is not the use of the contested mark that is examined, but rather whether the proprietor, at the time of filing the application for registration, intended to make use of the mark (§ 68). The absence of such an intention may be inferred from the complete absence of evidence relating to the commercial activities of the proprietor of the contested mark. Such evidence may be relevant in assessing the commercial logic underlying the filing of the application for registration of the contested mark (§ 69). Considering the circumstances of the case ‘there was no commercial logic underlying the application for registration of the contested mark’; the applicant’s only activity related to that mark was that of ‘hindering others’. Therefore, the applicant ‘pursued dishonest purposes’ (§ 72-74).

A party may rely before the Court on the existence of evidence that may question the accuracy of the content or probative value of the evidence considered by the Office by requesting investigative measures for that evidence to be produced, for the first time, before the Court. However, in such a case, the party requesting such measures must explain in detail the reasons for considering that the evidence taken into account by the Office does not correspond, in its view, to reality, or the reasons for considering that the probative value of that evidence has not been established (§ 51). According to Article 97 EUTMR, parties to proceedings before the Office may request or propose investigative measures in order to establish facts relevant to the case. However, the applicable provisions do not impose an obligation on the Office to take such measures (§ 53).

results.quotation: (27/09/2021, R 1867/2018‑5, Zsolnay / ZSOLNAY 1853 (fig.) et al. I)
results.summary: The Board confirms the decision of the first instance that the contested decision was filed in bad faith. The decision includes a secion on belated evidence.

It is settled case-law that there is a degree of overlap between the respective scopes of the absolute grounds for refusal set out in Article 7(1)(b) to (d) EUTMR. There is also a degree of overlap between the respective scopes of Article 7(1)(b) and (g) EUTMR since a trade mark is incapable of fulfilling its essential function of guaranteeing the identity of origin of the goods or services covered where the information it contains is of such a nature as to deceive the public (§ 58, 59). 
However, it is clear from equally settled case-law that each of the grounds for refusal to register listed in Article 7(1) EUTMR is independent of the others and requires separate examination (§ 60). To deduce that a trade mark lacks distinctive character under Article 7(1)(b) EUTMR solely from the fact that it is descriptive under Article 7(1)(c) EUTMR constitutes an error in law (§ 62). The overlap between the absolute grounds for refusal set out in Article 7(1) EUTMR cannot compensate for the total lack of any arguments in relation to one of the grounds for refusal on the part of the applicant for a declaration of invalidity (§ 63). In the absence of any arguments, in the application for a declaration of invalidity, seeking to show that Article 7(1)(b) EUTMR applies, the BoA infringed Article 63(2) EUTMR, because, even though the application contained no reasoning in that regard, the adjudicating bodies of EUIPO nevertheless relied on that ground. In that respect, merely selecting that ground from the drop-down menu on the form is not sufficient (§ 68-69).

The relevant point in time for calculating the observance of time limits is the date on which the document was received and not the date on which it was sent.

The relationship between the parties prior to the filing of the contested EUTM and the fact that at the filing date the parties had two commercial agreements suffice to pretend that the proprietor, which did not inform the cancellation applicant about the filing, had a duty of correctness. results.ipRight: EUTM on which IA is based

results.outcome: EUTM partially assigned and cancelled for the remainder

results.summary: The Board agrees with the cancellation division that the registered EUTM was filed in bad faith.

The evidence shows the invalidity applicant’s use of the sign STILMETAL from 2013 that predates the date of filing of the EUTM.  The EUTM  is identical to the prior sign used .  The parties were  both manufacturers, wholesalers and retailers of  biomass boilers. It is not in dispute that the parties had a previous relationship.  The objective circumstances of the case point to the EUTM proprietor being aware of the use of invalidity applicant’s use of the identical sign for boilers at the filing date of the EUTM. The EUTM proprietor does not dispute that it has never used the sign STILMETAL. Indeed, the EUTM proprietor has only ever  has used  the sign BIODOM  for its own boilers. The objective circumstances of the present case indicate the EUTM proprietor’s  intention of preventing the invalidity applicant  from continuing to use the sign STILMETAL, which is confirmed by the subsequent seizure by Romanian customs of the invalidity applicant’s products and the cease and desist letters that were sent  to the distributor and clients of the invalidity  applicant invoking the rights to the EUTM.  The Cancellation Division rightly founds bad faith on the part of the EUTM proprietor in filing the EUTM

results.earlierName: M MAES INOX custom kitchens for passionate chefs

results.summary: The filing strategy of the predecessor in title is built on an abusive model, which has already been held once by the General Court in its judgment of 7 July 2016, T-82/14, Luceo, EU:T:2016:396, to be in bad faith within the meaning of Article 59(1)(b) EUTMR.

Bad faith: contractual relations, concealed act

In the present case, the mere fact that the proprietor of the contested mark has interpreted the provisions of an agreement concluded with the invalidity applicant in its own favour, does not constitute an indication of bad faith (§ 61).

Although the proprietor had not informed the invalidity applicant that it had filed the contested mark in advance, this was not a concealed act carried out to prevent the invalidity applicant from using the sign. The agreements between the parties had been terminated and were the subject of litigation long before the contested mark was filed. Indeed, the invalidity applicant knew that the proprietor of the contested mark was seeking to protect its interests by any available legal means (§ 73).

The arguments and evidence presented by the cancellation applicants certainly lead to the conclusion that the EUTM proprietor acted in bad faith when applying for the contested EUTM. Its attempts to explain its commercial logic in applying for the contested EUTM clash with both the proven facts and also contain inconsistencies and coincidences that further support that it acted in collusion with at least NERATAX in a bad faith scheme, and represented the continuation of NERATAX by way as the next link in the chain. The overall scheme, involving other Greek tms of KRENTIN and other EUTMs of NERATAX, was to remove assets of value from that company and thus to enable the persons controlling it to continue to enjoy the trade mark rights it had previously owned, but now registered as EUTMs. The fact that the cancellation applicants may no longer be creditors of KRENTIN is entirely irrelevant, given that no such interest is necessary in order to file an application for a declaration of invalidity of an EUTM. The timing of the application of the contested EUTM (the day NERATAX requested surrender of an identical EUTM but before recordal) shows  two were in league, and its accounts about applying for the EUTM on appeal show at first instance the explanation was untrue. Even if the contested EUTM also mirrors a different Greek trade mark of Berzeletos, bad faith is still shown.

The fact that the contested mark is composed of the elements ‘ELLO’ and ‘ERMOL’ the latter not being a trade which was owned and later relinquised by KRENTIN, but is still registered in the name of Mr D.T. Bergeletos, is irrelevant. It remains that  ‘ELLO’ forms a distinctive element and indeed the top part of the contested mark and  for a bad faith claim to succeed the identity of marks is not a requirement. As can be seen by the filing of the EUTM ‘ELLO Creamy’ on the same date, and EUTM ‘ELLO’ beforehand, the filing of the contested EUTM constitutes part of a strategy to take advantage of the earlier reputed Greek trade mark ‘ELLO’, as is the case vis-à-vis ‘MORFAT’ in the other cases.The arguments and evidence presented by the cancellation applicants certainly lead to the conclusion that the EUTM proprietor acted in bad faith when applying for the contested EUTM. results.quotation: (22/06/2021, R 1357/2020‑1, giordano / ladies (fig.) / Giordano et al.)
results.summary:
In order to assess the outcome of the invalidity request filed by the cancellation applicants under the provisions invoked, it is necessary to carry out an examination of the request under Article 60(1)(a) EUTMR in conjunction with Article 8(1)(b) EUTMR on the basis of the earlier Benelux national trade mark ‘GIORDANO’, now substantiated, and of the request under Article 60(1)(c) EUTMR in conjunction with Article 8(4) EUTMR, on the basis of the non-registered right ‘GIORDANO’ used in the course of trade of more than mere local significance in Ireland and Germany. Moreover, the Cancellation Division is required, if needed, to examine the claim under Article 59(1)(b) EUTMR, on the basis of the new evidence filed by the cancellation applicants and the EUTM proprietor before the Board. 
Therefore, the case has to be remitted to the Cancellation Division for further examination.

results.summary:
Earlier certification mark — Likelihood of confusion — Criteria for the assessment of likelihood of confusion — Scope of protection afforded by Article 8(1)(b) EUTMR in case of earlier certification marks

Where the earlier marks relied on in support of the application for a declaration of invalidity are national certification marks, the likelihood of confusion within the meaning of Article 8(1)(b) EUTMR must be understood as being the risk that the public might believe that the goods or services covered by the mark applied for and those covered by the earlier marks all originate from persons authorised by the proprietor of those earlier marks to use them in the context of the certification scheme of which they are part or, where appropriate, from undertakings economically linked to those persons or to that proprietor (§ 35).

In the event of an application for a declaration of invalidity by the proprietor of a certification mark, although the essential function of that type of mark must be taken into account in order to understand what is meant by likelihood of confusion, within the meaning of Article 8(1)(b) EUTMR, the fact remains that the case-law establishing the criteria with regard to which the existence of such a likelihood must be assessed in practice is applicable to cases concerning an earlier certification mark (§ 36).

The question whether the effective compliance by the proprietor of the contested mark with the characteristics guaranteed by the proprietor of the earlier certification marks forms part of the essential function of those marks is outside the scope of the protection afforded by Article 8(1)(b) EUTMR. That question concerns, at most, the use of a mark, insofar as it could harm the essential function of a certification mark and mislead the public regarding the certification of the characteristics of the goods (§ 69).


Dominant element — Distinctive element — Secondary role in the overall impression

If a trade mark has multiple dominant elements, some of which are distinctive and others of which are weak, the weak ones can be considered secondary in the overall impression of the sign (§ 81).
The Board annuls the contested decision. The mark was filed in bad faith. The decision includes a section on belated evidence.

The evidence submitted by the cancellation applicant (CA) is not able to support the contested mark’s alleged descriptiveness. Hence, it cannot be inferred from the evidence whether a non-negligible part of the relevant public, perceived the term Portixol as referring to a geographical name at the time of filing the contested mark. Neither it will be perceived as an indication of the origin of the services covered by the contested EUTM. In fact, no sufficient and specific relationship has been demonstrated by the CA as regards the contested EUTM with its verbal elements INMOBILIARIA PORTIXOL and the relevant services at the time of filing. Consequently, the evidence presented does not lead to the conclusion that this denomination or the contested mark as a whole was already perceived as being descriptive in relation to the relevant goods and services at the filing date of the contested mark. The CA’s submissions also do not provide sufficient indications that the contested EUTM as a whole and the relevant services would come to have such a relationship in the future. Art 7(1)(b) EUTMR does not apply as a consequence, nor there are any other reasons why the contested EUTM would fall foul of this provision. None of the criteria for the application of Art 59(1)(b) EUTMR have been met. Nevertheless, the registration of the contested EUTM does not prevent the CA from continuing the use of its sign in the territory at issue, in accordance with the relevant national provision.

results.quotation: (09/06/2021, T‑398/20, RIVIERA AIRPORT (fig.) / Riviera airport et al., EU:T:2021:327)
results.nickName: RIVIERA AIRPORT (fig.) / Riviera airport et al.
results.summary:
Bad faith: use of an earlier right not a necessary condition

The use of an earlier right by a third party on the internal market, at the time of application for registration of a mark, is not a necessary condition in order to rely on bad faith (12/09/2019, C 104/18P, STYLO & KOTON (fig.), EU:C:2019:724, §52) (§ 42).


Extent of invalidity

Where the ground for invalidity exists in respect of only some of the goods or services for which the contested mark is sought to be registered, the trade mark is to be declared invalid as regards those goods or services only (29/01/2020, C 371/18, SKY, EU:C:2020:45, § 80). Therefore, upholding the action on the basis of bad faith does not automatically mean that the mark is invalid in its entirety (§ 71).

results.summary: The silhouette of a pine tree has a pure decorative and evocative character and indicates to consumers one of the characteristics of the goods. results.ipRight: EUTM on which IA is based
results.summary:
Invalidity proceedings ― Bad faith ― Contractual relationships ― Interpretation of agreements ― Transfer of rights ― Chronology of events ― Unregistered earlier right ― Concealed act

The case concerns the bad faith underlying the application for the EUTM ‘TORNADO’ for boats by one of the parties to an informal partnership. The other party (the invalidity applicant) is the proprietor of a corresponding unregistered sign which he had used and popularised. The EUTM proprietor claimed that the invalidity applicant had transferred his rights in the sign ‘TORNADO’ to the EUTM proprietor when forming the partnership. The Board of Appeal annulled the EUTM registration and the General Court dismissed the appeal. 

Examining the parties’ correspondence, the General Court found that use of the sign by the partnership was subject to the payment of royalties to the invalidity applicant, who was therefore considered the proprietor of the rights in that sign. If a transfer of rights was envisaged, it was never formalised (§ 57-64, 68). Moreover, the invalidity applicant was still monitoring the activity of the partnership (§ 65). The chronology of events supported a finding of bad faith since the contested EUTM application was filed shortly before the cessation of the relationship between the parties (§ 69). The fact that the filing of the EUTM was concealed from the invalidity applicant further supports the conclusion that the application for the contested EUTM essentially aimed to ‘put up obstacles to the [invalidity applicant’s] activities by preventing him from using that mark which, over time, he had made popular in the inflatable boats sector’ (§ 71, 73). The fact that the invalidity applicant’s rights concerned an unregistered mark is irrelevant to the extent that bad faith does not require the invalidity applicant to be the proprietor of a registered earlier mark (§ 77). The fact use of this unregistered mark may have stopped after the establishment of the partnership is equally irrelevant: ‘Even assuming that the [invalidity applicant] no longer used that sign for the purposes of marketing inflatable boats with his own company, the fact remains that that sign remained his, as he had consented to the use thereof by the [contested EUTM proprietor], in exchange for royalties paid by the latter, during the period preceding the application for the trade mark’ (§ 79).

results.ipRight: EUTM on which IA is based
EUTM on which IA is based

results.summary:
Invalidity proceedings ― Bad faith ― Scope of cancellation

Knowledge on the part of an EUTM applicant of the prior use made of a similar sign, corresponding to a rare first name of Arabic origin, can be deduced from the reputation acquired by this sign in a third country in adjacent economic fields. Bad faith does not require use, by a third party, of an identical or similar sign within the EU.

The scope of the invalidity (for all goods and services or parts of them) may depend on whether the EUTM applicant’s intention was:

•	to harm a particular third party, in which case it is not possible to distinguish between the EUTM applicant’s motives and the registration should therefore be cancelled as a whole, or;

•	to misuse the registration system otherwise than by harming one specific operator, in which case the EUTM applicant’s intention may be partly legitimate and partly abusive, therefore justifying the conclusion that the ground for invalidity exists only in respect of some of the goods or services for which the EUTM has been registered.

Invalidity proceedings ― Bad faith ― Scope of cancellation

Knowledge on the part of an EUTM applicant of the prior use made of a similar sign, corresponding to a rare first name of Arabic origin, can be deduced from the reputation acquired by this sign in a third country in adjacent economic fields. Bad faith does not require use, by a third party, of an identical or similar sign within the EU.

The scope of the invalidity (for all goods and services or parts of them) may depend on whether the EUTM applicant’s intention was:

•	to harm a particular third party, in which case it is not possible to distinguish between the EUTM applicant’s motives and the registration should therefore be cancelled as a whole, or;

•	to misuse the registration system otherwise than by harming one specific operator, in which case the EUTM applicant’s intention may be partly legitimate and partly abusive, therefore justifying the conclusion that the ground for invalidity exists only in respect of some of the goods or services for which the EUTM has been registered.

The case involves a business relationship between the cancellation applicant (a Syrian company), Mr H., a Syrian merchant and the EUTM proprietor (a Romanian company). Apart from the registration of a hand gel product ‘TOUCH’ in its name by the Syrian Ministry of Health, the cancellation applicant has not produced any evidence relating to its own use of the mark in Syria, or elsewhere. The cancellation applicant relies on the use of the mark on the products ordered by the EUTM proprietor from Mr H. (for export purpose to Romania), who ordered them from the cancellation applicant. There was a commercial agreement between the EUTM proprietor and Mr H. only. The application for the EUTM follows a normal business logic, since the EUTM proprietor was using the mark ‘TOUCH’ in Romania well before filing the mark in the EU. Whereas the cancellation applicant was unable to provide any evidence whatsoever of its own use of its Syrian mark, the EUTM proprietor submitted before the Cancellation Division invoices corresponding to sales of TOUCH goods and additional evidence of use before the Board (commercial contracts). There is nothing in the file that shows that when the trade mark application was filed the EUTM proprietor’s intention was of preventing the cancellation applicant from using such a sign. It is therefore confirmed that bad faith has not been established.

The evidence of the file, taken as a whole, leads to conclude that the relation between the EUTMR proprietors and the cancellation applicant was that of a non-written agreement for the distribution of perfumes in Croatia and Slovenia. All the facts of the case must be considered. As a result, the filing of the contested EUTM was unauthorized act of the agent/representative of the cancellation applicant. Article 8(3) EUTMR must apply. However, it is applicable only for those goods and service which are identical/similar/comparable with the goods covered by the earlier marks registered in non-EU countries of the cancellation applicant.

keywords: Similarity of the goods and services
results.summary:
Earlier EU collective mark — Assessment of distinctiveness — Reliance on earlier case-law concerning individual or certification marks. 

It is for the proprietor of an earlier collective mark to show what level of distinctiveness it has beyond the minimum level, since he intends to rely on it in support of opposition proceedings or even cancellation proceedings (§ 50).

Finding of a weak degree of distinctiveness of the earlier mark does not amount to denying the very existence of the distinctive character of a validly registered trade mark, or to depriving that mark of the rights which it confers on its proprietor, but simply to finding that it cannot confer more rights than those it draws objectively from its distinctive character (§ 54).

Even supposing that the EU collective mark ‘HALLOUMI’ implicitly refers to the Cypriot geographical origin of the goods covered, that mark must still fulfil its essential distinctive function. The generic nature of the word ‘halloumi’, since that word alone constitutes the earlier mark, necessarily limits the effects of that mark in the light of that function (§ 57).

The need for more extensive protection on the basis of EU trade mark law does not exist in the present case, since the rules governing protected designations of origin and protected geographical indications, as laid down in Regulation (EU) No 1151/2012 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 21 November 2012 on quality schemes for agricultural products and foodstuffs (OJ 2012 L 343, p. 1), could provide sufficient protection outside the scope of trade mark law (§ 58).

The Board of Appeal cannot be criticised, when assessing the inherent distinctiveness of the earlier mark, for having referred to decisions of the Court which concerned earlier rights also composed of the single word ‘halloumi’, as individual marks or certification, in proceedings relating to applications for registration or opposition, since the assessment of the distinctiveness of the rights in question met criteria which could be perfectly transposed to the present case (§ 53).

Weak element — Likelihood of confusion

Where the elements of similarity between two signs arise from the fact that, as in the present case, they share a component that has a weak inherent distinctive character, the impact of such elements of similarity on the global assessment of the likelihood of confusion is itself low (§ 87).


Rights derived from a weakly-distinctive earlier mark

The rights which the applicant derives from the registration of the earlier mark cannot, in all circumstances, confer on it an exclusive right to use the word ‘halloumi’, when, moreover, the extent of its rights under that mark is determined by the distinctiveness of that mark, whether inherent or acquired through use, which is weak (§ 115).

Bad faith — Criterion of dishonesty

The Board of Appeal was entitled to rely on the criterion of dishonesty in order to assess the alleged bad faith of the proprietor of the contested mark (§ 117).

results.summary: the cancellation applicant informed the Office that the parties had reached an amicable settlement, whereby the Proprietor surrendered its EUTM registration No. 16 357 535 ‘mesotix’ in its entirety, and asked the Board of Appeal to close the appeal proceedings, which had become devoid of purpose.

The Board confirmed the findings of the Cancellation Division that the trade mark ‘Villa Borghetti’ had been filed in bad faith. The EUTM proprietor, previously exclusive licensee of the cancellation applicants, filed a trade mark very similar to the licensed non-registered one, almost at the end of their contractual relationship, without asking for the prior authorization of the cancellation applicants. The assessment took into account the chronological order of the facts and the underlying intention of the EUTM proprietor to exclude the cancellation applicants from the EU market. The fact that the proprietor had registered national marks in its name does not exclude bad faith.The declaration, submitted by the EUTM proprietor, released by the former vice-president of a company previously affiliated to the cancellation applicants, is contradictory and does not prove knowledge of the registration on the part of the cancellation applicants.

In light of all the facts of the case, it must be held that it can be inferred from the particular circumstances of the present case that the real purpose of the EUTM proprietor’s application for registration of a EUTM was to ‘free-ride’ on the reputation of the historic   marks and to take advantage of that reputation
The BoA confirms the decision of the first instance that the contested mark was not filed in bad faith.

results.quotation: (08/03/2021, R 2437/2019‑2, Richmond / John richmond et al.)
results.summary:
Cancellation action based on BAD FAITH upheld - Article 59(1)(b) EUTMR. 
The EUTM application was filed in 2014 by Moschillo Srl, co-owned by Mr M., a person who collaborated between 1995 and 2015 with Mr. Richmond (fashion designer), first owner of the TMs “Richmond” (first EUTMs filed in 1997). Mr M. could not ignore that the filing of the EUTM “Richmond” was made in a clear conflict of interest with the cancellation applicant’s trade marks (Fashioneast SARL, actual owner of the signs), thereby causing prejudice to that company. At the time of filing of the contested EUTM, a contractual and commercial relationship existed between Akkurate Ltd, owner of the sign at that time, Mr M and the companies under his control.
Mr M’s argument that he did so in light of the consent of Mr. R. and Akkurate is not supported by the evidence on file. 
Furthermore, a series of actions taken by Mr M. after the filing of the contested trade mark are indicative of his intention e.g. he organized fashion events under the “Richmond” sign declaring himself the owner: the applicant sent warning letters and filed an application for injunction before the Court of Milan that was granted but Mr M. continued to use the sign "Richmond". Some products sold by him under the sign “Richmond” were then seized by the Italian authorities.
The Board is called to assess whether, from the documents on file, it can be inferred that the contested EUTM was filed in bad faith. The answer is affirmative.

results.quotation: (11/01/2021, R 1692/2019‑2, milf meat i live for (fig.))
results.summary: BAD FAITH: There existed personal and business relationship between the parties, qualified by the cancellation applicant as employer-employee relations and by the EUTM proprietor as ‘silent partnership’. At the filing date of the contested mark, it is not clear to the Board that the relationship between the parties was a simple employer–employee relationship. Nothing in the file shows that when the trade mark application was filed the EUTM proprietor’s intention was of preventing the cancellation applicant from continuing to use such a sign. The disagreement occurred much later when the cancellation applicant served to the EUTM proprietor an Extrajudicial Declaration Complaint. Bad faith has not been established. ARTICLE 60(2)(a), (b) EUTMR: The cancellation applicant based its application on a right to a name and to a personal portrayal but failed to submit any information on the relevant national law and did not put forward any line of arguments in that regard. Before the Board, it referred for the first time to the protection of a ‘trade name’ and cited articles of the Greek trade mark law and Greek unfair competition law. The new pieces of evidence are not relevant for the outcome of the case and are not supplementary. They are inadmissible. The sign is not a personal portrayal and the cancellation applicant did not refer to any provision of the Greek law according to which a trade mark use could be prohibited based on a personal portrayal.

results.summary:
KEY POINTS: 
The potentially descriptive nature of a verbal element comprising a mark does not prevent the conclusion that the proprietor of that mark acted in bad faith when filing the application for the mark (13/11/2019, C 528/18 P, Outsource 2 India (fig.), EU:C:2019:961, § 69) (§ 28).

The EUTM proprietors were not linked to the applicant and bad faith on the part of the EUTM proprietors cannot be presumed based merely on their business relationship with an intermediary who knew of the existence of the mark EGYPTIAN EARTH (§ 35-36).

The fact that the EUTM proprietors are also proprietors of other marks using the same term in different language versions suggests that the registration of that mark is not artificial in nature or devoid of logic in commercial terms and had a legitimate aim (§ 39-41).

The addition of the distinctive term ‘bikor’ also suggests a lack of bad faith on the part of the EUTM proprietors when filing the application for the mark (§ 42-43).

FACTS:

Ms Elżbieta Korbut Bikor and Bikor Professional Color Cosmetics Małgorzata Wedekind (‘the EUTM proprietors’), registered the word mark BIKOR EGYPTIAN EARTH on 22/08/2012 as an EUTM for goods and services in Classes 3 and 41.

An invalidity application was filed on 23/12/2013 by Pareto Trading Co., Inc. (‘the applicant’), based on Article 52(1)(b) and Article 7(1)(b) and (e)(ii) CTMR on the ground that the EUTM proprietors had applied for the mark in bad faith since the applicant had registered the mark ‘EGYPTIAN EARTH’ in the United States and Canada and used it as a non-registered mark in Europe. The Cancellation Division upheld the invalidity application, considering that the EUTM proprietors had acted in bad faith when they filed the mark.

The EUTM proprietors filed an appeal before the Board of Appeal (BoA). The BoA upheld the appeal and dismissed the invalidity action. The BoA found that the applicant had not proved that the EUTM proprietors were acting in bad faith when they filed the contested mark.

The applicant filed an action before the General Court (GC), relying on a single plea in law: infringement of Article 52(1)(b) CTMR. The GC dismissed the action.

SUBSTANCE:

INFRINGEMENT OF ARTICLE 52(1)(b) CTMR

The potentially descriptive nature of a verbal element comprising a mark does not prevent the conclusion that the proprietor of that mark acted in bad faith when filing the application for the mark (13/11/2019, C 528/18 P, Outsource 2 India (fig.), EU:C:2019:961, § 69) (§ 28).

The applicant had entrusted the distribution of its products in Europe to an intermediary and had authorised that intermediary to use the mark ‘EGYPTIAN EARTH’ under certain conditions. That intermediary also had a business relationship with the EUTM proprietors, as they were clients of the intermediary (§ 32).

However, the EUTM proprietors were not linked to the applicant and bad faith on the part of the EUTM proprietors cannot be presumed based merely on their business relationship with an intermediary who knew of the existence of the mark ‘EGYPTIAN EARTH’ (§ 35-36). There is no evidence that the EUTM proprietors knew or should have known that the applicant was using the mark ‘EGYPTIAN EARTH’ for the marketing of goods similar to those covered by the contested mark when they filed the EUTM (§ 38).

The applicant also fails to demonstrate that the EUTM proprietors had the sole intention of preventing the applicant from using its mark and that they did not intend to use the contested mark. The fact that the EUTM proprietors are also proprietors of other marks using the same term in different language versions suggests that the registration of that mark is not artificial in nature or devoid of logic in commercial terms and had a legitimate aim (§ 39-41).

The addition of the distinctive term ‘bikor’ also suggests a lack of bad faith on the part of the EUTM proprietors when filing the application for the mark (§ 42-43).

keywords: Identity of the goods and services
results.summary: Identity of the goods and of the marks is a relevant factor. There is evidence of the longstanding use of the earlier mark in the sector of firearms and their accessories, which the EUTM proprietor must have known of at the date of filing . ‘first-to-file’ principle does not rule out the possibility for the proprietor of an unregistered trade mark to rely on the bad faith . Having regard to the risks and specificities of the tightly regulated market for weapons and firearms, , and the fact that EUTM proprietor does not profess to have any expertise in that market, but operates in the distinct field on online and mail order retail. those factors would indicate that the sole objective in applying for the EUTM, which is identical to the earlier sign, which has been used for many  years for identical goods, was to prevent the invalidity applicant from entering the market  without any intention of using the EUTM. The affidavits of the invalidity applicant attest to the fact that the  EUTM proprietor required EUR 150, 000 for the transfer of the EUTM. The circumstances suggest that she proposed that amount knowing  that was what the sign was worth from the longstanding use the sign Sabatti. The objective circumstances of the case indicate bad faith

KEY POINTS: 
To hold that bad faith involves conduct which departs from accepted principles of ethical behaviour or honest commercial and business practices, and presupposes a dishonest intention or other sinister motive, would be to interpret bad faith too restrictively. In fact, the intention of obtaining, without even targeting a specific third party, an exclusive right for purposes other than those falling within the functions of a trade mark, in particular the essential function of indicating origin, may be sufficient for finding bad faith by the trade mark applicant (12/09/2019, C-104/18 P, STYLO & KOTON (fig.), EU:C:2019:724, § 45-46; 29/01/2020, C-371/18, SKY, EU:C:2020:45, § 74-75) (§ 27). Accordingly, registration of the contested mark ‘TARGET VENTURES’ not with the intention to use it but with the sole purpose of strengthening the scope of protection of the proprietor’s actually used mark ‘TARGET PARTNERS’, was inconsistent with the essential function of a trade mark to indicate to consumers the commercial origin of goods and services (§ 25-27, 35-44). ). In this context, it was irrelevant whether or not the proprietor knew or ought to have known about someone else’s prior use of an identical sign, so lack of proof of that knowledge could not have been a reason for dismissing the bad faith claim (§ 28-30, 46).

FACTS:

Target Partners GmbH (‘the EUTM proprietor’), a venture capital fund, registered the word mark TARGET VENTURES on 28/05/2015 as an EUTM for services in Classes 35 and 36. The EUTM proprietor has been the proprietor of the domain names ‘www.targetventures.com’ and ‘www.targetventures.de’ since 2002 and 2009 respectively. However, the content of these websites has only ever referred to ‘Target Partners’, which is the sign under which the EUTM proprietor offers its services. Those sites are merely a means of redirection to the EUTM proprietor’s official site, ‘www.targetpartners.de’.

Pursuant to Article 52(1)(b) CTMR, an invalidity application was filed on 13/07/2015 by Target Ventures Group Ltd., a venture capital fund. The applicant claims that it has been operating under the sign ‘TARGET VENTURES’ in the Russian venture capital market since 2012 and in the EU market since 8 March 2013, and that the EUTM proprietor had applied for the EUTM in bad faith. The Cancellation Division dismissed the invalidity application in its entirety.

The invalidity applicant filed an appeal before the Board of Appeal (BoA). The BoA dismissed the appeal. It found that the invalidity applicant had not proved that the EUTM proprietor knew, or had presumed knowledge of, the services offered in the EU by it under the sign ‘TARGET VENTURES’. It was also not proven that the proprietor solely intended to prevent the invalidity applicant from entering the EU market. On the contrary, the evidence showed that the EUTM proprietor had a legitimate business interest in having the EUTM registered.

The invalidity applicant filed an action before the General Court (GC), relying on two pleas in law: (i) infringement of Article 52(1)(b) CTMR and (ii) infringement of Article 75 CTMR. The GC upheld the action.

SUBSTANCE:

(i) INFRINGEMENT OF ARTICLE 52(1)(b) CTMR

The BoA interpreted the concept of bad faith too restrictively when holding that it involves conduct which departs from accepted principles of ethical behaviour or honest commercial and business practices and presupposes a dishonest intention or other sinister motive. In fact, the intention of obtaining, without even targeting a specific third party, an exclusive right for purposes other than those falling within the functions of a trade mark, in particular the essential function of indicating origin, may be sufficient for finding bad faith by the trade mark applicant (12/09/2019, C 104/18 P, STYLO & KOTON (fig.), EU:C:2019:724, § 45-46; 29/01/2020, C 371/18, SKY, EU:C:2020:45, § 74-75) (§ 27).

Consequently, the BoA erred in law in interpreting Article 52(1)(b) CTMR by finding that the lack of proof of actual or presumed knowledge of prior use of the sign TARGET VENTURES was sufficient to reject the application for a declaration of invalidity (§ 29, 46). Moreover, the examination of whether the proprietor of the contested mark had prior knowledge of a third party’s use of that sign must not be confined to the European Union market (12/09/2019, C 104/18 P, STYLO & KOTON (fig.), EU:C:2019:724, § 51, 52 and 55) (§ 47).

It is apparent from objective, relevant and consistent indicia that the proprietor’s intention at the time of filing the contested mark was not to put it to a use falling within the functions of a trade mark, and that may indeed constitute bad faith pursuant to C 371/18 Sky judgment (§ 35 36). In fact, registering a mark in order to prevent a likelihood of confusion with another mark to protect the element that is common to them is extraneous to the functions of a trade mark, in particular the function of indicating commercial origin. The proprietor's conduct contributed more towards strengthening and protecting the proprietor’s first mark, namely ‘TARGET PARTNERS’, considering also that the websites ‘www.targetventures.de’ and ‘www.targetventures.com’ merely provide a means of redirection to the proprietor’s main website (§ 38-39).

In addition, the BoA did not consider the chronology of events that were relevant to the present case (§ 48).

The contested decision must be annulled without examining the second plea (§ 49).

keywords: Contrary to public policy or principles of morality
results.summary:
1. The contested mark as well as the earlier registration were genuinely used, as it was admitted by the cancellation applicant. Therefore, no dishonest intention based on non-use of the contested mark might exist on the part of the EUTM proprietor. 
2. Since there is a commercial logic to the filing of the contested mark and it can be assumed that the EUTM proprietor intended to use the sign as a trade mark, there is no dishonest intention. 
3. The burden of proof cannot be shifted to the EUTM proprietor as requested by the cancellation applicant. 
4. Since the cancellation applicant has failed to meet the burden of proof and the application for a declaration of invalidity (i) on the grounds of bad faith pursuant to Article 59(1)(b) EUTMR and (ii) on the grounds set in Article 59(1)(a) EUTMR in conjunction with Article 7(1)(f) EUTMR, must be rejected as unfounded.

results.quotation: (13/10/2020, R 1838/2018‑5, DEVICE OF A DRAGON AND TWO MARTIAL ARTS FIGHTERS (fig.) / DEVICE OF A DRAGON AND TWO MARTIAL ARTS FIGHTERS (fig.) et al.)
results.nickName: DEVICE OF A DRAGON AND TWO MARTIAL ARTS FIGHTERS (fig.) / DEVICE OF A DRAGON AND TWO MARTIAL ARTS FIGHTERS (fig.) et al.
results.summary: The evidence produced before the Cancellation Division and at the stage of appeal is insufficient to demonstrate the alleged bad faith of the EUTM proprietor at the date of filing. The other grounds also fail. An EU non-registered trade mark is not an eligible basis for invalidity proceedings. The applicant failed to substantiate its right to a copyright.

It has not been demonstrated that the EUTM proprietor knew or had to know that the cancellation applicant used the sign ‘AMINOSAN’. There is no evidence that the EUTM proprietor applied for registration of a sign as an EU trade mark without intending to use it, its sole objective being to prevent a third party from entering the market. On the contrary, the lodging of an EUTM application identical to the mark it has been used on the national Italian mark is consonant with commercial logic.

results.quotation: (05/10/2020, T‑264/19, viscover (fig.) / VISCOVER et al., EU:T:2020:470)
results.summary:
FACTS:

The proprietor registered the figurative mark ‘viscover’ as an EUTM for goods and services in Classes 1, 2, 5, 9, 10, 42 and 44. An invalidity application was filed pursuant to Article 52(1)(b) CTMR, Article 53(1)(c) CTMR and Article 53(2)(c) CTMR.

The invalidity applicant and the proprietor of the contested EUTM concluded a cooperation and distribution agreement in 2009. That agreement was terminated in 2013 following a deterioration of the business relationship. Under the agreement, the EUTM proprietor was the exclusive distributor of the goods developed and produced by the invalidity applicant. In this context, the EUTM proprietor applied for the contested mark in 2010.

The invalidity applicant argued, in particular, that the EUTM proprietor had applied for the contested mark (i) in bad faith and (ii) in breach of its copyright in the term ‘viscover’. The Cancellation Division dismissed the invalidity application on both accounts.

The invalidity applicant filed an appeal before the Board of Appeal (BoA). The BoA dismissed the appeal. With respect to the earlier copyright, the BoA found that the invalidity applicant had not established its existence. As regards bad faith, the BoA found that the EUTM proprietor had a right to file the contested mark in its own name under the cooperation and distribution agreement. It further stated that the facts subsequent to the filing of the contested mark did not indicate the existence of bad faith at the time of filing.

The invalidity applicant filed an action before the General Court (GC), relying on two pleas in law: (i) infringement of Article 52(1)(b) CTMR and (ii) infringement of Article 53(2)(c) CTMR. The GC dismissed the action.


EUTM	Earlier right: copyright (DE)
 	viscover


SUBSTANCE:

(i) INFRINGEMENT OF ARTICLE 52(1)(b) CTMR

Neither the letter nor the spirit of the cooperation agreement required the EUTM proprietor to file the mark in the name of both parties or to inform the invalidity applicant that it had only been filed in the distributor’s own name (§ 48-57). The filing of the contested mark cannot be constitutive of bad faith, considering the wording of the cooperation agreement. Rather, it followed commercial logic, stemming from the proprietor’s contractual position as exclusive distributor (§ 58-77). The invalidity applicant does not succeed in proving that, at time of filing of the contested trade mark in June 2010, the proprietor had the intention to prevent the invalidity applicant from selling its goods under the trade mark after the end of the term of the contract. Nor does the invalidity applicant submit any evidence that the proprietor could foresee that the cooperation between them would be terminated prematurely (§ 78-84).

(ii) INFRINGEMENT OF ARTICLE 53(2)(c) CTMR

The invalidity applicant does not succeed in proving the existence of its earlier copyright in the term ‘viscover’ (§ 88-106). The invalidity applicant merely referred to several provisions of German copyright law without providing any evidence that the designation ‘viscover’ could be considered a literary work under German law (§ 91).

results.quotation: (30/09/2020, R 2604/2019‑2, DEVICE OF A DEER'S HEAD (fig.) / TRORY IRELAND (fig.) et al.)
results.nickName: DEVICE OF A DEER'S HEAD (fig.) / TRORY IRELAND (fig.) et al.
keywords: Identity of the goods and services
Used in the course of trade
results.summary: The opponents' arguments based on bad faith and deceptiveness of the mark applied for are inadmissible in opposition proceedings. The visually similarity is of a a very low degree, if any. The signs cannot be compared aurally and are conceptually similar to an average degree. The visual aspect plays a greater role in the global assessment of the likelihood of confusion for goods in Classes 18 and 25. The visual dissimilarities neutralise any conceptual similarities. The Board confirms that there is no likelihood of confusion even for identical goods. As to 8(4) EUTMR ground of opposition,  the opponents did not sufficiently prove to have used the non-registered mark in the course of trade of more than local significance at the filing date of the EUTMA.

keywords: Similarity of the goods and services
results.summary: The EUTM reproduces as the equally dominant component the dominant figurative element of the earlier marks. There would be a likelihood of confusion as far as concerns the goods and services in Classes 25 and 35. There is a nexus or affinity with the contested services in Class 39 . There are in any case factual circumstances that establish bad faith on his part in filing the EUTM for all the contested services even in the absence of a likelihood of confusion. In all reasonableness, the EUTM proprietor being Spanish and having applied for an EUTM for goods and services in the same sector would have been aware of the economic success and expansion of the cancellation applicant which continued after 2003 and existed at the date the EUTM was filed and evidenced.

results.quotation: (04/09/2020, R 1541/2019‑4, PALLADIUM HOTEL ATHÉNEE PALACE (fig.) / Grand hotel palladium)
results.nickName: PALLADIUM HOTEL ATHÉNEE PALACE (fig.) / Grand hotel palladium
keywords: Sign used in the course of trade
results.summary: The evidence and arguments submitted by the applicant for a declaration of invalidity do not prove that the contested EUTM was applied for in bad faith. As regards the relative ground for invalidity, the evidence submitted by the applicant for a declaration of invalidity is not sufficient to prove the prior existence of the sign 'GRAND HOTEL PALLADIUM' on the date on which the contested EUTM was applied for, nor that that right still exists.

The principal request to assign the EUTM to the cancellation applicant (Article 21) is admissible but unfounded.
No principal-agent relationship in the sense of Article 8(3) was shown.
The other (subsidiary or alternative) requests are inadmissible.
It is in particular inadmissible to ask both the assignment and the declaration of invalidity on the mark, be it cumulatively or in the alternative.
This applies to the declaration of invalidity on the grounds of Article 8(3), but also to any other ground for declaration of invalidity (e.g. bad  faith).

The EUTM proprietor acted in bad faith when filing the EUTM 'AGATE' for tires in Class 12. It was in the same niche sector where the truck and bus tires ‘AGATE’ were sold by the cancellation applicant, a Chinese company, through its Bulgarian distributor already for more than two years. Email correspondence confirms that the EUTM proprietor has already been familiar with the Chinese tire manufacturers and the products offered by its competitors on the Bulgarian market. There is a very short term between the filing of the EUTM application, its registration and the enforcement actions initiated by the EUTM proprietor against the cancellation applicant and its Bulgarian distributors, another indicator that the EUTM proprietor must have known about the cancellation applicant’s trade mark ‘AGATE’ being used in Bulgaria.  Illustrative of the dishonest intentions of the EUTM proprietor is also the fact that within one month after the filing of the contested EUTM, the EUTM proprietor seemingly developed activities with the goal of taking over the clients of the cancellation applicant’s distributor. The EUTM proprietor has not shown any use of the mark ‘AGATE’ as an indication of the commercial origin of its own products. Email correspondence shows that the EUTM proprietor’s position was limited to prevent the cancellation applicant from using the mark ‘AGATE’ in the Bulgarian market.

results.summary: The EUTM against which the cancellation request (based on bad faith) was directed was surrendered immediately after the beginning of the proceedings. The cancellation applicant appealed against the closure of the file, but without success. He failed to demonstrate a legitimate interest to continue the case. All arguments in that direction relate to future hypothetical scenarios.

keywords: Used in the course of trade
results.summary: Evidence and arguments brought forward do not show that EUTM owner had fraudulent intentions when filing challenged EUTM. Agreement passed between the parties was strictly respected by the EUTM while this party also offered consistent argument that the application is in line with a commercial logic. Existence of use in the course of trade of more than local significance was not shown at the filing of the contested EUTM nor that it was still present when filing the invalidity action. Proof submitted do not show to a sufficient degree the economic and geografical dimension of more than local significance of the use in the course of trade. This is not proven even when the position of the GC in another case with the same parties would be applied here. Still no sufficient evidence on use of more than local signification at the time on which the nullity action was filed is missing. There is no need to enter into conditions established in Article 8(4)(B) EUTMR as it would not change outcome. Acquiescense not present, no use of challenged EUTM was shown for a continuous period of 5 years. No need to enter into whether other conditions are met.

results.quotation: (25/05/2020, R 2716/2019‑5, Fast fitness studio 20 minutes ems training (fig.) / Fast fitness)
results.entityName: FAST FITNESS STUDIO 20 MINUTES EMS TRAINING
results.nickName: Fast fitness studio 20 minutes ems training (fig.) / Fast fitness
results.summary: The objective circumstances of the present case indicate bad faith on the part of the EUTM proprietor in filing the EUTM, which  reproduces the invalidity applicant’s sign entirely at its beginning and  as the dominant element for goods that are similar to the invalidity applicant’s services. There was  a prior relationship between the parties,  and as a result the EUTM proprietor was able to secure the use of the sign ‘FAST FITNESS STUDIO’ for its fitness premises . The EUTM proprietor knew that the invalidity applicant was using the sign ‘FAST FITNESS’ that could be confused with the sign for which registration was sought. When the invalidity applicant proposed a ‘certification’, the  EUTM was filed as a reaction to the proposal for the certification policy offered by the invalidity applicant and invoked to prevent the invalidity applicant from using its ‘FAST FITNESS’ sign in every context. By the EUTM, the EUTM proprietor was able to secure the closure of the invalidity applicant’s Facebook page of the fitness studios. The letters of its lawyers clearly explain the EUTM proprietor’s intentions, which are that the EUTM was filed to gain access to the Greek market to the detriment of the invalidity applicant.

keywords: Used in the course of trade
results.summary: No dishonest intention of the EUTM proprietor at the date of filing of the contested mark was proven. Further, the invalidity applicant demonstrated that it usually uses a sign where ‘GEOGRAPHIC’ is depicted in very small letters and placed underneath the distinctive and dominant element ‘NAPAPIJRI’ (depicted in larger letters). ‘GEOGRAPHIC’ is therefore clearly secondary within the sign, contrary to and cannot be considered as valid use of the sign ‘GEOGRAPHIC’ for the purposes of Article 8(4) EUTMR.

results.summary: The General Court in case T-772/15 annuled the decision issued in case R 1540/2015-5. ECJ pourvoi not admissible. The Board takes note that GC concluded on the existence of bath faith.

GC in case T-773/17 annulled decision of BoA in R 1618/2015-5. It found that the refusal on the existence of bad baith was based on reasoning where had been found incorrect (dissimilarity on goods and signs). GC considered that the signs in dispute can produce that same overall impression and that some of the goods for which the contested EUTM was registered are similar to the goods/services for which the parties in dispute use the sign 'Café del Mar'. Subjective intention from the owner when EUTM was filed can be inferred from the factual circumstances of the case, similar overall impression of the signs and on the goods, and the position of the GC in two other parallel cases with the same parties involved (T-771/17 and T-772/17). It is concluded that in this case the EUTM was also applied for in bad faith.

Following the annulment of the decision of the First Board of Appeal by the General Court (T-136/18), this Board, to which the case has been reassigned, must take the necessary measures to comply with the Court's judgment. Given that, on the basis of the latter, the ground of invalidity laid down in Article 59(1)(b) EUTMR (bad faith at the moment of filing) must be upheld, the Board is called upon to reach this conclusion by confirming the Court's assessment in this regard also taking into account, for this purpose, an element on which the First Board of Appeal had not taken a position, namely the circumstance that, on the same day of filing of the contested mark, the then applicant had filed another figurative trademark "K", which made the application of the contested mark superfluous and is a further indication of the applicant's intention to impede the use of competitors' products.

results.quotation: (28/04/2020, R 2174/2019‑4, RIVIERA AIRPORT (fig.) / Riviera airport et al.)
results.nickName: RIVIERA AIRPORT (fig.) / Riviera airport et al.
results.summary:
The cancellation request was a broadside charge with different grounds for cancellation, even contradicting each other.
On appeal the cancellation applicant only invoked bad faith.
The gist of the cancellation applicant’s pleadings was that they had used a name for an airport ‘Riviera Airport’ in Italy. The entity running several airports on the French side of the ‘Riviera’ had applied for a figurative sign ‘Riviera Airport(s)’.
The evidence showed that the cancellation applicant never used the sign in conflict outwardly and just had a plan for modernizing their own airport.
Without any prior se none of the ‘Goldhase’ criteria (knowledge of a sign used by another party which has some degree of legal protection) is fulfilled. There was also no fiduciary relationship between the parties.

results.quotation: (14/04/2020, R 2433/2019‑5, Cognac henri iv dudognon heritage (fig.) / Dudognon)
results.nickName: Cognac henri iv dudognon heritage (fig.) / Dudognon
results.summary: The Board agrees with the cancellation division that the registered EUTM was filed in bad faith.

Based on the provided documents, it is obvious that the EUTM proprietor commits free-riding acts by (i) using a certainly similar sign even if not enough similar to establish a likelihood of confusion between the signs in question (ii) by copying the characteristics of the products sold by the cancellation applicant; (iii) by using similar stylization of the earlier sign on its products. The proprietor filed the EUTM with intent to harm and in order to compete unfairly with the cancellation applicant by taking advantage of its reputation. Therefore, the Board concluded that there is a bad faith on the date of filing of the contested EUTM.

results.nickName: GEOGRAPHICAL NORWAY EXPEDITION (fig.) / NAPAPIJRI geographic (fig.) et al.
results.summary: Based on the provided documents, it is obvious that the EUTM proprietor commits free-riding acts by (i) using a certainly similar sign even if not enough similar to establish a likelihood of confusion between the signs in question (ii) by copying the characteristics of the products sold by the cancellation applicant; (iii) by using similar stylization of the earlier sign on its products. The proprietor filed the EUTM with intent to harm and in order to compete unfairly with the cancellation applicant by taking advantage of its reputation. Therefore, the Board concluded that there is a bad faith on the date of filing of the contested EUTM.
The BoA confirms the decision of the first instance that the contested mark was filed in bad faith. The decision includes a section on belated evidence.

The cancellation applicant failed to submit a registration certificate or the like with the list of goods and services of the earlier mark. The Opposition Division correctly refused the cancellation request based on the German national mark as non-substantiated. The respective documents filed before the Board are belated and are not taken into account.
Also the ground for cancellation under Article 59(1)(b), bad faith, had to be rejected. The cancellation applicant was not able to show knowledge of the proprietor about the earlier mark, or any other special relationship between the parties, or any other dishonesty. There is no reversal of burden of proof to the detriment of the defendant.

results.quotation: (25/03/2020, R 2472/2018‑4, Munich10A.T.M. / MUNICH X (fig.) et al.)
results.summary: POU: despite doubts, the Board will proceed on the hypothetical basis that use has been proven as held by the Cancellation Division (mainly sports footwear). In light of the similarity of the signs, there is LOC for all the conflciting, goods and services which are identical or similar, but this does not include the contested 'sunglasses' in Class 9. These are dissimilar to retail and wholesale services for all kinds of sport articles, since they are not sport articles. Nor does the theory of aesthetic complementarity make them similar. Nor are 'artículos de relojería e instrumentos cronométricos’ in Cl 14 similar to sporting articles. Any use in sport is merely contingent and not necessary. The same is true of the contested Class 35 retail services for these goods as against the earlier retail services for sporting articles. Similarity of conflicting goods and services is required under 8(1)(b), 8(2) and 8(4) for the Spanish law invoked, so the application fails for all these. As for Art 8(5), the applicant has failed to prove reputation. So-called 'certificates' are probative of nothing in this respect, they also breach the right to defence and wrongly apply res judicata to the issue. The various studies all contain fundamental flaws, in particular leading questions, and/or small and/or  non-random sample sizes.

keywords: Similarity of the goods and services
results.summary:
The request to declare the EUTM invalid was based on a broadside of grounds and bases, absolute and relative. Already the grounds that the cancellation applicant owns used-based rights in the word CELERITAS whereas it argued that this word is descriptive for the contested mark, are contradictory.
As regards Article 8(1)(b) the priority claim for the contested EUTM is invalid (no proof of national filing has been submitted) so this ground for cancellation succeeds on the basis of the cancellation applicant’s (indeed) earlier EUTM, but only against the similar goods (measuring apparatus and the like). For the remaining goods and services, all grounds for cancellation fail, in particular those based on used-based rights in Italy (Italian law not substantiated) and on bad faith (no evidence that the EUTM proprietor had knowledge of a use of the same sign by the cancellation applicant, or that it had demanded money, or of any specific relationship between these two parties). Only general allegations were made, that the EUTM proprietor has filed many other marks, that he has been ‘condemned’ for bad faith in another proceedings, or that he does not have an intention to use, and those are not sufficient.

results.earlierName: IN VINO PAX WdW HUMANITAS VERITAS

results.summary:
The decision concerns the declaration of invalidity of the trade mark 'LA IRLANDESA' under two aspects: 

Firstly, the EUTM is declared invalid on the grounds of it being deceptive within the meaning of Article 7(1)(g) EUTMR. The dominant word elements 'LA IRLANDESA' establish a geographical link to Ireland. Used on the goods at issue in Class 29, ‘LA IRLANDESA’ will be understood by the relevant Spanish-speaking consumers as an indication that these products are of Irish origin. It has however been proven by the invalidity applicants that the goods are not of Irish origin. The serious risk that consumers would believe that the goods bearing the mark were of Irish origin, when in fact they were not, was already present at the time of filing the EUTM application. 

Secondly, the EUTM is declared invalid on the grounds that it was filed in bad faith. The objective circumstances of the case lead to this conclusion, namely, the use, in a deceptive manner, of the contested mark by its proprietor, the many previous EUIPO and Spanish Court decisions establishing other 'LA IRLANDESA' marks as being deceptive, and the previous business relationship with one of the (Irish) invalidity applicants. These factors lead to the conclusion that it was the EUTM proprietor's intention to take advantage of the image of Irish products, even though these were not of Irish origin.

The earlier IR was not properly substantiated. The belated extracts from WIPO´s database cannot be allowed at the stage of appeal. The EUTM proprietor filed three marks which are identical to the marks previously registered in another jurisdiction by the cancellation applicants on the same day. These objective circumstances involving the three parallel filings impose the element of awareness of the previously registered rights on the EUTM proprietor and furthermore serve as a strong indication of the EUTM proprietor’s dishonest intention at the date of filing. The awareness infers that the intention of the EUTM proprietor when these three trade marks were filed without the knowledge or consent of the cancellation applicants was to misappropriate the other trader’s goodwill. 	Considering the absence of any logical explanation or objective commercial trajectory by the EUTM proprietor for the filing of the contested EUTM (together with the other two EUTMs), the EUTM proprietor acted in bad faith when filing the contested EUTM.

KEY POINTS: Where certain objective circumstances show that the EUTM proprietor filed the mark in bad faith, the potentially descriptive character of the element common to both signs cannot prevail over the finding of bad faith (para. 69).

FACTS: 

The proprietor was granted registration of the EUTM for services in Classes 35, 36 and 42.

An invalidity application was filed pursuant to Article 52(1)(b) CTMR. The Cancellation Division (CD) upheld the invalidity application, considering that the proprietor had intentionally sought to appropriate the element ‘outsource2india’ already used by the invalidity applicant and thus acted in bad faith when filing for registration.

The proprietor appealed before the Board of Appeal (BoA). The BoA upheld the appeal, considering that the proprietor had not acted in bad faith when it registered the contested mark. The proprietor had envisaged establishing a commercial relationship with the invalidity applicant by helping undertakings established in Germany or elsewhere in the EU to outsource their services to India. It was not apparent from the case file that the proprietor intended to appropriate the element ‘outsource2india’. Since this term is descriptive and may therefore be used freely by all traders in the sector for outsourcing services to India, the intention of using it cannot be regarded as dishonest intent. It had not been established that the proprietor wanted to undermine the invalidity applicant’s interests.

The applicant filed an action before the General Court (GC), relying on a single plea in law: infringement of Article 52(1)(b) CTMR. The GC dismissed the action (31/05/2018, T 340/16, Outsource 2 India, EU:T:2018:314).

The GC stated that, by filing, shortly after the termination of the pre-contractual relationship, an application for registration as an EU trade mark of a sign similar to that used by the invalidity applicant, the proprietor intended to exploit the economic potential of the element ‘outsource2india’, which, in view of the correspondence exchanged between the parties and developments in their exchanges, demonstrated that the proprietor had acted in bad faith. The GC stated moreover that this assessment was ‘[independent] of the question whether the wording ‘outsource2india’ is descriptive’.


SUBSTANCE: 

The CJ confirms the GC’s finding that inclusion of a descriptive term in the EUTM does not exclude existence of bad faith on the part of the proprietor.

The CJ reveals numerous flaws in the GC’s judgment: some of the facts were distorted by the GC (paras 51-56); the GC did not justify its finding that the contested mark is similar to the sign used by the invalidity applicant and that it could be confused with it (para. 59); the GC was wrong to find that the proprietor intended to exploit the economic potential that could be mined from the name of the invalidity applicant’s sign, because it is based on an unsubstantiated assertion that the signs in question are similar (para. 66).

However, despite those flaws, the CJ upholds the GC’s judgment, holding that the GC had highlighted certain objective circumstances and, solely on the basis of these circumstances, it was entitled to find that the proprietor had acted in bad faith. These circumstances are: the proprietor’s attempt to establish commercial collaboration with the invalidity applicant in Germany, from November 2006; the proprietor’s establishment, for that purpose and without prior consent of the invalidity applicant, of a German company named ‘Outsource2India’, which includes the element ‘outsource2india’ used by the invalidity applicant; the EUTM owner’s use of that element in the name of its website; the reluctance and subsequent objections expressed between February and May 2007 by the invalidity applicant regarding this situation, which it did not intend to continue unless a collaboration agreement was reached (paras 67-68).

The proprietor acted in bad faith when, shortly after the invalidity applicant refused cooperation, it requested registration of the EUTM specifically containing the element ‘outsource2india’, used by the invalidity applicant in its commercial activities (para. 70). The potentially descriptive character of ‘outsource2india’ does not prevent the conclusion that there was bad faith on the part of the proprietor (para. 69).

results.summary: In the absence of any evidence that the cancellation applicant has used its earlier rights in the EU, the mere knowledge that these earlier rights enjoy protection in the US and/or outside the EU cannot suffice. The fact that the EUTM proprietor knows that a third party is using a mark abroad that is liable to be confused with the EUTM applied for is in itself not sufficient to reason bad faith. Indeed, no particular recognition or extensive use of the cancellation applicant’s mark in the European Union has been demonstrated as seen under reputation section above for Article 8(5) EUTMR. Also, as already mentioned, it has not been shown that the EUTM proprietor’s sole purpose in registering the mark at issue was to prevent the cancellation applicant from marketing its products. Nothing in the EUTM proprietor’s behaviour at the time of filing suggests that the application was made with the intention to prevent the cancellation applicant from using the mark in the European Union.

keywords: Dissimilarity of the goods and services
results.summary:
FACTS: 

The EUTM proprietor sought to register the figurative mark as an EUTM for goods and services in Classes 25, 35 and 39.

An opposition based on the earlier figurative marks, registered for goods and services in Classes 18, 25 and 35, was filed pursuant to Article 8(1)(b) CTMR. The Opposition Division (OD) upheld the opposition insofar as it related to goods and services in Classes 25 and 35. The decision was confirmed by the Board of Appeal (BoA).

An invalidity application was filed, pursuant to Article 52(1)(b) CTMR, against the EUTM that was registered in Class 39. The Cancellation Division (CD) dismissed the invalidity application, finding that it had not been proven that the intervener had acted in bad faith.

The invalidity applicant appealed. The BoA dismissed the appeal, finding that there could be no bad faith within the meaning of Article 52(1)(b) CTMR since there was neither identity nor similarity between the goods or services.

The invalidity applicant filed an action before the General Court (GC), relying on a single plea in law: (i) infringement of Article 52(1)(b) CTMR. The GC dismissed the action. It found that the BoA was fully entitled to decide that bad faith had not been established by relying on the fact that the contested mark was registered for services dissimilar to those designated by the earlier marks, thus precluding any likelihood of confusion (LOC) between the marks at issue.

The invalidity applicant appealed to the Court of Justice of the European Union (CJ). The CJ upheld the appeal.

SUBSTANCE: 

The ground of bad faith applies where it is apparent from relevant and consistent indicia that the proprietor of an EU trade mark filed its application for registration, not with the aim of engaging fairly in competition, but with the intention of undermining the interests of third parties (in a manner inconsistent with honest practices), or with the intention of obtaining, without even targeting a specific third party, an exclusive right for purposes other than those falling within the functions of a trade mark, in particular, the essential function of indicating origin (para. 46)

There may be situations where the application for registration of a trade mark had been filed in bad faith notwithstanding the fact that, at the time of that application, there was no use by a third party on the internal market of an identical or similar sign for identical or similar goods. In applications for invalidity based on Article 52(1)(b) CTMR, there is no requirement whatsoever that the invalidity applicant be the proprietor of an earlier mark for identical or similar goods or services (paras 52 and 53).

In cases where, at the time of the application for the contested mark, a third party was using, in at least one Member State, a sign identical with, or similar to, that mark, the existence of LOC need not necessarily be established for Article 52(1)(b) CTMR to apply (para. 54).

In the absence of LOC, or if there has been no use by a third party of a sign identical with, or similar to, the contested mark, other factual circumstances may constitute relevant and consistent indicia establishing the bad faith of the applicant (para. 56).

The GC should have taken account of the fact that the EUTM proprietor had sought registration of a sign comprising the stylised word KOTON as an EUTM, not only for services in Class 39, but also for goods and services in Classes 25 and 35 (para. 60).

keywords: Similarity of the goods and services

Most of the contested goods & services are similar, against them the cancellation request succeeds on the basis of Article 8(1)(b), as the signs are identical. For the few remaining dissimilar services bad faith needs to be examined and must be upheld.
Whether or not the defendant is a disguised straw man of a company with whom the appellant is in legal dispute, the evidence shows that the defendant merely sought to extort money (in completely disproportionate amounts) although he should have known that the appellant owns and uses an earlier trade mark.
