results.summary: The contested EUTM contains numerous goods and services which are simply a repeat of the earlier, already existing EUTMs ‘MONOPOLY’. All of the mentioned circumstances imply that the intention of the EUTM proprietor was to take advantage of the EU trade mark rules by artificially creating the situation where it would not have to prove genuine use of its earlier marks for the goods and services mentioned. Bad faith partially proven.

KEY POINTS :

The contested mark is the abbreviation of the earlier figurative sign and therefore the signs may produce the same overall impression (para. 49).

Leather goods may be sold within the framework of the sale of clothing and fashion accessories. Furthermore, umbrellas and perfumes may be part of the merchandising goods of a cafeteria or may be offered for sale as fashion accessories (para. 52).

FACTS:

On 24 April 2007 the proprietor applied for registration of the figurative mark ‘C del M’ as an EUTM for goods in Classes 3, 4 and 18. The EUTM was registered on 27 March 2008.

An invalidity application was filed pursuant to Article 52(1)(b) CTMR. The Cancellation Division (CD) dismissed the invalidity application.

The invalidity applicants filed an appeal. The Board of Appeal (BoA) dismissed the appeal: it found that the registered goods were not related to the exploitation of a cafeteria or disco and were not merchandising goods usually sold in an establishment known for its music.

The invalidity applicants filed an action with the General Court (GC), relying on one plea in law: (i) infringement of Article 52(1)(b) CTMR. The GC upheld the action.

SUBSTANCE:

(i) INFRINGEMENT OF ARTICLE 52(1)(b) CTMR. The GC found that the invalidity applicants and the proprietor had used the figurative sign ‘Café del Mar’ since 1980 when they opened the music bar ‘Café del Mar’ in Ibiza (Spain). The sign was also used to distinguish goods and services provided by various companies that the individual invalidity applicants and the proprietor had incorporated since 1987 (paras. 35 and 37).

The contested mark coincides in the letters ‘c’ and ‘m’ with the initial letters of the terms of said earlier sign, in the preposition ‘del’ and the typography is identical. The contested mark is the abbreviation of the earlier figurative sign and therefore the signs may produce the same overall impression (para. 49).

The bar ‘Café del Mar’ became well-known over the course of the years and its activities expanded to include music products, clothing and merchandising bearing the figurative sign ‘Café del Mar’ (paras. 43 and 50). It cannot be excluded that leather goods are sold within the framework of the sale of clothing and fashion accessories and umbrellas and perfumes may be part of the merchandising goods of a cafeteria or may be offered as fashion accessories (para. 52).

Therefore the contested mark is not completely different to the earlier figurative sign ‘Café del Mar’ and is registered for goods and services at least partially similar to those distinguished by the earlier sign (para. 53).

results.ipRight: EUTM on which IA is based
results.summary:
KEY POINTS:

The power of attorney granted to act on behalf of the company and to represent it cannot be considered as an acknowledgment of the supremacy of the representative regards the other partners as to rights in the sign ‘Café del Mar’. Furthermore, even if the company’s representative plays an outstanding role in the promotion and development of the sign, he is not entitled to use its power in his own name (para. 54). The fact that, for a certain period, the proprietor paid the profits obtained from the exploitation of the trade mark into the accounts of Can Ganguil is not relevant. This is because bad faith must be proven at the time of filing the application and the profits were paid later (para. 56).

FACTS:

The proprietor applied for the registration of the figurative mark ‘Café del Mar’ as an EUTM for goods and services in Classes 9, 25 and 42 on 26 January 1999. The EUTM was registered on 16 December 2003.

An invalidity application was filed pursuant to Article 52(1)(b) CTMR and Article 53(1)(c) CTMR in conjunction with Article 8(4) CTMR. The Cancellation Division (CD) upheld the invalidity application based on Article 52(1)(b) CTMR.

The proprietor filed an appeal. The Board of Appeal (BoA) upheld the appeal: it found that bad faith had not been proven at the time of filing the application for registration; the ground based on Article 8(4) CTMR was rejected as unfounded.

The invalidity applicants filed an action with the General Court (GC), relying on two pleas in law: (i) infringement of Article 52(1)(b) CTMR and (ii) infringement of Article 53(1)(c) CTMR. The GC upheld the action on the basis of Article 52(1)(b) CTMR.

SUBSTANCE:

(i) INFRINGEMENT OF ARTICLE 52(1)(b) CTMR. The GC found that the invalidity applicants and the proprietor had used the prior sign ‘Café del Mar’ since 1980 when they opened the music bar ‘Café del Mar’ in Ibiza (Spain). The sign was also used to distinguish goods and services provided by various companies that the individual invalidity applicants and the proprietor had incorporated since 1987 (paras 39-43), including goods and services similar to those for which the contested trade mark was registered (para. 47). All the companies incorporated by the individual invalidity applicants and the proprietor belonged to them equally. One of these companies, Can Ganguil, S.L., incorporated in 1997, granted a power of attorney to the proprietor to act on behalf of the company and to represent it (para. 40). Contrary to the BoA’s conclusion, the power of attorney granted to act on behalf of the company and to represent it cannot be considered as an acknowledgment of the supremacy of the representative regards the other partners as to rights in the sign ‘Café del Mar’. In any case, even if the company’s representative plays an outstanding role in the promotion and development of the sign, he is not entitled to use its power in his own name (para. 54). In his position as attorney of Can Ganguil and partner in the rest of companies using the earlier sign ‘Café del Mar’, the proprietor could not ignore the detriment caused by the registration in his exclusive name of the sign ‘Café del Mar’ (para. 52). Contrary to the BoA’s conclusion, there is no evidence on file showing that the invalidity applicants knew that the proprietor had registered the contested trade mark in his name (para. 53).

Since the proprietor registered a trade mark generating confusion with the earlier sign ‘Café del Mar’ while he was the representative of one of the companies exploiting said sign and paid the registration costs with funds from said company, he departed from accepted principles of ethical behaviour or honest commercial and business practices and therefore acted in bad faith (para. 55). In this regard, it is irrelevant that the proprietor had paid the profits obtained from the exploitation of the trade mark into the accounts of Can Ganguil until 2009, since bad faith must be proven at the time of filing the application and the profits were paid later. In addition, the proprietor ceased to share the profits of the trade mark exploitation as from 2009 and this shows the detrimental nature of the registration of the sign in his own and exclusive name (para. 56).

KEY POINTS: 

The power of attorney granted to act on behalf of the company and to represent it cannot be considered as an acknowledgment of the supremacy of the representative regards the other partners as to rights in the sign ‘Café del Mar’. Furthermore, even if the company’s representative plays an outstanding role in the promotion and development of the sign, he is not entitled to use its power in his own name (para. 53). The fact that, for a certain period, the proprietor paid the profits obtained from the exploitation of the trade mark into the accounts of Can Ganguil is not relevant. This is because bad faith must be proven at the time of filing the application and the profits were paid later (para. 55).

(i) INFRINGEMENT OF ARTICLE 52(1)(b) CTMR. In his position as attorney of Can Ganguil and partner in the other companies using the earlier sign ‘Café del Mar’, the proprietor could not ignore the detriment caused by the registration in his exclusive name of the sign ‘Café del Mar’ (para. 51). The invalidity applicants filed an action with the General Court (GC), relying on two pleas in law: (i) infringement of Article 52(1)(b) CTMR and (ii) infringement of Article 53(1)(c) CTMR. The GC upheld the action on the basis of Article 52(1)(b) CTMR.

SUBSTANCE:

(i) INFRINGEMENT OF ARTICLE 52(1)(b) CTMR. The GC found that the invalidity applicants and the proprietor had used the prior sign ‘Café del Mar’ since 1980 when they opened the music bar ‘Café del Mar’ in Ibiza (Spain). The sign was also used to distinguish goods and services provided by various companies that the individual invalidity applicants and the proprietor had incorporated since 1987 (paras 38-42), including services similar to those for which the contested trade mark was registered (para. 47). All the companies incorporated by the individual invalidity applicants and the proprietor belonged to them equally. One of these companies, Can Ganguil, S.L., incorporated in 1997, granted a power of attorney to the proprietor to act on behalf of the company and to represent it (para. 39). Contrary to the BoA’s conclusion, the power of attorney granted to act on behalf of the company and to represent it cannot be considered as an acknowledgment of the supremacy of the representative as regards the other partners as to rights in the sign ‘Café del Mar’. In any case, even if the company’s representative plays an outstanding role in the promotion and development of the sign, he is not entitled to use its power in his own name (para. 53). In his position as attorney of Can Ganguil and partner in the other companies using the earlier sign ‘Café del Mar’, the proprietor could not ignore the detriment caused by the registration in his exclusive name of the sign ‘Café del Mar’ (para. 51). Contrary to the BoA’s conclusion, there is no evidence on file showing that the invalidity applicants knew that the proprietor had registered the contested trade mark in his name (para. 52).

Since the proprietor registered a trade mark generating confusion with the earlier sign ‘Café del Mar’ while he was the representative of one of the companies exploiting said sign and paid the registration costs with funds from said company, he departed from accepted principles of ethical behaviour or honest commercial and business practices and therefore acted in bad faith (para. 54). In this regard, it is irrelevant that the proprietor had paid the profits obtained from the exploitation of the trade mark exploitation into the accounts of Can Ganguil until 2009, since bad faith must be proven at the time of filing the application and the profits were paid later. In addition, the proprietor ceased to share the profits of the trade mark exploitation as from 2009 and this shows the detrimental nature of the registration of the sign in his own and exclusive name (para. 55).

KEY POINTS :

The contested mark is the abbreviation of the earlier figurative sign and therefore the signs may produce the same overall impression (para. 49).

Leather goods may be sold within the framework of the sale of clothing and fashion accessories. Furthermore, umbrellas and perfumes may be part of the merchandising goods of a cafeteria or may be offered for sale as fashion accessories (para. 52).

FACTS:

On 24 April 2007 the proprietor applied for registration of the figurative mark ‘C del M’ as an EUTM for goods in Classes 3, 4 and 18. The EUTM was registered on 27 March 2008.

An invalidity application was filed pursuant to Article 52(1)(b) CTMR. The Cancellation Division (CD) dismissed the invalidity application.

The invalidity applicants filed an appeal. The Board of Appeal (BoA) dismissed the appeal: it found that the registered goods were not related to the exploitation of a cafeteria or disco and were not merchandising goods usually sold in an establishment known for its music.

The invalidity applicants filed an action with the General Court (GC), relying on one plea in law: (i) infringement of Article 52(1)(b) CTMR. The GC upheld the action.

SUBSTANCE:

(i) INFRINGEMENT OF ARTICLE 52(1)(b) CTMR. The GC found that the invalidity applicants and the proprietor had used the figurative sign ‘Café del Mar’ since 1980 when they opened the music bar ‘Café del Mar’ in Ibiza (Spain). The sign was also used to distinguish goods and services provided by various companies that the individual invalidity applicants and the proprietor had incorporated since 1987 (paras. 35 and 37).

The contested mark coincides in the letters ‘c’ and ‘m’ with the initial letters of the terms of said earlier sign, in the preposition ‘del’ and the typography is identical. The contested mark is the abbreviation of the earlier figurative sign and therefore the signs may produce the same overall impression (para. 49).

The bar ‘Café del Mar’ became well-known over the course of the years and its activities expanded to include music products, clothing and merchandising bearing the figurative sign ‘Café del Mar’ (paras. 43 and 50). It cannot be excluded that leather goods are sold within the framework of the sale of clothing and fashion accessories and umbrellas and perfumes may be part of the merchandising goods of a cafeteria or may be offered as fashion accessories (para. 52).

Therefore the contested mark is not completely different to the earlier figurative sign ‘Café del Mar’ and is registered for goods and services at least partially similar to those distinguished by the earlier sign (para. 53).

results.quotation: (04/07/2019, R 1344/2018‑1, WORLD UNITED KARATE ORGANIZATION A.D. (fig.))
results.summary: The EUTM proprietor has refiled a trade mark for the same services; the previous EUTM was invalidated on grounds of bad faith. There is identity of the parties, purpose and the submissions. There has not been a fresh examination of new facts, evidence or arguments. The later filing date accorded in this case would have no impact. No new facts have been put forward in support of the second application, that could have altered the assessment in the first decision and required a new assessment on the issue of bad faith. The contested decision is confirmatory and  is not open to legal challenge. To hold otherwise would jeopardize legal certainty. The appeal is inadmissible. Even if admissible, the EUTM would be invalidated on grounds of bad faith. The EUTM in question is the third in a series of EUTMs filed by herself or her late husband; the previous two were invalidated on grounds of bad faith by decisions that were confirmed by the BoA.  The EUTM proprietor, as the widow of Carlo Henke, who was a member of the invalidity applicant’s Referee Commission from 1977 to 1988 and one of the co-founders of a karate organization, that adopted the same name as the invalidity applicant’s former name. IT .is unlikely that residual knowledge about said name would have disappeared from the relevant public’s mind

results.quotation: (23/05/2019, T‑3/18, ANN TAYLOR / ANNTAYLOR et al., EU:T:2019:357)
results.summary:
KEY POINTS: 

For the purpose of proving bad faith it is not necessary to systematically establish the existence of a likelihood of confusion between an earlier trade mark and the contested mark (paras 56-57). In particular, it is not mandatory to show the presence in the European Union of a sign identical or similar to the sign whose registration is sought, for identical or similar goods or services, giving rise to a likelihood of confusion (paras 52-57). A correlation between the goods or services (i.e. between watches and clothing) is sufficient (paras 58, 64-65 and 69-72). Further, it is not necessary to systematically establish a reputation of the earlier mark in the European Union (paras 59-61).

Bad faith can be proved on the basis of sworn written statements of the invalidity applicant’s lawyers, acting as independent third parties (paras 94-99) or an email exchange between the lawyers of both parties establishing an attempt of the parties to obtain a licence agreement prior to the application of registration (paras 84-88). The use of these communications in the context of invalidity proceedings is not precluded, since they do not constitute a correspondence between lawyer and client that might be qualified confidential (paras 102-103).

The attempt to obtain the right to market goods under an identical trade mark owned by the invalidity applicant, that the latter had refused to follow up, constitutes an indication of bad faith (paras 124 and 125).

The use of the contested sign may constitute a factor to be taken into account when establishing the intention underlying the application for registration of the sign, including use subsequent to the date of that application (paras 118, 119 and 126).

FACTS:

The EUTM proprietor registered the word mark ANN TAYLOR as an EUTM for goods, i.e. clocks and watches in Class 14.

An invalidity application was filed pursuant to Articles 59 and 60 EUTMR in conjunction with Article 63(1) EUTMR. The Cancellation Division (CD) upheld the invalidity application. It found the mark to be invalid since the application for the contested mark had been filed in bad faith.

The proprietor filed an appeal. The Board of Appeal (BoA) dismissed the appeal. It found that the contested mark and the earlier marks were identical or highly similar and that the goods covered by the contested mark were products adjacent to the clothing industry, to which the goods of the earlier marks belonged, and there was therefore a close correlation between them.

The proprietor filed an action before the General Court (GC), relying on a single plea in law: infringement of Article 59(1)(b) EUTMR, divided into 3 grounds of complaint. The GC dismissed the action.

SUBSTANCE:

(i) THE EXISTENCE OF A SIMILARITY BETWEEN THE SIGNS ENTAILING A LIKELIHOOD OF CONFUSION AND THE PROPIETOR’S KNOWLEDGE OF THE EXISTENCE OF AN IDENTICAL OR SIMILAR MARK. 

The existence of bad faith at the time of filing of the application for registration must be assessed globally, account being taken of all factors relevant to the circumstances of the case; it cannot be confined to a limited category of specific circumstances (11/06/2009, C-529/07, Lindt Goldhase, EU:C:2009:361, § 35, 37 and 53) (paras 50-53).

For the purpose of proving bad faith, it is not necessary to systematically establish the existence of a likelihood of confusion between the earlier trade mark and the contested mark (paras 56-57). In particular, it is not mandatory to show the presence in the European Union of a sign identical or similar to the sign whose registration is sought, for identical or similar goods or services, giving rise to a likelihood of confusion (paras 52-57). A correlation between the goods, i.e. between watches and clothing is sufficient (paras 58, 64-65 and 69-72). Further, it is not necessary to systematically establish a reputation of the earlier mark in the European Union (paras 59-61).

In the present case, marks which are identical or highly similar to the marks at issue were used and protected since 1954 for goods belonging to a related market. They constituted a relevant factor for the purposes of assessing the existence of the applicant’s bad faith when filing the marks (para. 72).

The applicant was aware of the existence of an earlier mark comprising the sign ANN TAYLOR, as found on the bases of sworn written statements of lawyers of the invalidity applicant, acting as independent third parties (paras 94-99), and an email exchange between the lawyers of both parties establishing an attempt of the parties to obtain a licence agreement prior to the application of registration (paras 84-88). The use of these communications in the context of invalidity proceedings is not precluded, since they do not constitute a correspondence between lawyer and client that might be categorised as confidential (paras 102-103).

(ii) THE PROPRIETOR’S INTENTIONS THE TIME THE MARKS AT ISSUE WERE FILED.

The proprietor’s attempt to obtain the right to market watches under an identical trade mark owned by the invalidity applicant, that the latter had refused to accept, constitutes a first indication of bad faith (paras 124 and 125).

The use of the contested sign may constitute a factor to be taken into account when establishing the intention underlying the application for registration of the sign, including use subsequent to the date of that application (paras 118, 119 and 126).

The applicant filed trade mark applications in Mexico for marks identical to well-known marks without authorisation of the holders and without licensing agreements (paras 153-155). 

The fact that the filing of the marks is part of a commercial strategy to extend protection of the respective marks filed in Mexico is not sufficient to establish the legitimate nature of the applicant’s intentions, since the filing and use of the Mexican trade marks at issue are themselves part of a dishonest strategy (paras 160, 161 and 167).

The fact that the applicant did not request financial compensation or the fact that there was a genuine and continuous use of the marks from the time of their registration does not constitute evidence capable of demonstrating that that registration reflected legitimate intentions (para. 166).

(iii) THE PROBATIVE VALUE OF THE EVIDENCE IN SUPPORT OF THE APPLICATION FOR A DECLARATION OF INVALIDITY AND THE BURDEN OF PROOF.

The evidence on which the BoA’s findings are based is not incidental, doubtful or weak and there was no reversal of burden of proof (paras 177 and 180).

results.ipRight: EUTM on which IA is based
results.summary:
KEY POINTS:

The concept of bad faith relates to a subjective motivation on the part of the trade mark applicant, namely a dishonest intention or other sinister motive. It involves conduct that departs from accepted principles of ethical behaviour or honest commercial and business practices (para. 23).

The existence of bad faith on the part of the applicant for registration at the time of filing the application for registration of an EU trade mark must be assessed, inter alia, in the light of his intention. The intention of the applicant for registration at the relevant time is a subjective factor, which must be determined by reference to the objective circumstances of the particular case (para. 49).

Neymar was already recognised as a very promising football player on the relevant date (paras 30-33). It can be deduced from this objective fact, proved by evidence, and from the other objective fact that the EUTM proprietor had filed an application for registration of the word mark IKER CASILLAS on the same day, that the EUTM proprietor possessed more than a little knowledge of the world of football (para. 36). In the light of only those factors and the particular circumstances of the case, the real purpose of the commercial logic behind the application for registration of the EUTM was to ‘free-ride’ on NEYMAR’s reputation and take advantage of that reputation (paras 50-51).

FACTS:

The EUTM proprietor filed an application on 17 December 2012 and obtained registration of the word mark NEYMAR for goods in Class 25, namely clothing, footwear and headgear.

In February 2016, the Brazilian footballer Neymar Da Santos Júnior filed an invalidity application pursuant to Article 52(1)(b) CTMR. The Cancellation Division upheld the invalidity application.

The EUTM proprietor appealed against this decision. The Board of Appeal (BoA) dismissed the appeal, finding that the applicant had acted in bad faith when he filed the application for registration of the contested mark, because he had known that Neymar Da Santos Júnior — internationally known under his first name Neymar — was a rising star in football, whose talent was recognised internationally on the relevant date, and that the applicant had no motive other than to exploit Neymar’s renown to benefit from it.

The EUTM proprietor filed an action with the General Court (GC), relying on a single plea in law: (i) infringement of Article 52(1)(b) CTMR.

SUBSTANCE:

(i) INFRINGMENT OF ARTICLE 52(1)(b) CTMR.

The concept of bad faith relates to a subjective motivation on the part of the trade mark applicant, namely a dishonest intention or other sinister motive. It involves conduct that departs from accepted principles of ethical behaviour or honest commercial and business practices (para. 23).

The evidence submitted shows that Neymar Da Silva Santos Júnior was already recognised as a very promising football player, having drawn the attention of top-flight clubs in Europe with a view to future recruitment, several years before his actual transfer to FC Barcelona in 2013. He was therefore already well known ‘in the word of football’ at the time when the application for registration of the mark NEYMAR was filed (paras 31-33).

The EUTM proprietor possessed more than a little knowledge of the world of football, as proven by the fact that he had filed an application for registration of the word mark IKER CASILLAS, a mark corresponding to the name of another famous football player, on the same day that he sought registration of the mark ‘NEYMAR’ (paras 34 and 50).

As the mark, which consists of the single word element ‘NEYMAR’, corresponds precisely to the name under which Neymar Da Silva Santos Júnior became known for his football performances, it was inconceivable that the EUTM proprietor had not been informed of the footballer’s existence on the relevant date (paras 36, 44 and 46).

The existence of bad faith on the part of the applicant for registration must be assessed, inter alia, in the light of his intention. The intention of the applicant for registration at the relevant time is a subjective factor, which must be determined by reference to the objective circumstances of the particular case (para. 49).

It can be deduced from the objective fact that Neymar was already recognised as a very promising football player on the relevant date, and from the other objective fact that the EUTM proprietor had filed an application for registration of the word mark IKER CASILLAS on the same day, that the EUTM proprietor possessed more than a little knowledge of the world of football. In the light of only those factors and the particular circumstances of the case, the real purpose of the commercial logic behind the application for registration of the EUTM was to ‘free-ride’ on Neymar’s reputation and take advantage of that reputation (paras 50-51).

results.summary:
KEY POINTS:

The concept of bad faith relates to a subjective motivation on the part of the trade mark applicant, namely a dishonest intention or other sinister motive. It involves conduct that departs from accepted principles of ethical behaviour or honest commercial and business practices (para. 23).

The existence of bad faith on the part of the applicant for registration at the time of filing the application for registration of an EU trade mark must be assessed, inter alia, in the light of his intention. The intention of the applicant for registration at the relevant time is a subjective factor, which must be determined by reference to the objective circumstances of the particular case (para. 49).

Neymar was already recognised as a very promising football player on the relevant date (paras 30-33). It can be deduced from this objective fact, proved by evidence, and from the other objective fact that the EUTM proprietor had filed an application for registration of the word mark IKER CASILLAS on the same day, that the EUTM proprietor possessed more than a little knowledge of the world of football (para. 36). In the light of only those factors and the particular circumstances of the case, the real purpose of the commercial logic behind the application for registration of the EUTM was to ‘free-ride’ on NEYMAR’s reputation and take advantage of that reputation (paras 50-51).

FACTS:

The EUTM proprietor filed an application on 17 December 2012 and obtained registration of the word mark NEYMAR for goods in Class 25, namely clothing, footwear and headgear.

In February 2016, the Brazilian footballer Neymar Da Santos Júnior filed an invalidity application pursuant to Article 52(1)(b) CTMR. The Cancellation Division upheld the invalidity application.

The EUTM proprietor appealed against this decision. The Board of Appeal (BoA) dismissed the appeal, finding that the applicant had acted in bad faith when he filed the application for registration of the contested mark, because he had known that Neymar Da Santos Júnior — internationally known under his first name Neymar — was a rising star in football, whose talent was recognised internationally on the relevant date, and that the applicant had no motive other than to exploit Neymar’s renown to benefit from it.

The EUTM proprietor filed an action with the General Court (GC), relying on a single plea in law: (i) infringement of Article 52(1)(b) CTMR.

SUBSTANCE:

(i) INFRINGMENT OF ARTICLE 52(1)(b) CTMR.

The concept of bad faith relates to a subjective motivation on the part of the trade mark applicant, namely a dishonest intention or other sinister motive. It involves conduct that departs from accepted principles of ethical behaviour or honest commercial and business practices (para. 23).

The evidence submitted shows that Neymar Da Silva Santos Júnior was already recognised as a very promising football player, having drawn the attention of top-flight clubs in Europe with a view to future recruitment, several years before his actual transfer to FC Barcelona in 2013. He was therefore already well known ‘in the word of football’ at the time when the application for registration of the mark NEYMAR was filed (paras 31-33).

The EUTM proprietor possessed more than a little knowledge of the world of football, as proven by the fact that he had filed an application for registration of the word mark IKER CASILLAS, a mark corresponding to the name of another famous football player, on the same day that he sought registration of the mark ‘NEYMAR’ (paras 34 and 50).

As the mark, which consists of the single word element ‘NEYMAR’, corresponds precisely to the name under which Neymar Da Silva Santos Júnior became known for his football performances, it was inconceivable that the EUTM proprietor had not been informed of the footballer’s existence on the relevant date (paras 36, 44 and 46).

The existence of bad faith on the part of the applicant for registration must be assessed, inter alia, in the light of his intention. The intention of the applicant for registration at the relevant time is a subjective factor, which must be determined by reference to the objective circumstances of the particular case (para. 49).

It can be deduced from the objective fact that Neymar was already recognised as a very promising football player on the relevant date, and from the other objective fact that the EUTM proprietor had filed an application for registration of the word mark IKER CASILLAS on the same day, that the EUTM proprietor possessed more than a little knowledge of the world of football. In the light of only those factors and the particular circumstances of the case, the real purpose of the commercial logic behind the application for registration of the EUTM was to ‘free-ride’ on Neymar’s reputation and take advantage of that reputation (paras 50-51).

KEY POINTS:

Bad faith of a distributor when filing an EUTM application in the context of a deterioration of the contractual relationships (distribution agreement) with the manufacturer.

The factors to be taken into consideration are: (i) the question whether the EUTM proprietor knew, at the date of filing of the EUTM, that the invalidity applicant had previously been using the sign corresponding to the EUTM, (ii) the nature of the contractual relationship between the parties, (iii) the origin of the sign corresponding to the EUTM, (iv) the degree of legal protection of the sign corresponding to the EUTM and (v) the EUTM proprietor’s intention at the time of filing of the EUTM (para. 46).

The distributor acted in bad faith, since the application for the EUTM was filed — without genuine intention to use it — for the sole purpose to prevent the marketing of competing products (paras 82-83). The lack of intention of using the sign corresponding to the EUTM is of particular importance because it goes against the essential function of a mark, which is guaranteeing the identity of the origin of the goods and services it protects (para. 85).

FACTS:

The EUTM proprietor registered the figurative mark ‘K’ as an EUTM for goods and services in Classes 9, 12, 25 and 35.

An invalidity application was filed pursuant to Article 52(1)(b) CTMR. It was claimed that the EUTM proprietor registered the EUTM, without the consent of the invalidity applicant, despite the existence of a distribution agreement that governed the relations between the parties, and which also granted the EUTM proprietor a licence to use the mark KUOTA.

The Cancellation Division (CD) dismissed the invalidity application. It found that the invalidity applicant had not proven to have a better right to the sign registered as an EUTM and that it could not be established, on the basis of objective evidence, that the intention of the EUTM proprietor on the date of filing of the EUTM application was illegitimate. Under these circumstances, the ‘first to file’ principle had to be applied and, as a consequence, the ground of invalidity had to be rejected.

The invalidity applicant filed an appeal. The Board of Appeal (BoA) dismissed the appeal. It found that the distribution agreement that governed the relations between the parties provided for a joint collaboration, where each party took separate roles and functions. Moreover, good faith should be presumed; there was no objective evidence to conclude that the EUTM proprietor acted in bad faith.

The invalidity applicant filed an action before the General Court (GC), relying on a single plea in law, namely, the infringement of Article 52(1)(b) CTMR. The GC upheld the action. It found that the evidence on the file and the circumstances of the case lead to the conclusion that the EUTM was registered in bad faith.

SUBSTANCE:

ON THE ADMISIBILITY OF NEW EVIDENCE. The EUTM proprietor submits, for the first time before the GC, a judgment of the Tribunale de Grande Instance de Paris (Paris Regional Court) of 9 February 2018, which postdates the decision of the BoA of 15 December 2017. The purpose of an action before the GC is to review the legality of the BoA decision in the light of the facts and evidence known at the time the decision was issued, with the result that it is not the GC’s function to review the facts adduced for the first time before it (para. 29). The admission of new evidence is contrary to Article 188 of the Rules of Procedure of the GC, according to which the pleadings cannot change the subject matter of the proceedings before the BoA (para. 29). The judgment submitted by the invalidity applicant could not be submitted before the BoA, since it postdates the contested decision (para. 30). It follows that the judgment is inadmissible (para. 31).

PRELIMINARY REMARK ON THE FIRST TO FILE PRINCIPLE. Article 8(2) EUTMR provides for the ‘first to file’ principle, according to which an exclusive right is conferred on the first applicant (para. 34). Pursuant to that principle, a sign may be registered as an EUTM insofar as it is not precluded by an earlier mark (para. 35). However, the mere use by a third party of a non-registered mark does not prevent an identical or similar mark to be registered as an EUTM (para. 36). In the present case, it is common ground that the EUTM proprietor was the first to file the EUTM (para. 37).

ON THE ASSESSMENT OF BATH FAITH. The principle of ‘first to file’ is nuanced, in particular, by Article 59(1)(b) EUTMR, according to which an EUTM must be declared invalid if the EUTM proprietor was acting in bad faith when it filed the application (para. 38). The concept of bad faith is not defined, delimited or described in any way in the legislation (para. 40). According to case-law, in determining whether an EUTM proprietor was acting in bad faith on the date of application, all relevant factors specific to the particular case must be taken into account (11/06/2009, C 529/07, Lindt Goldhase, EU:C:2009:361, § 53) (para. 41). In this particular case, the factors to be taken into consideration are: (i) the question whether the EUTM proprietor knew, at the date of filing of the EUTM, that the invalidity applicant had previously been using the sign corresponding to the EUTM, (ii) the nature of the contractual relationship between the parties, (iii) the origin of the sign corresponding to the EUTM, (iv) the degree of legal protection of the sign corresponding to the EUTM and (v) the EUTM proprietor’s intention at the time of filing of the EUTM (para. 46).

(i)	The question whether the EUTM proprietor knew, at the date of filing, that the invalidity applicant was using the sign corresponding to the EUTM. It is common ground that the EUTM proprietor knew that the invalidity applicant was using the mark, KUOTA, together with the sign corresponding to the EUTM for bicycles (paras 49-51). However, this is not sufficient to conclude that the EUTM was registered in bad faith, and, consequently, other circumstances of the case must be analysed (para. 52).

(ii)	The nature of the contractual relationship between the parties. Under the distribution contract between the parties, the EUTM proprietor was called to perform both the roles of distributor and buyer, however, the first role appears to be preponderant for various reasons (para. 55). First, the EUTM proprietor was designated in several occasions as ‘KUOTA dealer’ (para. 56). Second, the provisions of the contract titled ‘cessation of contract’ specified that if the agreement was terminated, the EUTM proprietor would be required to ‘cease all activities’ under the mark KUOTA, with the exception of a possible flow of stock (para. 56). Third, the contract authorised the EUTM proprietor to sell goods manufactured by the invalidity applicant under the mark KUOTA (para. 56). Fourth, the contract provided that the invalidity applicant had to comply with its own quality standards and did not mention the participation of the EUTM proprietor in their development (para. 56). Fifth, according to the evidence on the file, the EUTM proprietor’s manager himself affirmed that he was the ‘official distributor of KUOTA’ (para. 56). The BoA erred when it found that the parties assumed separate roles and functions on a ‘fundamentally equal position’ (para. 57).

(iii)	The origin of the sign corresponding to the EUTM. It is common ground that the EUTM proprietor used a communication agency to create the sign corresponding to the EUTM (para. 63). However, the circumstances under which the EUTM was created are irrelevant (para. 64), since the parties’ contractual relationship show that the development of the sign corresponding to the EUTM was actually performed by the EUTM proprietor on behalf of the invalidity applicant under the marketing mission assigned from the former by the latter (para. 69). As a result, the origin of the sign corresponding to the EUTM does not preclude a finding of bad faith (para. 70).

(iv)	The degree of legal protection of the sign corresponding to the EUTM. It is undisputed that the applicant did not have the exclusive property of the sign corresponding to the EUTM (paras 71-72).

(v)	The intention of the EUTM proprietor at the time of filing of the EUTM. The contested mark was applied for on 28 November 2012, 2 days before the end of the contractual relationship between the parties, which, was already deteriorated (para. 76). Moreover, on the same date, the EUTM proprietor sought to register another figurative ‘K’ mark and, at the same time, launched a new trade mark, KEMO, for the sale of bicycles (para. 77). In view of the lack of sales of the EUTM proprietor under the sign corresponding to the EUTM and the fact that it sent cease and desist letters to some of the invalidity applicant’s distributors, it must be considered that the filing of the EUTM had the sole purpose to prevent the marketing of competing products (paras 82-83). The lack of intention of using the sign corresponding to the EUTM is of particular importance because it goes against the essential function of a mark, which is guaranteeing the identity of the origin of the goods and services it protects (para. 85). As a consequence, on the date of the application for the EUTM, the EUTM proprietor acted in bad faith.

KEY POINTS:

Bad faith of a distributor when filing an EUTM application in the context of a deterioration of the contractual relationships (distribution agreement) with the manufacturer.

The factors to be taken into consideration are: (i) the question whether the EUTM proprietor knew, at the date of filing of the EUTM, that the invalidity applicant had previously been using the sign corresponding to the EUTM, (ii) the nature of the contractual relationship between the parties, (iii) the origin of the sign corresponding to the EUTM, (iv) the degree of legal protection of the sign corresponding to the EUTM and (v) the EUTM proprietor’s intention at the time of filing of the EUTM (para. 46).

The distributor acted in bad faith, since the application for the EUTM was filed — without genuine intention to use it — for the sole purpose to prevent the marketing of competing products (paras 82-83). The lack of intention of using the sign corresponding to the EUTM is of particular importance because it goes against the essential function of a mark, which is guaranteeing the identity of the origin of the goods and services it protects (para. 85).

FACTS:

The EUTM proprietor registered the figurative mark ‘K’ as an EUTM for goods and services in Classes 9, 12, 25 and 35.

An invalidity application was filed pursuant to Article 52(1)(b) CTMR. It was claimed that the EUTM proprietor registered the EUTM, without the consent of the invalidity applicant, despite the existence of a distribution agreement that governed the relations between the parties, and which also granted the EUTM proprietor a licence to use the mark KUOTA.

The Cancellation Division (CD) dismissed the invalidity application. It found that the invalidity applicant had not proven to have a better right to the sign registered as an EUTM and that it could not be established, on the basis of objective evidence, that the intention of the EUTM proprietor on the date of filing of the EUTM application was illegitimate. Under these circumstances, the ‘first to file’ principle had to be applied and, as a consequence, the ground of invalidity had to be rejected.

The invalidity applicant filed an appeal. The Board of Appeal (BoA) dismissed the appeal. It found that the distribution agreement that governed the relations between the parties provided for a joint collaboration, where each party took separate roles and functions. Moreover, good faith should be presumed; there was no objective evidence to conclude that the EUTM proprietor acted in bad faith.

The invalidity applicant filed an action before the General Court (GC), relying on a single plea in law, namely, the infringement of Article 52(1)(b) CTMR. The GC upheld the action. It found that the evidence on the file and the circumstances of the case lead to the conclusion that the EUTM was registered in bad faith.

SUBSTANCE:

ON THE ADMISIBILITY OF NEW EVIDENCE. The EUTM proprietor submits, for the first time before the GC, a judgment of the Tribunale de Grande Instance de Paris (Paris Regional Court) of 9 February 2018, which postdates the decision of the BoA of 15 December 2017. The purpose of an action before the GC is to review the legality of the BoA decision in the light of the facts and evidence known at the time the decision was issued, with the result that it is not the GC’s function to review the facts adduced for the first time before it (para. 29). The admission of new evidence is contrary to Article 188 of the Rules of Procedure of the GC, according to which the pleadings cannot change the subject matter of the proceedings before the BoA (para. 29). The judgment submitted by the invalidity applicant could not be submitted before the BoA, since it postdates the contested decision (para. 30). It follows that the judgment is inadmissible (para. 31).

PRELIMINARY REMARK ON THE FIRST TO FILE PRINCIPLE. Article 8(2) EUTMR provides for the ‘first to file’ principle, according to which an exclusive right is conferred on the first applicant (para. 34). Pursuant to that principle, a sign may be registered as an EUTM insofar as it is not precluded by an earlier mark (para. 35). However, the mere use by a third party of a non-registered mark does not prevent an identical or similar mark to be registered as an EUTM (para. 36). In the present case, it is common ground that the EUTM proprietor was the first to file the EUTM (para. 37).

ON THE ASSESSMENT OF BATH FAITH. The principle of ‘first to file’ is nuanced, in particular, by Article 59(1)(b) EUTMR, according to which an EUTM must be declared invalid if the EUTM proprietor was acting in bad faith when it filed the application (para. 38). The concept of bad faith is not defined, delimited or described in any way in the legislation (para. 40). According to case-law, in determining whether an EUTM proprietor was acting in bad faith on the date of application, all relevant factors specific to the particular case must be taken into account (11/06/2009, C 529/07, Lindt Goldhase, EU:C:2009:361, § 53) (para. 41). In this particular case, the factors to be taken into consideration are: (i) the question whether the EUTM proprietor knew, at the date of filing of the EUTM, that the invalidity applicant had previously been using the sign corresponding to the EUTM, (ii) the nature of the contractual relationship between the parties, (iii) the origin of the sign corresponding to the EUTM, (iv) the degree of legal protection of the sign corresponding to the EUTM and (v) the EUTM proprietor’s intention at the time of filing of the EUTM (para. 46).

(i)	The question whether the EUTM proprietor knew, at the date of filing, that the invalidity applicant was using the sign corresponding to the EUTM. It is common ground that the EUTM proprietor knew that the invalidity applicant was using the mark, KUOTA, together with the sign corresponding to the EUTM for bicycles (paras 49-51). However, this is not sufficient to conclude that the EUTM was registered in bad faith, and, consequently, other circumstances of the case must be analysed (para. 52).

(ii)	The nature of the contractual relationship between the parties. Under the distribution contract between the parties, the EUTM proprietor was called to perform both the roles of distributor and buyer, however, the first role appears to be preponderant for various reasons (para. 55). First, the EUTM proprietor was designated in several occasions as ‘KUOTA dealer’ (para. 56). Second, the provisions of the contract titled ‘cessation of contract’ specified that if the agreement was terminated, the EUTM proprietor would be required to ‘cease all activities’ under the mark KUOTA, with the exception of a possible flow of stock (para. 56). Third, the contract authorised the EUTM proprietor to sell goods manufactured by the invalidity applicant under the mark KUOTA (para. 56). Fourth, the contract provided that the invalidity applicant had to comply with its own quality standards and did not mention the participation of the EUTM proprietor in their development (para. 56). Fifth, according to the evidence on the file, the EUTM proprietor’s manager himself affirmed that he was the ‘official distributor of KUOTA’ (para. 56). The BoA erred when it found that the parties assumed separate roles and functions on a ‘fundamentally equal position’ (para. 57).

(iii)	The origin of the sign corresponding to the EUTM. It is common ground that the EUTM proprietor used a communication agency to create the sign corresponding to the EUTM (para. 63). However, the circumstances under which the EUTM was created are irrelevant (para. 64), since the parties’ contractual relationship show that the development of the sign corresponding to the EUTM was actually performed by the EUTM proprietor on behalf of the invalidity applicant under the marketing mission assigned from the former by the latter (para. 69). As a result, the origin of the sign corresponding to the EUTM does not preclude a finding of bad faith (para. 70).

(iv)	The degree of legal protection of the sign corresponding to the EUTM. It is undisputed that the applicant did not have the exclusive property of the sign corresponding to the EUTM (paras 71-72).

(v)	The intention of the EUTM proprietor at the time of filing of the EUTM. The contested mark was applied for on 28 November 2012, 2 days before the end of the contractual relationship between the parties, which, was already deteriorated (para. 76). Moreover, on the same date, the EUTM proprietor sought to register another figurative ‘K’ mark and, at the same time, launched a new trade mark, KEMO, for the sale of bicycles (para. 77). In view of the lack of sales of the EUTM proprietor under the sign corresponding to the EUTM and the fact that it sent cease and desist letters to some of the invalidity applicant’s distributors, it must be considered that the filing of the EUTM had the sole purpose to prevent the marketing of competing products (paras 82-83). The lack of intention of using the sign corresponding to the EUTM is of particular importance because it goes against the essential function of a mark, which is guaranteeing the identity of the origin of the goods and services it protects (para. 85). As a consequence, on the date of the application for the EUTM, the EUTM proprietor acted in bad faith.

The EU trade mark system is based on the first-to-file principle, an exception to which is Art 8(3). However, in the case of no business dealings or fiduciary duty between the EUTM proprietor and the owner of an identical trade mark outside the EU, first-to-first is the relevant rule, and here no bad faith has been shown. Nor can it be said that the proprietor is a serial offender, no offense at all having been proven. To discriminate against him merely because he comes from India is not excusable. Nor did he request an extortionate sum, rather he did not want to sell the mark but eventually was pushed into naming a price, as a response to an unsolicited offer. No duty to name any price at all.

keywords: Dissimilarity of the goods and services
results.summary:
FACTS: 

The EUTM proprietor sought to register the figurative mark as an EUTM for goods and services in Classes 25, 35 and 39.

An opposition based on the earlier figurative marks, registered for goods and services in Classes 18, 25 and 35, was filed pursuant to Article 8(1)(b) CTMR. The Opposition Division (OD) upheld the opposition insofar as it related to goods and services in Classes 25 and 35. The decision was confirmed by the Board of Appeal (BoA).

An invalidity application was filed, pursuant to Article 52(1)(b) CTMR, against the EUTM that was registered in Class 39. The Cancellation Division (CD) dismissed the invalidity application, finding that it had not been proven that the intervener had acted in bad faith.

The invalidity applicant appealed. The BoA dismissed the appeal, finding that there could be no bad faith within the meaning of Article 52(1)(b) CTMR since there was neither identity nor similarity between the goods or services.

The invalidity applicant filed an action before the General Court (GC), relying on a single plea in law: (i) infringement of Article 52(1)(b) CTMR. The GC dismissed the action. It found that the BoA was fully entitled to decide that bad faith had not been established by relying on the fact that the contested mark was registered for services dissimilar to those designated by the earlier marks, thus precluding any likelihood of confusion (LOC) between the marks at issue.

The invalidity applicant appealed to the Court of Justice of the European Union (CJ). The CJ upheld the appeal.

SUBSTANCE: 

The ground of bad faith applies where it is apparent from relevant and consistent indicia that the proprietor of an EU trade mark filed its application for registration, not with the aim of engaging fairly in competition, but with the intention of undermining the interests of third parties (in a manner inconsistent with honest practices), or with the intention of obtaining, without even targeting a specific third party, an exclusive right for purposes other than those falling within the functions of a trade mark, in particular, the essential function of indicating origin (para. 46)

There may be situations where the application for registration of a trade mark had been filed in bad faith notwithstanding the fact that, at the time of that application, there was no use by a third party on the internal market of an identical or similar sign for identical or similar goods. In applications for invalidity based on Article 52(1)(b) CTMR, there is no requirement whatsoever that the invalidity applicant be the proprietor of an earlier mark for identical or similar goods or services (paras 52 and 53).

In cases where, at the time of the application for the contested mark, a third party was using, in at least one Member State, a sign identical with, or similar to, that mark, the existence of LOC need not necessarily be established for Article 52(1)(b) CTMR to apply (para. 54).

In the absence of LOC, or if there has been no use by a third party of a sign identical with, or similar to, the contested mark, other factual circumstances may constitute relevant and consistent indicia establishing the bad faith of the applicant (para. 56).

The GC should have taken account of the fact that the EUTM proprietor had sought registration of a sign comprising the stylised word KOTON as an EUTM, not only for services in Class 39, but also for goods and services in Classes 25 and 35 (para. 60).

keywords: Identity of the goods and services
results.summary: 79	As a result, despite the consumers’ high level of attention, the conflicting signs at issue do not maintain such a sufficient distance. The conflicting signs are visually and aurally similar to an average degree. They share the common beginning ‘AmBi’ to which the consumers will pay the most attention. For the reasons set out above, at least a significant part of the relevant public will perceive the syllable ‘AMB/AMBI’ as a fanciful term enjoying an average degree of distinctiveness for the conflicting goods. For this part of the relevant public - despite its high level of attention - the similarities between the signs outweigh their dissimilarities. A likelihood of confusion is, therefore, inevitable for said part of the public.
No bad faith proven. Proprietor had knowledge of earlier US registrations, but no dishonest intention when filing the EUTM.

results.outcome: Cancellation totally upheld and EUTM/IR declared partially invalid

keywords: Similarity of the goods and services
Identity of the goods and services
results.summary:
KEY POINTS:

Pleas that are invoked for the first time before the General Court are inadmissible since the parties to the proceedings cannot modify the legal framework of the dispute as it was brought before the BoA (para. 36).

FACTS:

The applicant sought to register the figurative mark ‘CARAJILLO LICOR 43 CUARENTA Y TRES’ as an EUTM for several goods in Classes 33, 35 and 41.

An opposition based on the earlier Spanish figurative mark ‘Carajillo’, registered for goods in Class 33, was filed pursuant to Article 8(1)(a) CTMR and Article 8(1)(b) CTMR in relation to goods in Classes 33 and 35 of the contested mark.

The Opposition Division (OD) partially upheld the opposition for wines, liqueurs and other alcoholic beverages (except beer) in Class 33 and for retail and wholesale services regarding wines, liqueurs and other alcoholic beverages in Class 35 finding likelihood of confusion (LOC).

The applicant filed an appeal against the OD’s decision insofar as the opposition was upheld. The Board of Appeal (BoA) partially allowed the appeal insofar as the OD upheld the opposition in relation to liqueurs and other alcoholic beverages (except beer) in Class 33 and retail and wholesale services, import, export, advertisement and exclusive sales, organization of festivals for advertising purpose in relation to liqueurs and other alcoholic beverages in Class 35 and dismissed the opposition in relation to such products. 
The opponent filed an action before the General Court (GC), relying on four pleas in law: (i) infringement of Article 94(1) EUTMR; (ii) infringement of Article 8(1)(b) EUTMR; (iii) infringement of Article 8(2)(c) EUTMR and (iv) infringement of Article 8(5) EUTMR and Article 59(1)(b) EUTMR.

SUBSTANCE:

(i) SUBJECT MATTER OF THE ACTION BEFORE THE GC. The BoA analysed the opposition in relation to goods that were not part of the appeal’s subject matter, namely services of import, export, advertisement and exclusive sales, organization of festivals for advertising in relation to wines and in the statement of reasons of the contested decision ruled there was LOC in relation to them (para. 24). Since the operative part of the contested decision does not go beyond the subject matter of the dispute, the fact that in the statement of reasons the BoA ruled on services that were not part of the subject matter of the appeal before it is not going to affect the legality of the contested decision (para. 27).

(ii) INFRINGEMENT OF ARTICLE 8(2)(c) EUTMR AND INFRINGEMENT OF ARTICLE 8(5) EUTMR AND ARTICLE 59(1)(b) EUTMR. The third and the fourth pleas are inadmissible as they are invoked for the first time before the GC. The parties to the proceedings cannot modify the legal framework of the dispute as it was brought before the BoA (paras 36-37).

(iii) INFRINGEMENT OF ARTICLE 94(1) EUTMR. The opponent’s argument that the BoA concluded there was LOC by taking into consideration only one factor, namely that the word element ‘carajillo’ had weak distinctive character is a matter of substantiation of the contested decision and does not refer to the breach of the obligation to state reasons.
The risk of the contested mark being perceived as a sub-brand of the earlier mark is not one of the factors taken into consideration for the existence of LOC, but rather a consequence of its existence. The plea is rejected as manifestly unfounded (para. 48).

(iv) INFRINGEMENT OF ARTICLE 8(1)(b) EUTMR. The similarity between the signs is due to the coincidence in the term ‘carajillo’. There is NO LOC for the goods and services in relation to which this term is weak. The term ‘carajillo’ designates ‘a beverage that is generally prepared by adding a strong alcoholic beverage to the hot coffee’. This definition renders ‘carajillo’ as descriptive in relation to liqueurs and other alcoholic beverages (except beer) in Class 33 and retail and wholesale services regarding liqueurs and other alcoholic beverages in Class 35 (para. 57). There is LOC for the remaining goods and services, in relation to which this term is fully distinctive. The plea is rejected as manifestly unfounded (paras 50-76).

results.summary: There exists no likelihood of confusion within the meaning of Article 8(1)(b) EUTMR and the conditions of Articles 60(1)(a) and 8(2) EUTMR are not met. 43No argument at all is provided upon appeal in relation to the findings of the contested decision in relation to Article 60(1)(a) and Article 8(2) EUTMR, and the Board fully endorses the findings of the Cancellation Division in this respect. The evidence filed falls far short of establishing that the earlier trade mark is a well-known trade mark, and so these provisions do not apply.

FACTS:

The word mark MOULDPRO was registered for goods in Class 17.

The invalidity application lodged on the basis of Article 60(1)(b) and (c) EUTMR in conjunction with Article 8(3) and (4) EUTMR and Article 59(1)(b) EUTMR was rejected.

The invalidity applicant appealed. The Board of Appeal (BoA) found the appeal partially inadmissible as the invalidity applicant invoked a company name as an additional earlier right for the first time before the BoA. For the remainder, the appeal was unfounded. The invalidity applicant did not show that the EUTM proprietor had acted in bad faith. Neither did it prove the existence of an ‘agent-principal’ relationship nor its use of the sign MOULDPRO in the course of trade.

PROCEDURAL ISSUES:

The plea relating to the ‘agent-principal’ relationship is admissible despite the fact that the invalidity applicant did not challenge the conclusion of the Cancellation Division before the BoA (para. 28). Even if the appellant has not raised a specific ground of appeal, the BoA is required to examine whether a new decision with the same operative part as the decision under appeal may be lawfully adopted at the time of the appeal ruling (para. 27). The invalidity applicant made submissions in regard to the ‘agent-principal’ relationship before the Cancellation Division, thus it also appeared in the documents before the BoA who correctly proceeded to analyse the conditions of the invalidity ground (para. 28).

SUBSTANCE:

(i) INFRINGEMENT OF ARTICLE 60(1)(B) EUTMR IN CONJUNCTION WITH ARTICLE 8(3) EUTMR
There is no evidence to indicate that the relationship between the EUTM proprietor and a parent company of the invalidity applicant exceeded a mere purchaser-seller relationship or that there was an agreement of commercial cooperation establishing a fiduciary relationship between the parties and, accordingly, a duty of loyalty (para. 33). In any case, the invalidity applicant and its parent company are separate legal entities and any obligation on the EUTM proprietor in respect of the latter does not automatically entail an obligation in respect of the invalidity applicant (para. 32). Article 8(3) EUTMR can apply to agreements that have expired before the date of filing of the EUTM application, provided that the duty of trust and confidentiality still existed on the filing date (para. 35). The mere existence of pre-contractual negotiations concerning commercial cooperation does not justify the application of Article 8(3) EUTMR (para. 36). An ‘agent-principal’ relationship between the EUTM proprietor and the invalidity applicant cannot be based on the fact that their directors were acquainted with each other in the context of a professional relationship which, itself, cannot be characterised as an ‘agent-principal’ relationship and which, moreover, involves different companies, marks and times (para. 37).

(ii) INFRINGEMENT OF ARTICLE 59(1)(B) EUTMR
The existence of cooperation, correspondence or a distribution agreement with the director of the EUTM proprietor does not, on its own, prove bad faith. The mark was not mentioned in the emails, nor is there any information relating to its use with respect to the specific goods or services (paras 90-93, 96). The mention of the company name without any relation to identical or similar goods does not lead to a contrary conclusion (para. 91). Knowledge of the use of the earlier mark cannot be presumed since use, if any, was limited to a maximum of 16 months (paras 94-95). Nor can there be any assumption of such knowledge inferred from the fact that the directors of the parties knew each other and that a degree of collaboration existed between them when different companies, marks and goods were concerned (para. 97). Finally, the identity of the signs does not suffice on its own to prove such knowledge. The sign is a relatively simple combination, which consists of a reference to the moulding sector and the suffix ‘pro’, a common abbreviation of the word ‘professional’ (para. 98). Mere knowledge that the sign was used as a company name does not point to a dishonest intention (para. 104). Nor did the invalidity applicant prove that the EUTM proprietor sought to keep it out of the German market (para. 103). Ultimately, the fact that the EUTM proprietor used its mark supports the conclusion that the filing was made in good faith (para. 105).

FACTS:

The word mark MOULDPRO was registered for goods in Class 17.

The invalidity application lodged on the basis of Article 60(1)(b) and (c) EUTMR in conjunction with Article 8(3) and (4) EUTMR and Article 59(1)(b) EUTMR was rejected.

The invalidity applicant appealed. The Board of Appeal (BoA) found the appeal partially inadmissible as the invalidity applicant invoked a company name as an additional earlier right for the first time before the BoA. For the remainder, the appeal was unfounded. The invalidity applicant did not show that the EUTM proprietor had acted in bad faith. Neither did it prove the existence of an ‘agent-principal’ relationship nor its use of the sign MOULDPRO in the course of trade.

PROCEDURAL ISSUES:

The plea relating to the ‘agent-principal’ relationship is admissible despite the fact that the invalidity applicant did not challenge the conclusion of the Cancellation Division before the BoA (para. 28). Even if the appellant has not raised a specific ground of appeal, the BoA is required to examine whether a new decision with the same operative part as the decision under appeal may be lawfully adopted at the time of the appeal ruling (para. 27). The invalidity applicant made submissions in regard to the ‘agent-principal’ relationship before the Cancellation Division, thus it also appeared in the documents before the BoA who correctly proceeded to analyse the conditions of the invalidity ground (para. 28).

SUBSTANCE:

(i) INFRINGEMENT OF ARTICLE 60(1)(B) EUTMR IN CONJUNCTION WITH ARTICLE 8(3) EUTMR
There is no evidence to indicate that the relationship between the EUTM proprietor and a parent company of the invalidity applicant exceeded a mere purchaser-seller relationship or that there was an agreement of commercial cooperation establishing a fiduciary relationship between the parties and, accordingly, a duty of loyalty (para. 33). In any case, the invalidity applicant and its parent company are separate legal entities and any obligation on the EUTM proprietor in respect of the latter does not automatically entail an obligation in respect of the invalidity applicant (para. 32). Article 8(3) EUTMR can apply to agreements that have expired before the date of filing of the EUTM application, provided that the duty of trust and confidentiality still existed on the filing date (para. 35). The mere existence of pre-contractual negotiations concerning commercial cooperation does not justify the application of Article 8(3) EUTMR (para. 36). An ‘agent-principal’ relationship between the EUTM proprietor and the invalidity applicant cannot be based on the fact that their directors were acquainted with each other in the context of a professional relationship which, itself, cannot be characterised as an ‘agent-principal’ relationship and which, moreover, involves different companies, marks and times (para. 37).

(ii) INFRINGEMENT OF ARTICLE 59(1)(B) EUTMR
The existence of cooperation, correspondence or a distribution agreement with the director of the EUTM proprietor does not, on its own, prove bad faith. The mark was not mentioned in the emails, nor is there any information relating to its use with respect to the specific goods or services (paras 90-93, 96). The mention of the company name without any relation to identical or similar goods does not lead to a contrary conclusion (para. 91). Knowledge of the use of the earlier mark cannot be presumed since use, if any, was limited to a maximum of 16 months (paras 94-95). Nor can there be any assumption of such knowledge inferred from the fact that the directors of the parties knew each other and that a degree of collaboration existed between them when different companies, marks and goods were concerned (para. 97). Finally, the identity of the signs does not suffice on its own to prove such knowledge. The sign is a relatively simple combination, which consists of a reference to the moulding sector and the suffix ‘pro’, a common abbreviation of the word ‘professional’ (para. 98). Mere knowledge that the sign was used as a company name does not point to a dishonest intention (para. 104). Nor did the invalidity applicant prove that the EUTM proprietor sought to keep it out of the German market (para. 103). Ultimately, the fact that the EUTM proprietor used its mark supports the conclusion that the filing was made in good faith (para. 105).

results.summary: The appeal is dismissed. The cancellation applicant did not provide sufficient proof that the EUTM proprietor acted in bad faith when filing the application for the contested mark. There is no sufficient proof that the EUTM proprietor knew or must have known at the time of filing that the cancellation applicant or a third party was offering financial services under the sign 'Target Ventures'.

results.ipRight: EUTM on which IA is based

results.summary: The EUTM proprietor is clearly trying to exploit for himself the existing authority of an original ‘KUKKIWON’ exam or certificate provided by (an important) organisation of the whole sports branch of Taekwondo. The EUTM proprietor was aware or should have been aware that it was the cancellation applicant and not he who was the rightful owner of the trade mark ‘KUKKIWON’. Moreover, this exploitation also extends to the other goods and services covered by the contested mark.
The decision deals with a German copyright under Art. 8(4) EUTMR and bad faith.

keywords: Claim for alteration of EUIPO decision
results.summary: FACTS: The EUTM proprietor registered the word mark KHADI as an EUTM for goods in Classes 3, 21 and 31. An invalidity application was filed pursuant to Article 59(1)(b) EUTMR, Article 59(1)(a) in conjunction with Article 7(1)(g) and (i) EUTMR, Article 60(1)(a) in conjunction with Article 8(1)(a) and (b) EUTMR, and Article 60(1)(c) in conjunction with Article 8(4) EUTMR. The Cancellation Division (CD) dismissed the invalidity application. The invalidity applicant filed an appeal. The Board of Appeal (BoA) dismissed the appeal. It found that the applicant had not adduced proof of any of the grounds of invalidity relied on. The invalidity applicant filed an action with the General Court (GC), relying on five pleas in law: (i) infringement of Article 95(2) EUTMR, (ii) distortion of the evidence, (iii) infringement of Article 7(1)(g) and Article 59(1)(a) EUTMR, (iv) infringement of Article 7(1)(g) and Article 59(1)(a) EUTMR and (v) infringement of Article 59(1)(b) EUTMR. SUBSTANCE: Submission of items of evidence for the first time before the BoA. Since the special rule contained in the third subparagraph of Rule 50(1) of Regulation 2868/95, which allows the BoA to take into account additional and supplementary facts and evidence in certain cases, refers only to opposition proceedings, the BoA erred in concluding that it applied to these invalidity proceedings based on both relative and absolute invalidity grounds. However, that error has no effect on the lawfulness of the contested decision, as the BoA examined the nature and the content of the evidence and concluded it was not relevant to the outcome of the case, before finding it to be inadmissible in the exercise of its discretion (paras 22-25). Distortion of evidence. The invalidity applicant has failed to provide proof of distortion of evidence by the BoA (paras 33-36). Deceptiveness. None of the invalidity applicant’s evidence or arguments permit the inference that the general public, or even the part of the United Kingdom population that is of Indian origin, is generally familiar with the term ‘khadi’ or its role for Indian industry and, consequently, the BoA rightly assessed that the term is an uncommon word and that it cannot be assumed that the term will be understood by that public. If the relevant public does not associate the term ‘khadi’ with any specific meaning, the existence of actual deceit or a sufficiently serious risk that the consumer will be deceived cannot be established (paras 48-53). Prohibition of marks which include badges, emblems or escutcheons of particular public interest. Since the evidence submitted by the applicant is insufficient to allow it to be concluded that the relevant public will attribute any meaning to the term ‘khadi’ or would associate it with any organisation whatsoever, the BoA rightly concluded that no badge, emblem or escutcheon would be identified by that public in the sign at issue (para. 61). Bad faith. The BoA did not err in concluding that it had not been established that, when the application for registration of the contested mark was filed, the intention of the applicant for that registration was to usurp the invalidity applicant’s reputation, inter alia because no recognition or extensive use of the invalidity applicant’s mark had been demonstrated (para. 79). The contested decision is not vitiated by any ground of unlawfulness and the action of the invalidity applicant must therefore be rejected (paras 81-82).
